If concurrency.rs has a bug or sessions leak, ffmpeg can saturate all NAS CPU/RAM and OOM-kill co-tenant workloads — including the backend the transcoder path depends on. The compose comment at line 237 even acknowledges fan-out ('Sized for concurrent sessions; raise if MAX_CONCURRENT_TRANSCODES grows') while capping only the /scratch tmpfs, not CPU/RAM. The recommender (loads a sentence-transformer model into RAM) is similarly uncapped.

Evidence
docker-compose.yml transcoder (220-257) has tmpfs '/scratch:size=2g' and environment but no 'mem_limit'/'cpus'/'pids_limit'/'deploy:'; same absence across all services.
Recommendation

Add cpus/mem_limit/pids_limit (or deploy.resources.limits under swarm/compose v3) to at least the transcoder and recommender, sized relative to the concurrency caps, so a runaway ffmpeg or model load cannot take down the NAS or the backend.

high Server->recommender internal-principal Bearer is minted but the receiver DEFAULTS to 'off' (declared-but-unenforced auth on the bridge) security Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge recommender/app/config.py:93-98 unverified
Description

The server mints and attaches the internal-principal Bearer to recommender calls, but the recommender receiver's enforcement mode (RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE) DEFAULTS to 'off', meaning the principal header is ignored entirely unless an operator explicitly flips it to 'log' then 'enforce'. recommender.ts:73-76 confirms this is intentional/pending. So today the only real auth on the server->sidecar channel is the shared x-recommender-secret HMAC header, and the sub is carried as a plain BODY field (recommender.ts:164-174 postFeedback({sub,...})), not bound to a verified principal. Any process that can reach recommender:8000 inside the docker network with the static secret can post feedback/impressions/rejections/shown for ANY user. This is the section-4 Hybrid-D internal-auth decision (a recorded one-way-door in MEMORY.md) being wired on the mint side but a no-op on the verify side for the default deployment.

Evidence
config.py:94 `raw = os.environ.get('RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE', 'off')`; config.py:42-44 comment 'Mode is \"off\" by default so a deployment that hasn't yet provisioned the secret keeps working'. recommender.ts:73-76 'The recommender service does not enforce the principal yet ... one-line recommender-side enforcement flip.' Bodies carry sub directly (recommender.ts:164-174).
Recommendation

Land PyO3 verification in the recommender image, set RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE=enforce in the production compose env, and take sub from the verified principal rather than the request body. Until then, document that cross-user event spoofing is possible by any holder of x-recommender-secret on the internal network, and confirm the secret is actually required (it is validated when present in config.py but the channel is only as strong as 'enforce' being on).

high Local-availability tagging works but is never reachable from search/detail — the 'in your library' signal only appears on the Discover strip feature-gap Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge src/lib/api/media.ts:whole-file unverified
Description

tagLocalAvailability is real and correct (tmdb_id JOIN against media.db movies/shows plus a strict normalized-title + EXACT-year fallback) and IS applied inside the suggestions engine (suggestions.ts:2147). But the web media client (src/lib/api/media.ts) exposes only posterFor + media DTO types, and grepping src for grant/stream_token/watch against /api/media returns only IPTV grant usage (iptvApi.grant*). There is no search/detail JSON endpoint that runs TMDB results through tagLocalAvailability. So Plex's pervasive 'you already own this' badge only shows on the personalized Discover strip, not on search or browse — the highest-traffic discovery surfaces don't consume the capability that was specifically built for them.

Evidence
src/lib/api/media.ts has no searchMedia/getMediaDetail returning localAvailable; grep across src shows grant/StreamGrant used only via iptvApi (LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab). tagLocalAvailability is imported only by the suggestions path.
Recommendation

Add a typed media search/detail endpoint (or extend an existing one) that runs results through tagLocalAvailability, and render an 'in your library' badge everywhere the SPA lists titles, matching Plex's availability indicator.

high Grant-time source precedence cannot select local/media-core — the section-9 local-first resolver only ever returns IPTV feature-gap Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/services/sourcePrecedence.ts:110-126 unverified
Description

sourcePrecedence.ts is named/documented as the section-9 Resolution-A local-first resolver, but buildCandidates() only ever returns an IPTV candidate: the media-core (local) slot is a comment ('media-core (M3+): not wired, always absent in M1.5') and Plex is probed only to populate available_alternatives, never as an auto-fallback because 'we cannot construct a Plex play URL for an IPTV stream_id in M1.5'. So despite local-first being the contract, a title that exists locally in media.db can NEVER be selected by this resolver — it resolves to IPTV or null. There is no media_title_link/getRatingKey(tmdb_id) lookup, and routes/media.ts has no grant step in front of it, so there is no working local-media playback-authorization path — the defining Plex capability.

Evidence
sourcePrecedence.ts:111-125 buildCandidates: 'media-core (M3+): not wired, always absent in M1.5.' then `const iptv = await probeIptv(); if (iptv) return [{source:'iptv',...}]; return []`. Comment at :108-109 'M3+ will replace this function with one that queries media_title_link...'.
Recommendation

Implement the media_title_link/getRatingKey lookup so local (media-core) and Plex become real ResolvedSource candidates ranked above IPTV, and put a grant step in front of routes/media.ts streaming so playback is authorized per the precedence result.

high Node toolchain majors pinned to versions that were not GA-stable as of the knowledge cutoff (typescript ~6, vite 8, eslint 10, vitest 4) — reproducibility and prerelease-toolchain risk dependency Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/package.json:46-68 unverified
Description

Several devDependency majors are pinned ahead of any GA stable as of the Jan 2026 cutoff: typescript ~6.0.2 (5.x was the stable line; 6.0 not GA), vite ^8.0.10 (6.x stable line), eslint ^10.2.1 and @eslint/js ^10.0.1 (9.x stable line), vitest ^4.1.5 and @vitest/coverage-v8 ^4.1.5 (3.x stable line), @vitejs/plugin-react ^6.0.1, globals ^17.5.0. CI runs `npm ci` (which respects the committed lockfile) so CI may be green TODAY off the author's lockfile, but these are the entire build/typecheck/lint/test toolchain: if any of these majors is in fact a prerelease/canary or does not exist on the registry, a clean `npm install` or any lockfile regeneration on another machine will not resolve reproducibly, and the 'green typecheck/vitest' claims rest on one frozen lockfile rather than a stable toolchain. Even where a prerelease exists, shipping an App-Store-bound product on non-GA TypeScript/ESLint/Vite/Vitest majors means depending on churning, pre-stable APIs.

Evidence
package.json devDependencies: 'typescript': '~6.0.2', 'vite': '^8.0.10', 'eslint': '^10.2.1', '@eslint/js': '^10.0.1', 'vitest': '^4.1.5', '@vitest/coverage-v8': '^4.1.5', '@vitejs/plugin-react': '^6.0.1', 'globals': '^17.5.0'. Build script: 'tsc -b && vite build && tsc -p server/tsconfig.json'; CI step 'Type-check (SPA): npx tsc --noEmit' and 'Test + coverage: npm run test:coverage' depend on these majors.
Recommendation

Run `npm view typescript@6 version`, `npm view vite versions`, `npm view eslint versions`, `npm view vitest versions` to confirm what actually published. Pin to the latest GA stable majors (realistically TS 5.x, Vite 6.x, ESLint 9.x, Vitest 3.x as of early 2026) unless there is a documented reason to ride a prerelease. Regenerate package-lock.json from a clean install on a clean machine and verify `npm ci && npm run build && npm test && npm run lint` from scratch. Do not ship on prerelease toolchain majors.

high Caret ranges on every security-sensitive Node runtime dependency (jose, simplewebauthn, better-sqlite3, sentry) security Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/package.json:24-44 unverified
Description

All runtime deps, including auth/crypto-critical ones, use caret ranges: jose ^6.2.3 (JWT/JWE — device_token and stream_token primitives ride on this), @simplewebauthn/server ^13.3.1 and @simplewebauthn/browser ^13.3.0 (the entire passkey auth surface), better-sqlite3 ^11.10.0 (native module, every DB read/write), @sentry/node ^9.47.1 (the §15-mandatory crash lifeline). Caret means any in-major minor/patch satisfies the range. On any host that runs `npm install` rather than `npm ci`, or after a lockfile regeneration, these can silently float to a build that was never reviewed — a supply-chain exposure for the exact libraries that gate authentication. The Dockerfile and CI correctly use `npm ci`, which mitigates the FLOAT at deploy time, but deploy-nas.sh and any local dev `npm install` are unguarded, and lockfile regen is unconstrained.

Evidence
package.json dependencies: 'jose': '^6.2.3', '@simplewebauthn/browser': '^13.3.0', '@simplewebauthn/server': '^13.3.1', 'better-sqlite3': '^11.10.0', '@sentry/node': '^9.47.1' — every one a caret range.
Recommendation

Pin auth/crypto deps (jose, both @simplewebauthn packages, @sentry/node) to exact versions; enforce `npm ci` in deploy-nas.sh as well as CI/Docker; add `npm audit --audit-level=high` and `npm audit signatures` as a CI gate; add Renovate/Dependabot with a manual-review group for the crypto/auth deps so version bumps are deliberate, not silent.

high Python recommender deps are unbounded (>= floors only) with no committed lockfile — non-reproducible builds dependency Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/recommender/pyproject.toml:6-19 unverified
Description

Every recommender dependency uses an open-ended lower bound with no upper cap: fastapi>=0.115, uvicorn[standard]>=0.32, pydantic>=2.9, sqlite-vec>=0.1.9, numpy>=1.26, httpx>=0.28, apscheduler>=3.10, anyio>=4.6, anthropic>=0.42, tenacity>=9.0, python-dateutil>=2.9, sentence-transformers>=3.3. No poetry.lock / requirements.txt with hashes is tracked, and the build-backend is hatchling (no lock). The Dockerfile does `pip install .` (line 103), so a build today vs next month resolves a different transitive closure — pydantic v2->v3, numpy 1.x->2.x ABI breaks, and the sentence-transformers/torch stack churn that routinely breaks at runtime. sqlite-vec>=0.1.9 is a pre-1.0 native extension floor with no ceiling, so a breaking 0.2 is silently accepted. The Dockerfile already had to special-case torch (CPU-only index, line 102) to avoid a 6GB CUDA pull — evidence that the unbounded ML stack is fragile. This undermines the cross-language byte-identical-contracts guarantee: the PyO3 ABI (abi3-py312) is fixed but the surrounding Python stack is not.

Evidence
recommender/pyproject.toml lines 6-19: all entries are '<name>>=<floor>' with no '<' upper bound. recommender/Dockerfile:102-103: 'RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch' then 'RUN pip install .' — no --require-hashes, no lockfile. No poetry.lock/requirements*.txt under recommender/ in git ls-files.
Recommendation

Add upper bounds on the breaking-major-risk libs (pydantic>=2.9,<3; numpy>=1.26,<3; sqlite-vec; pin torch + sentence-transformers explicitly) and commit a lockfile (uv.lock or pip-compile requirements.txt --generate-hashes). Install with --require-hashes in the Dockerfile. Evaluate whether anthropic>=0.42 is still needed (see unused-SDK finding) given the recommender's own description is 'Replaces per-request Claude calls'.

high BadTtl / MAX_TTL_SECS forged-token defense is unit-tested in Rust but unmapped through both FFI bindings and absent from the cross-language vector oracle testing Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/stream_token.rs:187-209 unverified
Description

The merge resolution that landed mid-audit added two security-relevant checks to enforce_time_window: an upper-bound TTL cap (exp - iat > MAX_TTL_SECS=91d => BadTtl) and a forged-window check (nbf > iat && exp < nbf => BadTtl). These ARE covered by a Rust unit test (stream_token.rs:415 bad_ttl_rejected, covering over-cap, forged-forward-nbf, and the expired-stays-Expired guard) — good. However the defense is Rust-only end-to-end: (1) BadTtl is never mapped through the N-API or PyO3 bindings (grep BadTtl|MAX_TTL in both binding src dirs returns nothing, rc=1); the napi stream_token_enforce_time_window simply format!("{:?}")s whatever error returns, with no contract that the TS/Python sides apply the SAME 91-day cap and forged-window rule. (2) There is no cross-language vector for it — tests/vectors.rs has only two test functions (canonical-bytes parity at line 37, HKDF parity at line 75), so the cap is never proven against the TypeScript verifier that actually gates content playback. A forged long-lived stream token is therefore defended only if the TS playback path independently re-implements the identical cap, which is unverified.

Evidence
stream_token.rs:192 `if claims.exp - claims.iat > MAX_TTL_SECS { return Err(TokenError::BadTtl); }`; stream_token.rs:200 `if claims.nbf > claims.iat && claims.exp < claims.nbf { return Err(TokenError::BadTtl); }`; stream_token.rs:114 `pub const MAX_TTL_SECS: i64 = 91 * 24 * 60 * 60;`. Unit test present: stream_token.rs:415 `fn bad_ttl_rejected()`. `grep -rnE 'BadTtl|MAX_TTL' crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src crates/emerald-contracts-pyo3/src` => no matches (rc=1). tests/vectors.rs has exactly two #[test] fns (lines 37, 75); no BadTtl vector.
Recommendation

Add a cross-language stream-token vector with expect:error_kind=BadTtl (over-cap exp-iat, and nbf>iat && exp<nbf) and verify the TypeScript verifier applies the identical 91-day cap and forged-window check; surface a stable BadTtl code through the napi/pyo3 bindings rather than an opaque debug-formatted string. Until the TS side is proven to match, the cap protects only the Rust services (media-core/transcoder), not the Hono playback grant path.

high Cross-language vector oracle does not exercise verify, expiry, tampering, dual-key, kid-dispatch, sub, or PII scrub — only canonical bytes + HKDF OKM testing Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/tests/vectors.rs:36-97 unverified
Description

The vector-driven cross-language gate contains exactly two tests: stream_token canonical_bytes + raw HMAC hex parity, and HKDF OKM hex parity. There is NO vector-driven Rust test for verify() rejecting a tampered signature, an expired/not-yet-valid token, wrong version (v!=1), unknown StreamKind, BadTtl, dual-key fallback, OR for device-token / internal-principal JWE decrypt across languages. device-token-kid-rotation.json, internal-principal.json, sub-namespace.json, and telemetry-pii-scrub.json all exist as committed vectors but are never loaded by vectors.rs — their negative checks (expired, wrong-iss, tampered-aad, unknown-kid, etc.) are encoded as JSON prose only. The adversarial paths are covered solely by in-crate Rust unit tests, so TypeScript or Python could diverge on rejection behavior and the oracle would stay green. For a product whose stream token is the only thing gating content delivery, 'Rust and TS agree on the happy-path HMAC' is necessary but nowhere near sufficient.

Evidence
vectors.rs defines exactly two #[test] fns: stream_token_canonical_vector_parity (line 37) and hkdf_parity_vector (line 75). load_vector is called only for stream-token-canonical.json and hkdf-parity.json; no call for device-token-kid-rotation.json, internal-principal.json, sub-namespace.json, or telemetry-pii-scrub.json. internal-principal.json's negative_checks (expired-rejected, wrong-iss-rejected, tampered-aad-rejects, unknown-kid-rejects) are JSON prose, not executed.
Recommendation

Add Rust (and mirrored TS/Python) loaders for every committed vector and assert REJECTION outcomes, not just bytes: tampered-sig->BadSignature, expired->Expired, nbf-future->NotYetValid, v=2->UnsupportedVersion, k=bogus->UnknownKind, over-cap->BadTtl, wrong-kid->UnknownKid, alg=RSA-OAEP header->BadHeader. Carry an explicit expect:{ok|error_kind} field per vector so all languages assert an identical error taxonomy.

high Device token TTL is 180 days with no in-crate replay or per-device revocation, and expiry enforcement is decoupled from decrypt security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/device_token.rs:24-110 unverified
Description

DEFAULT_TTL_SECS is 180 days (device_token.rs:24). device_token::decrypt validates signature/aud/iss and returns claims, but enforce_time_window is a SEPARATE function (device_token.rs:102) the caller must remember to invoke — a caller who forgets accepts expired tokens. The crate offers no jti-based replay cache and no per-device revocation primitive; the only documented rotation mechanism is bumping the kid and dropping the old key from the verifier map, which revokes EVERY device on that key generation, not a single compromised device. A leaked 180-day device token is valid for up to 180 days with no in-crate kill switch, and because the canonical crate provides no replay/revocation helper, each language binding must re-implement it independently — exactly the divergence this crate exists to prevent. (A server-side tokenReplayCache exists elsewhere per the brief, but it is outside this crate and not shared with PyO3/Swift consumers.)

Evidence
device_token.rs:24 `pub const DEFAULT_TTL_SECS: i64 = 180 * 24 * 60 * 60;`. device_token.rs:80 decrypt() returns Ok(claims) after only aud/iss checks; device_token.rs:102 enforce_time_window(claims, now) is a distinct call. No jti/replay/revocation function in device_token.rs or internal_principal.rs.
Recommendation

Fold time-window enforcement into the verify/decrypt path (take `now` as a parameter) so expiry can never be skipped, provide a canonical jti-extraction + revocation-list-check helper so replay/per-device revocation is implemented once for all bindings, and document the per-device revocation story (kid-drop only supports bulk rotation).

high Client has zero error/crash telemetry — violates the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract on the web tier production-readiness Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/main.tsx:1-20 unverified
Description

The contract makes Glitchtip telemetry mandatory (§15); the server initializes @sentry/node (server/index.ts:36-46) and even exposes a DSN-distribution endpoint at /api/telemetry (app.ts:167). The web client wires NOTHING: no @sentry/react, no Sentry.init, no window.onerror, no window.onunhandledrejection. A repo-wide grep for Sentry/@sentry in src/ returns only an unrelated CSS comment. Combined with the absent error boundary, every client crash, failed lazy import, and unhandled rejection is invisible to operators — production failures on remote NAS/TV/phone clients are undiagnosable. recommenderEvents.postClickEvent even swallows its own errors with `.catch(() => {})`.

Evidence
`grep -rn 'Sentry|@sentry|onunhandledrejection|window.onerror' src/` => only MediaCard.css comment; server/index.ts:37 `Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, ... })` exists server-side only; api/recommenderEvents.ts:17 `}).catch(() => {})`.
Recommendation

Initialize the Glitchtip/Sentry browser SDK in main.tsx using the DSN the server already serves at /api/telemetry, feed the new ErrorBoundary into it, and add a window.onunhandledrejection capture. Required by the contract, not optional.

high No centralized 401/403 handler — an expired cookie session leaves a silently broken UI production-readiness Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/queryClient.ts:3-11 unverified
Description

queryClient configures only staleTime/retry/refetchOnWindowFocus — no QueryCache or MutationCache onError. errors.ts maps 401 to 'Your session expired' copy but there is NO global interceptor (grep for isAuthError in src/ returns zero usages; it isn't even defined in this errors.ts but no equivalent global handler exists). The session cookie can expire mid-session; auth.tsx probes /api/me only ONCE on mount and never re-validates, so after expiry every query/mutation 401s independently, each component shows its own local error or nothing, and the user is never bounced to login or signed out. Result: a dead-looking dashboard requiring a manual reload.

Evidence
queryClient.ts has no cache-level callbacks; auth.tsx session probe is a single `useEffect(() => { fetch(apiUrl('/api/me'), ...) }, [applyUser])` with no refetch/re-validation; no data-query path calls signOut()/applyUser(null) on a 401.
Recommendation

Add a QueryCache/MutationCache onError to queryClient (or a check in apiFetch) that, on 401/unauthenticated, calls applyUser(null) so AuthGate re-renders login/walkthrough. Re-probe /api/me on window focus or a timer to detect expiry proactively.

high GET clients have no request timeout or AbortController — a hung request pins a query in 'pending' forever and never forwards React Query's signal production-readiness Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/api/iptv.ts:17-23 unverified
Description

Every client GET path calls fetch() with only { credentials:'include' } — no signal, no timeout. Only sonarr.ts/radarr.ts POSTs have a 60s AbortController; all GETs (iptv, media, sab, tmdb, grabs, suggestions, trending, recently-added, feedback, limits, plex-links) and the auth fetches have none. On the flaky NAS-over-Cloudflare-tunnel network this project explicitly fights (latest commit: 'tunnel-jitter rebuffering'), a stalled connection never rejects, so React Query stays 'pending' indefinitely — spinners that never resolve — and visibility-gated pollers can stack overlapping in-flight requests. React Query's queryFn AbortSignal is never forwarded, so cancelled queries keep their sockets open.

Evidence
iptv.ts:18 `const res = await fetch(apiUrl(...), { credentials: 'include' })` — no signal; identical in media.ts:16, sab.ts:8, tmdb.ts:37, grabs.ts:35, useTrending.ts (inline), useRecentlyAdded.ts (inline), useLimits.ts:45; only sonarr.ts:15-24 / radarr.ts:15-24 POSTs add an AbortController.
Recommendation

Add a default AbortController timeout in a shared apiFetch and forward React Query's queryFn `signal` so reads fail fast (retry/error) instead of hanging, and so cancelled queries abort their network calls.

high Sentry/Glitchtip initialized but NOT wired to capture Hono request errors — handler exceptions silently dropped despite mandatory-telemetry contract (§15) production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/index.ts:36-46; app.ts 34-171 unverified
Description

Sentry.init() runs at boot but the ONLY integration is onUnhandledRejectionIntegration. Grep across all non-test server .ts confirms Sentry appears only in index.ts (init) and telemetryPiiScrub.ts (setUser wrapper) — zero captureException, onError, requestHandler, or setupHono anywhere. In @sentry/node v9 with Hono, init does not auto-instrument route handlers; an exception thrown inside any /api/* handler becomes Hono's default 500 and is NEVER sent to Glitchtip. The contract makes telemetry MANDATORY (§15.1) and env.ts hard-fails prod boot without a DSN — yet the primary error class (handler exceptions) is uncaptured. Declared-but-effectively-unwired observability for the most important signal.

Evidence
index.ts 43-45: `integrations: [ Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration({ mode: 'warn' }) ]` is the only integration. app.ts global middleware is only `app.use('*', logger())` (36) and `app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin)` (66); no app.onError exists. Grep for captureException/onError/requestHandler/setupHono across non-test server *.ts: zero matches.
Recommendation

Register `app.onError((err, c) => { Sentry.captureException(err); return c.json({ error: 'internal' }, 500) })` in app.ts (or the framework Sentry handler), add a test that throws in a route and asserts captureException fired, and flush Sentry on shutdown. Set tracesSampleRate if any latency data is wanted (currently none).

high Rust transcoder AND media-core handle only ctrl_c (SIGINT); both IGNORE the SIGTERM Docker sends — every redeploy SIGKILLs in-flight transcodes after the grace period production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness crates/transcoder/src/main.rs:53-62 (and crates/media-core/src/main.rs 53-62) unverified
Description

Both services pass `with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal())` where shutdown_signal is `let _ = tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await`. tokio::signal::ctrl_c registers ONLY SIGINT. Docker/compose stop sends SIGTERM, which neither service handles — confirmed by grep: zero SignalKind/signal::unix/terminate matches in either crate. So in production the graceful path NEVER fires; the container runs until Docker's ~10s grace elapses, then SIGKILL hard-aborts every in-flight ffmpeg transcode mid-segment on every `docker compose up -d`. Identical defect in both Rust binaries.

Evidence
transcoder/src/main.rs 54 `.with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal())`, 59-62 `async fn shutdown_signal() { let _ = tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await; ... }`. media-core/src/main.rs 54/59-62 identical. Grep `SignalKind|signal::unix|terminate` across crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src returned zero matches.
Recommendation

Use tokio::signal::unix to await BOTH SIGINT and SIGTERM via select! before invoking axum graceful shutdown; ensure spawned ffmpeg sessions get SIGTERM with a bounded grace then SIGKILL. Apply to both crates.

high Node graceful shutdown does not drain in-flight requests/streams or kill ffmpeg children, and a SECOND competing signal handler races it production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/index.ts:72-102 (+ iptvScheduler.ts implicit cron, iptvRemux.ts sessions Map) unverified
Description

The index.ts SIGTERM/SIGINT handler closes the two SQLite singletons then immediately process.exit()s. It never (a) stops the listener — serve()'s returned http.Server is DISCARDED at line 72 so there is no handle to .close(); (b) awaits in-flight requests; (c) drains the iptvRemux sessions Map (which holds live ffmpeg children); or (d) stops the node-cron tasks. iptvRemux DOES have per-session SIGTERM->SIGKILL teardown, but shutdown() never calls stopRemuxSession for the live sessions, so on deploy every live viewer is hard-killed and ffmpeg children are orphaned (the 5s SIGKILL timer is unref'd, so process.exit can fire before it runs). The cron tasks registered in iptvScheduler are never stopped, so a sync mid-write to iptv.db can race the closeIptvDb() call in shutdown.

Evidence
index.ts 72 `serve({ fetch: app.fetch, port: env.port }, ...)` return discarded; 93-99 `function shutdown(signal){ closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...) }`. iptvRemux.ts maintains `const sessions = new Map(...)` (45) with live `proc: ChildProcess` (27) never drained on shutdown. iptvScheduler.ts cron.schedule tasks (29, 34) are never cancelled.
Recommendation

Capture `const server = serve(...)`; in one centralized shutdown: stop accepting new streams, server.close() and await in-flight with a bounded timeout, stop cron tasks, iterate sessions and stopRemuxSession() each (awaiting child exit), THEN closeIptvDb()/closeServerDb()/exit. Replace the unref'd SIGKILL timer with an awaited bounded kill during drain.

high No crash telemetry in 3 of 4 runtimes — both Rust services and the Python recommender have ZERO Sentry/Glitchtip wiring despite §15 making it mandatory production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness crates/media-core/src/main.rs:10-15 (Rust); recommender/app/main.py 1-93 (Python) unverified
Description

The contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15). Only the Node backend initializes Sentry. Grep found NO sentry/sentry-sdk references in crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src or their Cargo.toml, and NONE in recommender/app or pyproject. The Rust services use tracing_subscriber to stdout only; the Python service uses logging.basicConfig to stdout only. So a panic in media-core/transcoder or an unhandled 500 in the recommender produces zero events in Glitchtip — the operator is blind to crashes in the entire media-serving and recommendation path, which is exactly where production failures will concentrate (ffmpeg, scanning, vector queries).

Evidence
Grep `sentry|glitchtip` across crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src + Cargo.toml: only tracing_subscriber::fmt() (media-core/main.rs 10, transcoder/main.rs 7). Grep `sentry|sentry_sdk|glitchtip` across recommender/app + pyproject.toml: zero matches. recommender main.py 76 only `logging.basicConfig(...)`.
Recommendation

Add sentry (Rust) and sentry-sdk (Python) initialized from the same EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, with the canonical PII scrubber's denylist applied (the contract anticipates a Rust canonical impl). At minimum wire FastAPI's exception middleware to sentry-sdk and add a panic hook in the Rust binaries. If a runtime is intentionally exempt, document it against §15.

high Backups are manual and migration-gated only — no scheduled/automated backup, and a project audit records the Python backup gate as silently weakened with 2 RED tests data-integrity Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/services/migrator.ts:54-92; docker-compose.yml 24-27/135/188-189 unverified
Description

There IS a destructive-migration backup gate (migrator.ts refuses `-- DESTRUCTIVE` SQL unless server_state.last_backup_at is within 10 minutes) and a POST /api/admin/backup endpoint — good. But that only fires on destructive migrations; there is NO scheduled/automated backup of server.db, iptv.db, media.db, or the recommender's exchange.db. The only routine durability is a bind-mounted host directory. env.ts 531-535 warns that losing server.db 'silently revokes all device tokens' and regenerates server_id (every paired Apple device bricked) — a non-migration data loss (bad disk, accidental volume delete, corrupted -wal on power loss) is unrecoverable. Worse, docs/AUDIT-2026-05-28 records that the Python recommender's backup gate was silently weakened so two tests now go RED (auto-backup short-circuits the sibling-missing/stale-timestamp abort branches) — i.e. the cross-language backup contract is already drifting and being shipped 'green' falsely.

Evidence
migrator.ts 68-92 gate logic; 283-285 enforcement. docker-compose bind-mounts only (backend 27, recommender 135, media-core 188-189); no backup service/cron. docs/AUDIT-2026-05-28-honesty-and-best-practice.md: 'recommender Python suite is RED ... 2 failed ... _check_backup_gate now calls _auto_backup(...) first and returns on success, short-circuiting the sibling-server.db-missing and stale-timestamp abort branches'.
Recommendation

Add scheduled sqlite .backup/VACUUM INTO snapshots (or litestream) for all four DBs to a separate retention location, plus a tested restore runbook; snapshot server.db before every deploy (the most frequent restart). Resolve the 2 RED Python backup-gate tests and reconcile the auto-backup-vs-abort contract across Node and Python before shipping.

high README.md describes a dead V1 Sonarr/Radarr/SAB dashboard - the front-door doc misrepresents the entire product docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty README.md:1-70 unverified
Description

The top-of-repo README, the first artifact a new engineer or App-Store reviewer reads, describes a superseded V1: a 4-tab dashboard that 'replaces the operator-grade UIs of Sonarr, Radarr, and SAB', served by a single Nginx container, with NO auth, NO IPTV, NO recommender, NO media-core, NO transcoder, NO Hono backend, NO mention of the Apple/App-Store goal. The real product (confirmed by DEPLOY.md, docker-compose.yml, and the code) is an invite-only IPTV viewer + Plex bridge + Rust media-core + ffmpeg transcoder + Python recommender + a React SPA on a Hono server doing SIWA+Plex auth. README does not merely omit; it asserts a different architecture. DEPLOY.md already KNOWS README is stale (DEPLOY.md:16) yet README carries no warning.

Evidence
README.md:3 'A unified media dashboard at http://theemeraldexchange.local:8085 that replaces the operator-grade UIs of Sonarr, Radarr, and SAB.'; README.md:16 'Watch (-> Plex)   .   TV   .   Movies   .   Downloads'; README.md:58-67 V1 phase list ending at 'Deploy'. IPTV, auth, recommender, transcoder, media-core, Hono, Apple, App Store appear NOWHERE. Real topology: docker-compose.yml defines backend+recommender+media-core+transcoder+cloudflared+glitchtip services.
Recommendation

Rewrite README.md for the actual system, or prepend a line-1 banner: 'SUPERSEDED - this describes the V1 *arr dashboard. Current product + status: see DEPLOY.md and docs/ROADMAP-STATUS.md.' Cheapest, highest-leverage onboarding fix in the repo.

high README documents an Nginx-injects-API-keys security model that is provably false against the code security Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty README.md:11,26,32-38 unverified
Description

README tells a new engineer the security model is: Nginx reverse-proxies the *arr/SAB APIs and injects API keys server-side 'so they never reach the browser' (README:11), bills nginx/default.conf as 'Production Nginx with API-key-injecting reverse proxies' (README:26), and says local setup means SSHing into the NAS to grep Sonarr/Radarr/SAB keys out of config XML (README:33-34). ALL of this is false against the code: nginx/default.conf is a plain SPA static-serve with ZERO proxy/api-key blocks (only location / try_files, /index.html no-cache, /assets caching - lines 14-27), and nginx is not a service in docker-compose.yml. The real model is session+SIWA+Plex auth through a Hono backend with STREAM/DEVICE/INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL token secrets (compose:33-69). Documenting the wrong security model can lead an engineer to assume there is no user auth or to build against a phantom proxy.

Evidence
README.md:11 'Nginx (Docker) ... injecting API keys server-side so they never reach the browser'; README.md:26 'nginx/default.conf - Production Nginx with API-key-injecting reverse proxies.' vs nginx/default.conf (full): only try_files / index.html no-cache / assets immutable - NO proxy_pass, NO api-key headers. docker-compose.yml has no nginx/dashboard service (grep exchange-dashboard = 0). DEPLOY.md:123 'the nginx/ directory ... is dead code'.
Recommendation

Replace README's security section with the real auth surface (SIWA + Plex invite allowlist, Hono csrf/deviceTokenAuth/ssrfGuard middleware); delete the obsolete *arr-key-via-SSH path. Follow DEPLOY.md's own advice and git rm nginx/.

high PRODUCT.md is entirely V1 and contradicts the Plex-replacement / App-Store mission on three concrete, verifiable points docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty PRODUCT.md:9,41,47,54 unverified
Description

PRODUCT.md, the 'Impeccable gate' README says governs strategy (README:23), describes only the V1 dashboard and contradicts the shipped product. It frames Plex as something you OPEN, not replace ('open Plex', PRODUCT.md:9; 'Watch (-> Plex)', PRODUCT.md:47) - the literal opposite of the goal to be a FULL PLEX REPLACEMENT. It bans recommendation rails (PRODUCT.md:41) while the repo ships a Python recommender service AND a documented AI recommendation pipeline (docs/recommendations.md). It lists 'Netlify production deploy' as out-of-scope V2+ (PRODUCT.md:54) even though Netlify is the SHIPPED production frontend (DEPLOY.md:3-6). A new engineer using PRODUCT.md as the 'why' would make decisions opposite to the actual roadmap.

Evidence
PRODUCT.md:9 '...see what's downloading, open Plex.'; PRODUCT.md:41 'Search is the verb. ... no recommendation rails.'; PRODUCT.md:47 'Watch (-> Plex) ...'; PRODUCT.md:54 'Netlify production deploy. All deferred to V2 or later.' Contradicted by docs/recommendations.md (live AI pipeline) + compose recommender service; DEPLOY.md:3 'The SPA lives on Netlify'.
Recommendation

Rewrite PRODUCT.md for the current product (Plex replacement, invite-only multi-household, recommender-driven, App-Store-bound) or add a superseded banner; at minimum fix the three direct contradictions.

high SSRF guard validates hostname only and never pins the resolved IP — DNS-rebinding bypass at the IPTV segment/sub-playlist proxy security Cross-Cutting: Security server/services/ssrfGuard.ts:62-71 (consumed at routes/iptv.ts:1024,680,1035,682) unverified
Description

isPublicHttpsUpstream inspects url.hostname only; isPrivateIPv4/isPrivateIPv6 match ONLY IP literals (ssrfGuard.ts:18 regex matches dotted-quad), so any DOMAIN name (the normal case for CDN segment URLs) skips every private-range branch. The subsequent fetch() (iptv.ts:1035,682) does its OWN DNS resolution. An attacker who controls an HLS manifest line — the exact threat the guard's own comment at iptv.ts:1013-1023 describes — can point a segment at attacker.com resolving to a public IP at check time and 169.254.169.254 / 127.0.0.1 / an RFC1918 address (e.g. media-core:8002, recommender:8000) at fetch time, defeating the check. No resolve-then-validate-then-pin step and no per-redirect re-validation.

Evidence
ssrfGuard.ts:64-70 `const host = url.hostname; if (isPrivateIPv4(host)) return false; if (host.includes(':') && isPrivateIPv6(host)) return false; if (isInternalHostname(host)) return false; return true`. Guard result trusted at iptv.ts:1024 `if (!isPublicHttpsUpstream(url)) return c.json({error:'bad_upstream'},400)` immediately before iptv.ts:1035 `await fetch(upstream, ...)` which re-resolves DNS independently.
Recommendation

Resolve the host yourself (dns.lookup {all:true}), reject if ANY resolved address is private/reserved, then connect to the pinned IP carrying the original Host header, re-validating on every redirect hop. Without IP pinning the private-range logic is bypassable by any attacker controlling a manifest URL or redirect — the modeled threat for an IPTV proxy.

high clippy and cargo fmt are continue-on-error — Rust lint/format regressions cannot turn CI red testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor .github/workflows/ci.yml:232-240 unverified
Description

Both `cargo fmt --all -- --check` and `cargo clippy --all-targets --workspace -- -D warnings` are marked continue-on-error: true. The `-D warnings` flag is therefore cosmetic: clippy can emit any number of denied warnings and the job stays green. For a workspace that is the CANONICAL cross-language source of truth for crypto/token formats (emerald-contracts) plus the transcoder and media-core, soft-failing static analysis means real correctness smells (unwrap-in-Drop, integer-overflow lints, dead error paths) ship unnoticed. The comment admits the deferral ('Allow this to soft-fail until we wire a pre-commit hook') but no such hook exists in the repo and the deferral has no expiry.

Evidence
ci.yml:236 `continue-on-error: true` under cargo fmt --check; ci.yml:240 `continue-on-error: true` under `cargo clippy --all-targets --workspace -- -D warnings`.
Recommendation

Remove continue-on-error from clippy now (it already runs and the workspace builds clean per my cargo check, so the cost is near-zero). Keep fmt as a separate hard-failing job, or auto-format via pre-commit AND enforce in CI. A crypto-contract crate must not allow clippy regressions to merge silently.

high Playwright 'E2E' is a frontend smoke test against fully-mocked APIs — zero true end-to-end coverage of the real backend testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor playwright.config.ts:4-7, 27-34 unverified
Description

The Playwright job is the only browser-driven suite and the only thing that could exercise full user flows. By design it boots Vite ALONE (npm run dev:vite, no Hono backend) and every /api/* call is intercepted by page.route() mocks in mockApi.ts. So auth.spec.ts, add-movie-search-mode.spec.ts, and downloads-permissioning.spec.ts validate React rendering against canned JSON — they never touch session cookies, CSRF middleware, the device-token JWE path, the real member allowlist, or the recommender. Net effect: the most realistic-looking green check proves the least about production correctness. Apple/passkey round-trip against the real verifier, member-gate enforcement, IPTV player error recovery, and transcode playback have NO end-to-end test anywhere.

Evidence
playwright.config.ts:4-7: 'Thin browser-smoke layer ... We start Vite alone ... because every /api/* call is intercepted via page.route() in each spec. Booting a real backend just to mock it would be wasted ceremony'. tests/e2e/helpers/mockApi.ts intercepts all backend routes.
Recommendation

Add a second Playwright project that boots the REAL server with a seeded SQLite DB and runs a small set of true E2E specs: a full Apple/passkey auth round-trip, a denied non-member hitting the gate, and an IPTV play/stop. Keep the mocked specs for fast UI iteration but stop calling the mocked layer 'E2E' in CI — it is a render smoke test.

high media-core TMDB matcher takes results.first() with no confidence/language scoring, and has NO accuracy benchmark and NO <5s/100-file perf fixture testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor crates/media-core/src/tmdb.rs:84 (selection); scanner.rs:11 (unenforced target) unverified
Description

M3's value proposition is automatically matching scanned files to TMDB metadata (the Plex 'it just identifies my library' experience). Confirmed in code: parse_search_response does `doc.get("results")?.as_array()?.first()?` (tmdb.rs:84) — it blindly takes the first result with no popularity/title-similarity/year-confirmation/language scoring. A first()-takes-all matcher silently mis-identifies foreign-language and same-title films, and nothing in the suite would catch it: there is NO accuracy fixture asserting precision over a set of known-correct titles. media-core has NO tests/ directory at all (only inline #[cfg(test)] unit tests), NO criterion bench and NO [[bench]] in any crates/*/Cargo.toml, and scanner.rs:11 states 'Target: a 100-file fixture library scans in < 5s' but no test times it — so the perf budget is documentation, not a gate.

Evidence
tmdb.rs:84 `let first = doc.get("results")?.as_array()?.first()?;` with no scoring of the array. scanner.rs:11 `//! Target: a 100-file fixture library scans in < 5s (§ success criteria).` No benches/ dir; no criterion/[[bench]] in Cargo.toml; no media-core tests/ dir.
Recommendation

Commit a fixture set of ~30 representative filenames with known-correct TMDB ids and assert match precision in a test (catch foreign-title / same-title regressions). Add year/title-similarity scoring to the selection instead of first(). Add a criterion bench (or a timed test) that scans 100 synthetic files and asserts wall-clock under the stated 5s budget on a fixed CI runner class.

medium No rate limit, request timeout, or concurrency cap on /score; a 5000-item inline library forces full embedding stacks in-process per request performance Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/app/context.py:load_user_context 235-268; schemas.py library max_length=5000; retrieval.py overfetch 48 unverified
Description

ScoreRequest.library/feedback/household_rejections are each capped at 5000 items. A /score call with 5000 library ids triggers _load_title_rows + _stack_embeddings which np.vstacks up to 5000 384-dim float32 vectors (and again for liked/disliked) on the request thread before any recipe runs; retrieve_candidates over-fetches max(pool_size*3, pool_size+len(excluded), 200) KNN rows. There is no per-request timeout, no concurrency semaphore, and no rate limit. At App-Store scale with many concurrent households this is a CPU/allocation amplification vector on a single sqlite-vec process. elapsed_ms is measured but nothing acts on it.

Evidence
schemas.py: 'library: list[LibraryItem] | None = Field(default=None, max_length=5000)'. context.py positive_centroid/_stack_embeddings build np.concatenate/np.vstack over all supplied ids. No timeout/semaphore/limiter anywhere in main.py.
Recommendation

Add a server-side concurrency semaphore around recipe scoring, a hard request timeout, and a smaller default inline-library cap; reject oversized inline libraries and force /events/library/sync instead.

medium sub_validation and internal_principal fail OPEN when the emerald_contracts wheel is absent — security boundaries degrade to pass-through with only a log line security Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/app/sub_validation.py:23-34, 42-54 (and internal_principal.py 37-49 'log' mode) unverified
Description

validate_sub() enforces the canonical <provider>:<id> contract (section 8). If emerald_contracts fails to import, _AVAILABLE=False and validate_sub() returns the value UNCHANGED — any arbitrary sub passes to SQL (still parameterized, so not injection, but it defeats the cross-service identity contract and lets malformed/unnamespaced subs partition feedback). internal_principal.py has the same fail-open shape in 'log' mode. The only thing guaranteeing the wheel in prod is a code comment ('CI guarantees the wheel is present in the prod image'), not a runtime assertion — a wheel ABI mismatch on a Python minor-version bump would silently disable the gate.

Evidence
sub_validation.py: 'if not _AVAILABLE: return value' with comment 'we log once at import time and skip validation — the recommender keeps working but loses the cross-language parity gate'.
Recommendation

In NODE_ENV=production, fail startup (or 503 /score) if emerald_contracts is unavailable instead of passing all subs. Expose is_available() in /health so the degraded state is observable.

medium Feedback outcome attribution silently drops any signal older than a hard-coded 10-minute window, starving the optimizer of training data data-integrity Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/app/main.py:235-266 (FEEDBACK_ATTRIBUTION_MINUTES=10 window) unverified
Description

post_feedback only writes a rec_outcomes row if a matching rec_log row exists with ts within the last 10 minutes; otherwise it logs a warning and writes nothing to rec_outcomes. A user who likes a recommendation 11 minutes after it was shown produces user_feedback but NO outcome, so the optimizer never learns from it. Combined with run()'s 'total_outcomes < 50 -> skip' guard, on a realistic small household this can mean the optimizer almost never accrues enough signal to run while the operator believes the learning loop is active. This is a silent learning-signal leak that directly undermines the self-improvement premise.

Evidence
main.py: 'WHERE ... datetime(ts) >= datetime("now", "-10 minutes") ... if rec is not None: insert rec_outcomes else: log.warning("feedback attribution skipped: no in-session rec_log row ...")'. optimizer run(): 'if stats.total_outcomes < 50: ... return 0'.
Recommendation

Make the attribution window configurable and widen it substantially, or attribute to the most recent rec_log row regardless of age with an age column; track and surface the attribution-skip rate as an operational metric.

medium Nightly optimizer eval runs against the LIVE production DB; correctness depends on a single load-bearing persist_library=False flag data-integrity Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/workers/optimizer.py:_evaluate_entries (load_user_context persist_library=False); run() conn = connect() unverified
Description

evaluate_pair runs the full holdout score-pass against the same connect() used in production (exchange.db). The only thing preventing holdout tmdb_ids from being written into the live library_items table is persist_library=False, flagged 'load-bearing' in a comment. There is no separate read-only or snapshot DB for eval, and the pass competes for the single WAL writer during the eval window. A future edit that defaults persist_library=True, or a recipe that writes, would mutate production state from an offline job.

Evidence
_evaluate_entries: 'persist_library=False is load-bearing here: eval reads the same DB as production and must not write holdout tmdb_ids into the live library_items table.' run() uses 'conn = connect()' (the read-write production path; connect() supports readonly=True but it is not used here).
Recommendation

Run eval against connect(readonly=True) or a snapshot copy. Defense-in-depth beats a load-bearing comment for a path that can mutate production data.

medium Blocking std::fs calls inside async handlers while holding the sessions mutex performance Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/session.rs:67, 121, 142 unverified
Description

start() calls std::fs::create_dir_all (line 67), stop() calls std::fs::remove_dir_all (line 121), and the reaper calls std::fs::remove_dir_all (line 142) — all synchronous blocking syscalls on tokio worker threads. The reaper holds the sessions Mutex (locked at line 131) across the entire stale-removal loop including each blocking remove_dir_all, so during cleanup of a large work dir (hundreds of unbounded .ts segments — see the disk finding) all create/heartbeat/get/stop operations are blocked, and clients can spuriously miss the 30s heartbeat window. At single-NAS scale this is tolerable but degrades badly under concurrent load.

Evidence
session.rs:67 `std::fs::create_dir_all(&work_subdir)?;`, 121 `let _ = std::fs::remove_dir_all(&rec.work_subdir);`, 142 same inside the reaper while `sessions` is locked from line 131.
Recommendation

Use tokio::fs (async) or tokio::task::spawn_blocking, and in the reaper collect the records to remove, drop the lock, then do the FS cleanup outside the critical section.

medium No Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry despite the project's mandatory-telemetry contract (§15) production-readiness Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/Cargo.toml:14-25 unverified
Description

Project memory/contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15). The transcoder has no sentry/glitchtip dependency and no error reporting beyond tracing to stdout. Combined with no stderr drain and no child.wait(), an ffmpeg failure in production produces zero captured diagnostics — you cannot even see why a transcode died. main.rs uses .expect() on bind/serve so a startup failure is a bare panic with no crash report.

Evidence
Cargo.toml dependencies: tokio, axum, serde, serde_json, tracing, tracing-subscriber, uuid, thiserror — no sentry/glitchtip. main.rs:60 `.expect("failed to bind transcoder listener")`, main.rs:65-66 `.expect("transcoder server crashed")`.
Recommendation

Wire the sentry/glitchtip Rust SDK (DSN distributed at boot per the §15 island model), report ffmpeg non-zero exits with the captured stderr tail, and avoid bare .expect panics for runtime failures.

medium AppState.work_dir cleanup gap: child work subdirs are only removed on stop/reap, not on natural ffmpeg completion data-integrity Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/session.rs:115-148 unverified
Description

Work subdirs (/work/{id}) are removed only in stop() and the heartbeat reaper. There is no removal path when a session is created but the process exits and the client never explicitly DELETEs — the reaper will eventually catch it via heartbeat staleness, but only if heartbeats stop. If a buggy/abandoned client created sessions that error out instantly (ffmpeg fails — see Docker/stderr findings) yet kept the heartbeat alive, the empty/partial dir persists. There is also no startup sweep of work_dir to clear orphans left by a previous crash, so a crash-loop leaks directories across restarts.

Evidence
Removal occurs only at session.rs:121 (stop) and 142 (reap). main.rs creates SessionManager (line ~47) and spawns the reaper but performs no initial work_dir sweep.
Recommendation

On boot, clear/recreate work_dir to drop crash orphans. Tie subdir cleanup to the child.wait() completion task so any terminal state (success/failure/kill) cleans up deterministically.

medium Encoder probe matches by substring and selects HW encoders that are never validated to actually work correctness Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/encoders.rs:54-58, 70-77 unverified
Description

probe() detects HW encoders by `trimmed.contains(hw)` over `ffmpeg -encoders` output — a presence-in-build check, not a works-on-this-hardware check. ffmpeg lists h264_nvenc/h264_vaapi/h264_qsv even when no GPU/driver is present, so best_h264() can pick a HW encoder that fails at runtime (e.g. h264_vaapi with no /dev/dri, h264_nvenc with no NVIDIA runtime — the compose service mounts no GPU devices). Because no real transcode has run, this has never failed, but the first GPU-less deploy that lists nvenc would spawn ffmpeg, have it die immediately on device-open, and (per the stderr/wait findings) report nothing and hang as 'running'. The roadmap's 'HW encoder paths exist only as arg strings, never exercised' is confirmed.

Evidence
encoders.rs:55 `if trimmed.contains(hw)`; best_h264 returns the first listed HW name (71-76). docker-compose transcoder block (lines 44-58) mounts no devices and adds no GPU runtime, yet the image's ffmpeg may still advertise hw encoders.
Recommendation

Validate each candidate HW encoder with a tiny test encode (e.g. ffmpeg -f lavfi -i color -t 0.1 -c:v <enc> -f null -) during probe and only register ones that succeed, with automatic libx264 fallback at session start if the HW encode fails. Gate HW selection on the presence of the relevant device mount.

medium Only the first audio track is handled and audio is force-downmixed to stereo — Plex-parity gap for multi-track / surround feature-gap Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/plan.rs:129-142 unverified
Description

The plan models a single audio stream (SourceProbe.audio_codec is scalar) and, when re-encoding, hardcodes 2-channel AAC (APPLE_SAFE_AUDIO_CHANNELS), discarding 5.1/7.1. There is no -map selection, so ffmpeg's default mapping (one video + one audio) drops all other audio tracks and all subtitle tracks. Plex preserves/streams multiple audio languages, surround passthrough, and selectable subtitles. As built, a foreign film with English + original audio and forced subs would expose only one track and downmix surround to stereo whenever a re-encode is needed.

Evidence
plan.rs:138-140 `encoder: "aac", bitrate_kbps: 160, channels: 2 (APPLE_SAFE_AUDIO_CHANNELS)`; SourceProbe has a single audio_codec/audio_channels (plan.rs:29,35); args.rs emits no -map flags. plan.rs:140 always uses stereo regardless of probe.audio_channels.
Recommendation

Model multiple audio/subtitle streams, add -map for explicit track selection, support per-track copy vs encode, and only downmix when the client truly cannot handle the channel layout (keep AC3/EAC3 passthrough or 5.1 AAC where the client supports it). This is required for genuine Plex parity.

medium mpegts.js recovery counter is monotonic for the effect lifetime — a healthy multi-hour stream that hiccups 5 times total is permanently downgraded to terminal error correctness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/player/IptvPlayer.tsx:319-337 unverified
Description

`let recoveries = 0` is scoped to the effect and only ever increments. Live IPTV over a tunnel legitimately hits transient network errors spread across a long viewing session. After the 5th lifetime hiccup — even if each prior one recovered cleanly minutes apart — the next transient error shows 'Live stream interrupted. Close and re-open the channel to retry.' and gives up, despite the stream being fundamentally healthy. Real players reset their backoff after a sustained good period; this never does.

Evidence
`let recoveries = 0; player.on(mpegts.Events.ERROR, ...) { ... if ((isNetwork || isMedia) && recoveries < 5) { recoveries += 1; ... return } setError('Live stream interrupted. Close and re-open the channel to retry.') }` — recoveries is never reset after a successful resume.
Recommendation

Reset recoveries to 0 after N seconds of uninterrupted playback (e.g. on a timer armed on each successful timeupdate burst), so the cap means '5 failures in quick succession' not '5 ever'. Also render a Retry button on the terminal error so the user need not close and re-open.

medium startPositionSecs resume seek is best-effort, applied uniformly to live and VOD, with no post-seek verification correctness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/player/IptvPlayer.tsx:136-141 unverified
Description

Resume seeking sets video.currentTime = startPositionSecs inside onLoadedMetadata, regardless of grant.delivery. For hls.js/mpegts MSE streams, loadedmetadata can fire before a seekable range exists, so currentTime= may be silently clamped to 0. The value is applied even when the grant is live (though callers only pass startPositionSecs for VOD/series, the player itself does not distinguish). There is no 'seeked'/'canplay' re-assertion if the seek was clamped. This undermines Plex-grade resume on exactly the HLS-VOD transport where it matters.

Evidence
`const onLoadedMetadata = () => { updateNativeTracks(); if (startPositionSecs && Number.isFinite(startPositionSecs) && startPositionSecs > 0) { video.currentTime = startPositionSecs } }` — no grant.delivery gate, no verification the seek took.
Recommendation

Prefer hls.js startPosition config for MSE, re-assert currentTime on the first 'canplay' if it was clamped to ~0, and skip resume entirely for live delivery.

medium Track-switch dropdowns can desync from the engine: eager updateHlsTracks before MANIFEST_PARSED and no *_TRACK_SWITCHED reconciliation correctness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/player/IptvPlayer.tsx:243-249, 361-389 unverified
Description

updateHlsTracks() is called immediately after attachMedia (line 249) before MANIFEST_PARSED has populated hls.audioTracks, so the selectors can be empty on first paint and selectedAudio reflects uninitialized engine state. chooseAudioTrack sets hls.audioTrack and optimistically setSelectedAudio(trackId), but the AUDIO_TRACKS_UPDATED listener re-runs updateHlsTracks and resets setSelectedAudio(hls.audioTrack); there is no subscription to AUDIO_TRACK_SWITCHED/SUBTITLE_TRACK_SWITCHED to confirm the actually-playing track, so the UI value can diverge from what plays if hls.js clamps the selection.

Evidence
`hls.on(Hls.Events.AUDIO_TRACKS_UPDATED, updateHlsTracks)` resets selected state; `chooseAudioTrack` does `hls.audioTrack = trackId; setSelectedAudio(trackId)` with no SWITCHED confirmation; `updateHlsTracks()` is invoked at line 249 before MANIFEST_PARSED.
Recommendation

Subscribe to AUDIO_TRACK_SWITCHED / SUBTITLE_TRACK_SWITCHED and set selected state from the confirmed event; drop the eager pre-MANIFEST_PARSED updateHlsTracks() call.

medium Player exposes no buffering/connecting state and no retry affordance — users see a black <video> box for seconds on a slow tunnel production-readiness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/player/IptvPlayer.tsx:391-432 unverified
Description

The render is the native <video controls> plus optional track selectors plus a single <p> error string. Given the deliberately deep buffers, attach+fill can take several seconds during which there is no spinner/poster/connecting indicator and no state distinguishing connecting / playing / recovering. The terminal error is a bare paragraph with no Retry button, even though the engine supports re-init via a grant/key change. Plex always surfaces connection state; this player does not.

Evidence
Render returns `<div className={styles.player}><video ... controls .../>{trackSelectors}{error && <p className={styles.error}>{error}</p>}</div>` — no waiting/buffering UI, no retry control.
Recommendation

Track a status state (connecting|playing|recovering|error), show a spinner/poster while connecting/recovering, and render a Retry button on terminal error that re-keys the engine effect.

medium No app-level expiry/clock-skew test for the §4 JWE; Node has no verify path and key rotation (kid keymap) is comment-only security Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/services/internalPrincipal.ts:31,79-110 unverified
Description

The cross-binding test proves N-API mint -> PyO3 decrypt round-trips claims, but it sets a fixed exp and asserts ONLY equality — no test asserts that the receiver REJECTS an expired (exp in the past) or tampered JWE. The whole point of the 60s TTL (§4) is replay containment; that enforcement is unverified by any test in this repo. The Node side is mint-only (the test itself states 'N-API doesn't have internal_principal_decrypt', line 230-231), so Hono cannot verify an inbound principal if a service ever calls back. The kid is a single hardcoded constant INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_KID='internal-v1' (line 31); the rotation 'verifier keymap stays populated with both kids' is described only in the docstring — there is no code that accepts two kids, so a real key rotation would instantly invalidate every in-flight token. INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_TTL_SECS=60 (line 35) is exported but enforcement lives entirely on the Python receiver, which was not in scope to confirm.

Evidence
internalPrincipal.ts:31 `export const INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_KID = 'internal-v1'` (only kid). crossBinding.test.ts:161-163 uses fixed `iat: 1748169600, exp: 1748169660` and asserts equality only; no negative test for expired/tampered tokens. crossBinding.test.ts:230 comment: 'N-API doesn't have internal_principal_decrypt (Hono only mints, never receives)'.
Recommendation

Add negative cross-binding tests: a JWE with exp in the past must be REJECTED by PyO3 decrypt; a single-byte-flipped ciphertext must fail. Make the kid env-driven and implement an actual multi-kid verifier keymap before any rotation. Confirm (with a test) the receiver applies bounded clock skew, not unbounded acceptance.

medium No global error handler and no request body-size limit on the Hono app production-readiness Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/app.ts:34-66 unverified
Description

app.ts registers only logger, conditional CORS, and requireSafeOrigin (verified: the only app.use calls are lines 36, 42, 66) — there is no app.onError(), no app.notFound(), and no bodyLimit anywhere in bootstrap. Any route handler that throws yields Hono's default framework-shaped 500 rather than a consistent scrubbed JSON envelope, and such throws are not funneled to Sentry from a central place (only unhandledRejection is integrated). With no body limit, every POST (feedback, invites, device pairing, recommender events, the media proxy which does c.req.arrayBuffer() at media.ts:65) accepts an arbitrarily large payload — a trivial memory-pressure DoS on a NAS and at App-Store scale. sanitize.ts caps title length at 200 only AFTER the full body is parsed, so it does not bound ingress.

Evidence
app.ts middleware is exactly: app.use('*', logger()) (36), conditional cors (42-59), app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin) (66). No onError/notFound/bodyLimit/secureHeaders present anywhere in the file. media.ts:65 `body = await c.req.arrayBuffer()` with no size guard.
Recommendation

Add app.onError() mapping known errors to a stable JSON shape, reporting unknowns to Sentry, never leaking internals; add app.notFound(); add bodyLimit (e.g. 256KB JSON) before bodies are read; add secureHeaders() on API responses.

medium Shutdown closes DBs before stopping the HTTP listener and never drains in-flight requests production-readiness Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/index.ts:72,93-99 unverified
Description

shutdown() calls closeIptvDb() then closeServerDb() then process.exit() synchronously. The serve() return value is discarded (line 72), so the listener is never stopped, and there is no drain wait. On SIGTERM from docker/compose during a NAS redeploy, the DBs are closed out from under any request still executing a prepared statement — better-sqlite3 will throw 'The database connection is not open' on that in-flight request (e.g. an active device-token verify doing a SELECT on device_tokens, or a streaming media proxy). Closing DBs before stopping the listener is backwards: you must stop accepting/finish requests first, THEN close DBs. This matters for a 24/7 home server that restarts on every update.

Evidence
index.ts:72 `serve({ fetch: app.fetch, port: env.port }, ...)` — return value not captured. index.ts:93-99 shutdown(): `closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...)` — no server.close(), no drain.
Recommendation

Capture the handle from serve(); in shutdown() call server.close() first to stop accepting new connections, await a short drain window for in-flight requests, then closeIptvDb()/closeServerDb(), then exit. Prevents mid-request DB-closed errors and abrupt stream cuts on redeploy.

medium version.ts hardcodes auth_modes to ['plex'] only, contradicting the shipped Apple PIN-pair + passkey/local auth surface feature-gap Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/routes/version.ts:20-23 unverified
Description

The public /api/version response — which Apple apps use to decide which sign-in buttons to render (the file header says so explicitly) — always returns auth_modes: ['plex'] with a comment 'Plex is the only mode supported today.' But app.ts:107 mounts /api/auth/device (Apple PIN-pair), app.ts:111 mounts /api/auth/passkey, and migration 0003/0004 + the members allowlist establish apple:/local: subs and a passkey/SIWA flow as shipped (MEMORY: 'Auth = SIWA + Plex parallel + invites'). A correct Apple/SPA client reading this endpoint would NOT offer Sign in with Apple or passkey, defeating the very M2 pairing flow the endpoint exists to enable. (accepting_device_pairs is correctly derived from deviceTokenSecret — only auth_modes is stale.)

Evidence
version.ts:21-23 `// Plex is the only mode supported today...` then `auth_modes.push('plex')`, no apple/local/passkey. Contrast app.ts:107 `app.route('/api/auth/device', device)` and app.ts:111 `app.route('/api/auth/passkey', passkey)`.
Recommendation

Derive auth_modes from actual configured capability (plex if PLEX_CLIENT_ID; apple if Apple SIWA creds present; local/passkey if a WebAuthn RP is configured) instead of a hardcoded ['plex']. Clients gate on this contract surface — it must reflect reality.

medium Read forwarders pass arbitrary client query strings straight to upstream Sonarr/Radarr security Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/sonarr.ts:68-90 unverified
Description

forwardRead copies the client's entire query string (new URL(c.req.url).searchParams) into the upstream fetch via url.searchParams.set for every allow-listed read path (/series, /episode, /queue, /series/lookup, etc.). Only the PATH is allow-listed; query params are unfiltered. The X-Api-Key is a header so a ?apikey= override is harmless, but this lets any authenticated user drive arbitrary Sonarr/Radarr query parameters — /api/v3/series/lookup?term=<arbitrary> issues outbound indexer/metadata lookups, and unexpected params can change upstream behavior (paging, includeImages bloat) and amplify response size. Combined with the no-rate-limit finding, this is the cheap-amplification surface.

Evidence
sonarr.ts:70-71 'const search = new URL(c.req.url).searchParams; const r = await sonarrFetch(path, { method: "GET" }, search)'; services/sonarr.ts:20 'for (const [k, v] of query.entries()) url.searchParams.set(k, v)' — no param-name allowlist.
Recommendation

Allow-list query parameters per endpoint (e.g. seriesId/movieId numeric, term length-capped) and drop the rest, mirroring the path allow-list philosophy the file header champions.

medium Grab-event /by-item is a guessable-id IDOR and grab events have no user attribution security Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/grabs.ts:31-48 unverified
Description

GET /api/grabs/by-item?app=sonarr&itemId=N is available to any authenticated user (only /recent is requireAdmin). The route comment concedes 'itemId acts as a weak capability.' Sonarr/Radarr ids are small sequential integers, so any user can enumerate itemId=1..N and read the full grab history (titles, sizes, quality, failure reasons) for everything anyone added. GrabEvent has no sub/userId field at all (grabLog.ts:31-51), so events cannot be scoped to the requester even if desired, and grab activity can never be attributed to a user for abuse investigation. Low blast radius for an invite-only household, but a genuine horizontal-authZ gap that matters at App-Store scale.

Evidence
grabs.ts:31 'grabs.get('/by-item', async (c) => {' with only a Number.isSafeInteger(itemId) check, no requireAdmin and no ownership filter; grabLog.ts GrabEvent type (31-51) has no sub/userId.
Recommendation

Record the initiating sub on GrabEvent at append time (the add routes already hold session.sub), and either filter /by-item to the caller's own events or apply a real capability check.

medium JSONL grab/usage logs lose history and tail-scan O(file) per request — won't survive App-Store scale performance Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/services/grabLog.ts:53, 84-89, 158-174 unverified
Description

The store keeps only the live file plus one rotated .1 (rename overwrites any previous .1), so on a busy household the second rotation silently discards all older history — breaking the very 'why did this fail months ago' use case the module exists for, at exactly the volume that makes it useful. readEventsForItem scans a SCAN_WINDOW of max(500, limit*50) lines backward and filters in memory on every poll, and the DownloadsTab/DetailModal poll it. summarizeUsage can hold up to SUMMARY_HARD_CAP=100k events (~30MB) per call with no caching. The module's own comment says 'If we ever need richer queries, swap this module for SQLite' — at the stated scale that time is now.

Evidence
grabLog.ts:53 'const ROTATE_AT_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024' with single .1 rotation (84-87 renames over the previous .1); grabLog.ts:167 'const SCAN_WINDOW = Math.max(500, limit * 50)' re-scanned per /by-item call.
Recommendation

Move grab + usage events into better-sqlite3 (already a dependency) with indexes on (app,itemId,ts) and (sub,ts); eliminates the rotation-data-loss, the per-poll tail scan, and the per-request in-memory filter.

medium Sonarr add fires grabTvUnderCap fire-and-forget, so users see success even when the cap grab silently grabs nothing correctness Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/sonarr.ts:708-721 unverified
Description

The Radarr path was deliberately changed to AWAIT the capped grab and roll back / return 424 on failure (radarr.ts:427-485). The Sonarr path still does 'void grabTvUnderCap(...).catch(...)' and returns the upstream 2xx immediately. So a TV add where every release is over the per-episode cap (all_rejected_by_cap) or the indexer returns nothing returns success to the user; the only trace is a console.log + a grab-log event. The series sits monitored with no files and no user-visible signal — exactly the 'apparently-successful add with nothing downloaded' failure the surrounding comments repeatedly try to avoid. The asymmetry with Radarr is also a maintenance hazard.

Evidence
sonarr.ts:711 'void grabTvUnderCap(itemId, monitored, itemTitle, folderSnapshot).catch((e) => {' vs radarr.ts:439 'const grab = await grabBestUnderCap(...)' with 424 surfacing on no_releases/all_rejected_by_cap.
Recommendation

Either await the TV grab and surface a 424-equivalent when nothing was grabbed (mirroring Radarr), or return an explicit 'add accepted, grab pending' status the SPA can poll the grab log against, so the silent-no-download case is visible.

medium Test coverage is thin on cap/rollback/materialize security invariants for the highest-stakes paths testing Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/sonarr.test.ts:662-779 unverified
Description

Coverage is good on disk-gate, DELETE bad-id, and non-admin materialize, but the Sonarr cap-selection logic (grabTvUnderCap: per-episode cap math, season-pack vs episode dedup, all_rejected_by_cap, all_rejected_by_profile, the planned_size_exceeds_free_space reservation path) is essentially untested — the suite explicitly opts OUT of exercising the background grab ('grabTvUnderCap runs via void ... real setTimeouts we don't want to wait on', sonarr.test.ts:662-669) and only asserts the grab is NOT spawned in the skip cases. Radarr's upgrade/grab path is well-tested by contrast, but the Sonarr cap math (the most complex code in the subsystem) has no direct unit test. sab.test.ts notes 'full coverage of SAB modes lives in integration' with no such integration test found in scope.

Evidence
sonarr.test.ts:662-669 comment 'the background grab uses real setTimeouts (2s + repeated 1.5s polls) we dont want to wait on ... force searchForMissingEpisodes:false to skip spawning the grab entirely'; no test calls grabTvUnderCap's selection logic directly.
Recommendation

Extract grabTvUnderCap's release-selection/dedup/cap math into a pure function and unit-test it (season-pack preference, overlap avoidance, per-episode cap, profile rejection, reservation overcommit), so the most complex logic in the subsystem has a regression net independent of setTimeout timing.

medium Hono request-handler errors are NOT forwarded to Sentry — server-side 500s (incl. auth failures) are invisible to the mandated telemetry production-readiness Server — Auth & AuthZ server/app.ts:34-171 (no app.onError + no Sentry.captureException anywhere) unverified
Description

Sentry.init IS called at boot (index.ts:37) with only the onUnhandledRejectionIntegration. There is NO app.onError handler and NO Sentry.captureException call anywhere in server/. Hono catches errors thrown inside route handlers/middleware and turns them into a default 500 response WITHOUT re-throwing, so those errors never reach Node's unhandled-rejection/uncaught-exception hooks and are therefore never captured by Sentry. Concretely: an exception in verifyDeviceToken's DB calls, mintDeviceToken's INSERT, a redeemInvite throw, or any /api/* handler becomes a silent 500 with no crash report — defeating the §15 'mandatory telemetry' intent for the most operationally important class of errors (server-side request failures). The DSN hard-requirement gives false confidence that request errors are observed.

Evidence
index.ts:37-46 Sentry.init has integrations: [onUnhandledRejectionIntegration] only. grep for `onError` / `captureException` across server/app.ts + server/index.ts returns nothing. Hono's default behavior swallows handler throws into a 500 it generates itself, which never triggers process-level handlers.
Recommendation

Add `app.onError((err, c) => { Sentry.captureException(err); return c.json({ error: 'internal' }, 500) })` (or @sentry/node's Hono error handler) so request-scoped exceptions are captured. Add a test asserting a thrown handler error results in a Sentry capture.

medium Device-token role is never re-derived per-request — admin demotion does not reach paired devices for up to the 180-day token TTL security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/services/reconcileDeviceToken.ts:40-76 unverified
Description

reconcileDeviceToken's own comments say it should recompute role so 'admin demotions land on the next request without re-pairing' (lines 40-47), but the implementation only touches last_seen_at and passes the role claim through unchanged (lines 69-76: 'role passthrough — could re-derive via roleFor'). Device tokens don't store a username, so roleFor is never called. Consequence: removing an admin from env.ADMINS demotes their COOKIE sessions on the next request (sessionGate.ts:124) but their PAIRED tvOS/iOS device keeps admin until the 180-day token TTL, re-pair, or explicit revoke. member-REVOKE cascades to device tokens (sessionGate.ts:137), but admin-DEMOTE does not — so a demoted-but-still-member admin retains admin on Bearer. Cookie and Bearer paths have divergent privilege semantics.

Evidence
reconcileDeviceToken.ts:69-76 returns `{ ...claims, device_name }` with comment 'role passthrough — could re-derive via roleFor but device tokens don't carry username'; cookie path sessionGate.ts:124 `const role = roleFor(session.username)` recomputes every request; cascade fires only on member-revoke/not-member, not on demote.
Recommendation

Read the authoritative role from the members row by sub (members.role) in reconcileDeviceToken (or store username at mint time and call roleFor), so device-token role matches the cookie path's per-request demotion.

medium Device-token role check accepts 'guest' then casts it to Role ('admin'|'user'), laundering an unmodeled third role into the session correctness Server — Auth & AuthZ server/session.ts:286, 315 unverified
Description

verifyDeviceToken accepts claims.role === 'guest' as valid (session.ts:286) then does `role: claims.role as Role` (session.ts:315) where Role is `'admin' | 'user'` (session.ts:55). The cookie path rejects anything but admin/user (session.ts:387), so this is an asymmetry: a device token minted with role 'guest' authenticates via Bearer and flows a value the type system believes impossible into c.var.session.role. Today every `role === 'admin'` check treats guest as non-admin (safe by accident), but any future exhaustive branch (`role === 'user' ? userPath : adminPath`) would misclassify a guest as admin. A latent privilege bug seeded by the unsafe cast.

Evidence
session.ts:55 `export type Role = 'admin' | 'user'`; session.ts:286 `if (claims.role !== 'admin' && claims.role !== 'user' && claims.role !== 'guest') return null`; session.ts:315 `role: claims.role as Role`.
Recommendation

Either add 'guest' to the Role union and handle it explicitly everywhere role is branched, or reject 'guest' in verifyDeviceToken to match the cookie path. Remove the `as Role` cast so the compiler forces the decision.

medium In-memory auth rate limiter and revocation/membership caches are per-process — ineffective behind multiple replicas and reset on every redeploy security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/auth.ts:75-178 unverified
Description

All auth rate limiting (PIN create/check, Apple login + invite-redeem) lives in a single in-process Map capped at 256 buckets (auth.ts:68,75). Behind >1 backend replica, or after any restart/redeploy, counters are not shared and reset to zero, so the per-client and global limits guarding invite-code brute-force are far weaker than they look. Under a distributed-IP flood the 256-cap evicts legitimate per-client buckets first (auth.ts:126-135), degrading toward only the global limit. The sessionGate membership cache (sessionGate.ts:60,66 MAX 1000) is likewise per-process. Fine for one NAS; the project targets App-Store scale.

Evidence
auth.ts:75 `const authRateLimitBuckets = new Map<...>()`; auth.ts:68 `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_MAX_BUCKETS = 256`; auth.ts:126-135 eviction skips only `:global` keys; sessionGate.ts:60,66 `MAX_CACHE_ENTRIES = 1000`.
Recommendation

Document the single-replica assumption now; before horizontal scaling move rate-limit + revocation/membership state to a shared store (the existing sqlite serverDb or Redis). Persist invite-redeem attempt counters so a restart can't reset brute-force protection.

medium Auth rate-limit client identity trusts cf-connecting-ip / true-client-ip headers with no trusted-proxy attestation security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/auth.ts:83-89 unverified
Description
