Per-client rate limiting keys off cf-connecting-ip / true-client-ip request headers. If the backend is ever reachable without the Cloudflare/edge proxy (direct NAS exposure, misconfigured nginx, internal hop), an attacker forges a fresh value per request and gets a new per-client bucket each time, defeating the per-client limit (only the global limit survives). Nothing verifies the request actually arrived via the trusted edge.

Evidence
auth.ts:83-89 trustedAuthClientIdentity returns `cf:${header('cf-connecting-ip')}` / `true-client:${...}` with no trusted-proxy CIDR check; returns null (global-only) if both absent.
Recommendation

Honor these headers only when the immediate peer is a known proxy (trusted-proxy allowlist), else fall back to the socket remote address. Document the weaker guarantee when neither is verifiable.

medium Onboarding Walkthrough overlay is an aria-modal div with no focus trap or ESC handling accessibility Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System src/components/walkthrough/Walkthrough.tsx:(overlay root) unverified
Description

Walkthrough.tsx (the first-run onboarding + sign-in surface, hosting the passkey/Apple buttons via walkthrough__signin-button / walkthrough__invite-input) renders a plain div with role=dialog aria-modal=true rather than a native <dialog>. It does not trap Tab focus inside the overlay, does not handle Escape to dismiss, and does not move focus into the dialog on open. Keyboard and screen-reader users can tab to background content behind an element claiming to be modal, and cannot ESC out — and this is the very first screen a new user sees.

Evidence
Walkthrough.tsx uses `<div role="dialog" aria-modal="true">` with navigation buttons but no keydown/Escape handler and no focus-trap or initial-focus management (contrast with ConfirmModal which uses native <dialog> showModal()).
Recommendation

Convert the overlay to a native <dialog> with showModal() (free focus trap + ESC) or implement focus trap, Escape-to-dismiss, initial focus into the dialog, and focus restore on close.

medium gemScene WebGL has no context-loss recovery and no responsive resize wiring in EmeraldMark production-readiness Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System src/lib/gemScene.ts:(GemScene constructor / dispose) unverified
Description

GemScene exposes a resize() method but EmeraldMark never calls it after mount (it sizes once from props and only re-runs the effect on width/height/variant prop changes), and neither GemScene nor EmeraldMark registers webglcontextlost/webglcontextrestored handlers. On mobile Safari / backgrounded-tab GPU context loss — a real condition on the App-Store-bound client — the brand mark canvas goes permanently black with no recovery, and the mark does not adapt to DPR/zoom changes. Dispose hygiene is otherwise good, but resilience is missing.

Evidence
gemScene.ts has `resize(width,height)` and `dispose()` but no `renderer.domElement.addEventListener('webglcontextlost'...)`; EmeraldMark.tsx effect sizes the scene once and never attaches a ResizeObserver or context-loss listener.
Recommendation

Add webglcontextlost (preventDefault + stop RAF) and webglcontextrestored (rebuild scene) handlers in GemScene, and attach a ResizeObserver in EmeraldMark to call scene.resize() on layout/DPR changes.

medium No per-request timeout on the ServeFile direct-play stream path production-readiness Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/routes.rs:622-627 unverified
Description

stream_file serves bytes via `ServeFile::new(&file.path).oneshot(req)` with no surrounding timeout or connection-level limit. The transcoder handoff path has a 5s reqwest timeout (routes.rs:31-32) and TMDB has 5s, but the actual large-file streaming has none. On a slow/stalled NFS-mounted library volume (the NAS deployment), a hung read on ServeFile holds the request and its tokio task open indefinitely. There is no concurrency cap on stream_file either, so at App-Store scale a burst of stalled reads against a degraded volume can exhaust the connection pool/tasks. Plex/Jellyfin bound this with read timeouts and per-server stream limits.

Evidence
routes.rs:622-627 `let service = ServeFile::new(&file.path); let resp = service.oneshot(req).await.map_err(...)?;` — no tower timeout layer, no TimeoutLayer on the router, no max-in-flight-streams semaphore. The router (router(), lines 38-62) applies only the principal_layer; no tower-http TimeoutLayer or ConcurrencyLimitLayer despite tower/tower-http being deps.
Recommendation

Add a tower timeout/idle-timeout around the stream service (or a TimeoutLayer scoped to non-stream routes plus a sane socket read timeout), and consider a per-instance stream concurrency limit. At minimum document the NFS-stall risk and set a read timeout on the underlying file handle.

medium ffprobe spawn has no execution timeout — a hostile/corrupt file can wedge a scan slot production-readiness Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/probe.rs:29-40 unverified
Description

ffprobe() runs `Command::new("ffprobe")...output().await` with no timeout. ffprobe can hang or run for a very long time on a malformed/truncated/0-byte file or a network-mounted file whose backing store stalls. Because the scanner holds the single AtomicBool scan guard for the entire pass and probes files serially, one hung ffprobe blocks the whole scheduler indefinitely and leaves scanning=true forever — no further scheduled or manual scan can ever run again (POST /scan returns 409 permanently). The module's own header comment claims '5s timeout per request' but that text describes the TMDB module, not ffprobe; ffprobe has none.

Evidence
probe.rs:30-40 the Command chain ends in `.output().await.map_err(|e| ProbeError::Spawn(...))?` with no tokio::time::timeout wrapper. scanner.rs:120 calls `probe::ffprobe(entry_path).await` serially inside the guarded pass. There is no watchdog that resets scanning=true if a pass hangs.
Recommendation

Wrap the ffprobe child in tokio::time::timeout (e.g. 30-60s), killing the child on elapse and counting it as report.errors. Add `-analyzeduration`/`-probesize` caps. Separately, consider a wall-clock guard on scan_once so a wedged pass eventually releases the scanning flag.

medium Scan is fully serial (no concurrency) — contradicts the <5s/100-file target and is slow at library scale performance Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/scanner.rs:51-160 unverified
Description

scan_once walks every root and probes/enriches every changed file strictly sequentially in one loop — one ffprobe at a time, then serial TMDB round-trips (search + external_ids, plus a per-episode call). The module header sets a success target of '100-file fixture library scans in < 5s', but there is no benchmark proving it and no parallelism: 100 cold ffprobe spawns alone, serialized, will likely blow that budget, and a real Plex-scale library (tens of thousands of episodes) doing serial 5s-timeout TMDB calls is a multi-hour cold scan. ROADMAP confirms 'no perf benchmark'. spawn_blocking is never used; ffprobe is async-spawned but awaited inline, so the event loop isn't parallelizing probes.

Evidence
scanner.rs:53 `for entry in WalkDir::new(...)` with the probe at line 120 and TMDB at 259/268/291 all awaited inline; no buffer_unordered, JoinSet, or semaphore-bounded concurrency anywhere. `grep spawn_blocking crates/media-core` → 0 hits. No criterion/bench harness in the crate.
Recommendation

Bound-parallelize the per-file probe+enrich with a tokio Semaphore (e.g. 4-8 concurrent ffprobe, 2-4 concurrent TMDB to respect rate limits) via futures::stream::buffer_unordered or a JoinSet, and add a benchmark that asserts the 100-file <5s criterion in CI.

medium VOD/series/live grant does not bind the session to the minted token, so concurrency is defeatable by re-minting security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/routes/iptv.ts:489,904,966 unverified
Description

tryAcquire registers a sessionId, but the stream token minted right after carries only {kind, rid, sub} — not the sessionId. The byte endpoints verify the token and replay-cache the jti but never check that a live concurrency slot exists for this play. A client at the cap can therefore: acquire is refused (429), but any PREVIOUSLY minted, still-valid token (5-min default TTL) continues to stream because the byte path never consults the concurrency tracker. Equally, since the token isn't tied to the session, the 'kick this session' button (DELETE /sessions/:id) frees the Map entry but does not stop an in-flight proxied stream using an already-issued token. Enforcement is advisory at grant time only.

Evidence
iptv.ts:489-491 mints {kind:'live', resourceId, sub} with no sessionId claim; byte handlers (712 checkToken, 920, 982) validate kind+rid+replay but never verify an active session slot; DELETE /sessions/:id (iptv.ts:149-159) only mutates the in-memory Map.
Recommendation

Embed sessionId in the stream token rid/claim and have each byte request validate (and heartbeat) the live session, rejecting if the session was released/kicked. This makes the kick button and the cap actually terminate playback.

medium Upstream provider credentials are placed unencrypted into client-issued segment tokens security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/routes/iptv.ts:686-690 unverified
Description

For HLS VOD/series, rewriteHlsPlaylist signs each absolute upstream segment URL into a 'segment' stream token whose rid is the full upstream URL — which for Xtream movie/series paths embeds username AND password in the path. The stream token body is canonical JSON that is signed but NOT encrypted (the contracts crate produces payload.signature, payload is base64/plain). A client can decode its own segment token's rid and read the provider subscription username/password in cleartext. The replay cache and HMAC protect integrity/replay, not confidentiality. The system's stated model ('credentials never exposed to client') does not hold for the HLS segment path.

Evidence
iptv.ts:686-689 sign closure sets resourceId:url where url came from rewriteManifest over an upstream that is `${creds.host}/movie/${user}/${password}/...` (iptv.ts:924); stream tokens are signed-not-encrypted (iptvStreamToken.ts canonicalBytes is plain JSON).
Recommendation

Do not put the resolvable credentialed URL in the token. Store an opaque server-side segment-id -> upstreamUrl map (short TTL) keyed by jti, or encrypt the rid using the repo's JWE/keyDerivation infra. At minimum strip creds from the rid and re-attach server-side at fetch time.

medium Provider passwords stored in plaintext at rest security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/services/xtream.ts:17-26 unverified
Description

Xtream credentials are read straight from env (XTREAM_PASSWORD) and embedded into upstream URLs; while env is acceptable, the credentialed upstream URLs and creds also flow into ffmpeg stderr (scrubbed) and into signed segment tokens (unscrubbed, see prior finding). There is no use of the repo's secrets/keyDerivation service for the IPTV subscription secret, so any log path or token leak exposes the paid line. The scrubXtreamCreds regex (iptvRemux.ts:6-19) is a denylist that only catches env-matching user/pass and a couple URL shapes — a provider that 30x-redirects to a CDN with creds in a query param the regex doesn't match would leak.

Evidence
xtream.ts:23-24 returns password from env verbatim; iptvRemux.ts:6-19 scrub is best-effort denylist; segment-token rid carries unscrubbed creds (iptv.ts:686).
Recommendation

Centralize the IPTV credential behind the existing secrets service; never serialize a credentialed URL into a client-reachable artifact; make scrubbing allowlist-based (only emit known-safe fields).

medium Tests assert almost nothing about the named concurrency/expiry/remux behaviors testing Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/services/iptvSync.test.ts:1 unverified
Description

Coverage exists but is shallow against the load-bearing behaviors. There is no integration test that boots the mounted app and exercises /grant -> byte proxy -> SSRF guard -> replay -> concurrency end to end, so the redirect-SSRF bypass and the HLS slot-leak are untested. The remux test mocks spawn/fs so no ffmpeg runs (the same 'tests point at a stub' pattern flagged for M4). Stream-token tests can't fake the crate's clock easily, so natural-expiry replay/expiry interplay is under-tested. The concurrency tracker has unit tests but nothing verifies the route-level lifecycle (heartbeat from byte handlers), which is exactly where the slot-leak bug lives.

Evidence
iptvRemux.test.ts mocks child_process/fs (no real transcode); no test file boots app.ts or hits the proxy handlers; concurrency route lifecycle (heartbeat-from-bytes) is exercised by no test.
Recommendation

Add a supertest/app-boot integration suite over the real router covering grant->proxy->replay->concurrency and a redirect-to-private-host SSRF case (should 400/refuse). Assert the ffmpeg arg array in the remux test. Use fake timers around the contracts verify to test expiry+replay together.

medium cloudflared pinned to :latest — the one public-internet-facing container auto-upgrades through breaking changes production-readiness Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy docker-compose.yml:260 unverified
Description

cloudflared uses image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest. This is the single container exposed to the public internet (it terminates the Cloudflare Tunnel and dials the loopback backend). The same file demonstrates the team knows better — glitchtip is pinned to 6.1 with a comment that 'latest silently auto-upgrades through breaking changes.' A docker compose pull (which deploys trigger) can swap the tunnel client for a behavior-changed version at the worst layer, and it makes deploys non-reproducible.

Evidence
docker-compose.yml:260 'image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest'; contrast 281-284 glitchtip pin-and-warn comment.
Recommendation

Pin cloudflared to a specific version tag (and ideally a digest) and bump deliberately.

medium No container hardening: missing cap_drop, no-new-privileges, read_only across all services security Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy docker-compose.yml:16-368 (all services) unverified
Description

No service declares security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true], cap_drop: [ALL], or read_only: true (verified by grep). Combined with three of four containers running as root, each retains the full default Linux capability set and can gain new privileges via setuid binaries. For a platform accepting authenticated multi-user traffic over a public tunnel and bound for the App Store, this is below baseline; the root + ffmpeg + mounted-media transcoder is the highest-value target.

Evidence
docker-compose.yml: no occurrence of 'security_opt', 'cap_drop', 'read_only', or 'no-new-privileges' in the 375-line file.
Recommendation

Add security_opt: ["no-new-privileges:true"] and cap_drop: ["ALL"] (re-adding only required caps) to every service; make the app containers read_only with explicit writable mounts for /app/data, /data, and /scratch.

medium Coverage is collected but no threshold is enforced — the 'coverage' gate cannot fail on low coverage testing Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy vitest.config.ts:38-49 unverified
Description

CI runs npm run test:coverage (vitest run --coverage), but the coverage block in vitest.config.ts defines only provider/reporter/include/exclude — no thresholds (lines/functions/branches/statements). Coverage is therefore merely reported, never enforced; a PR can drop coverage to near-zero and the gate still passes as long as the (possibly trivial) tests pass. The roadmap's M3/M4 'unit-green against a stub' concern is directly enabled by this.

Evidence
vitest.config.ts coverage block (38-49) has provider 'v8', reporter, include, exclude — no 'thresholds' key. ci.yml:45 'npm run test:coverage' with no override.
Recommendation

Add coverage.thresholds with a realistic floor so the coverage step fails on regression; ratchet upward over time.

medium Playwright 'E2E' runs against the Vite SPA only with all APIs mocked — zero client<->server integration coverage testing Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy playwright.config.ts:27-34 unverified
Description

webServer.command is 'npm run dev:vite' (Vite only, port 5173) and the config comment states explicitly that the full backend is intentionally not booted because every /api/* call is intercepted via page.route(). The CI step is named 'Playwright E2E' but tests only the SPA against a mock. Real regressions in CORS credentialed requests, the auth session/device-token flow, stream-token issuance, or the cross-origin VITE_API_BASE_URL wiring are not exercised by any automated test. For an App-Store-bound product there is no automated integration proof of the client/server contract.

Evidence
playwright.config.ts:27-34 'webServer: { command: "npm run dev:vite", url: "http://localhost:5173" ... }' and comment lines 4-7 'We start Vite alone (not the full npm run dev which also boots the Hono backend) because every /api/* call is intercepted via page.route()'.
Recommendation

Add a second Playwright project (or a compose-based CI job) that boots the real backend (npm start) against a seeded test DB and runs true integration specs for auth, CORS credentials, and stream-token issuance; keep the mocked suite as fast UI smoke but stop labeling it 'E2E'.

medium network_mode: host on cloudflared bypasses Docker network isolation and can reach every loopback admin port security Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy docker-compose.yml:263 unverified
Description

cloudflared runs with network_mode: host (justified in the header comment so it can dial 127.0.0.1:3001). Host networking gives it the NAS host's full network namespace, so it can reach ANY loopback-published service — recommender :8001, media-core :8002, transcoder :8003, glitchtip admin :8100 — plus any other homelab service on 127.0.0.1, not just the backend it needs. With cloudflared also pinned to :latest, a compromise of that one image turns a single-port tunnel into broad host-network access. The tunnel only needs to reach the backend.

Evidence
docker-compose.yml:263 'network_mode: host'; the same file binds recommender/media-core/transcoder/glitchtip admin ports to 127.0.0.1 (130, 186, 230, 296) — all reachable by host-mode cloudflared.
Recommendation

Run cloudflared on the default bridge (or a dedicated network with backend only) and target the backend by service name (tunnel url: http://backend:3001); avoid host networking so a tunnel compromise cannot pivot to other loopback admin ports.

medium deploy-nas.sh rsyncs server/, recommender/, and crates/ with --delete into appdata subdirs that sit alongside the live data volumes data-integrity Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy scripts/deploy-nas.sh:142-177 unverified
Description

Three rsync blocks use --delete into ${APPDATA}/server, /recommender, and /crates. The compose bind-mount data volumes (/mnt/user/appdata/exchange-backend/data, /recommender-db, /media-core-db) are siblings under the same APPDATA root. The crates/ rsync (169-177) and the manifest rsync (183) and the .env scp (186) all write directly into APPDATA. The recommender rsync excludes 'data' (line 152) but the safety of the live DB volumes ultimately depends on no source tree ever producing a path that collides with data/recommender-db/media-core-db under --delete. The crates rsync's --delete with broad excludes is the riskiest: if any excluded-but-present dir ever stops matching, --delete removes destination content. This is a latent footgun where deploy artifacts and persistent SQLite/grab-log data share a root governed by --delete.

Evidence
scripts/deploy-nas.sh:142-145 'rsync -av --delete ... server/ ...:${APPDATA}/server/', 148-156 recommender with '--exclude data', 169-177 crates with '--delete'; compose volume '/mnt/user/appdata/exchange-backend/data:/app/data' (27) and '/recommender-db:/data' (135) are children of APPDATA.
Recommendation

Deploy the code tree to a dedicated subdirectory (e.g. ${APPDATA}/app) fully separate from the data volumes, or add explicit --exclude for data/recommender-db/media-core-db/media-test to every --delete rsync; consider --backup as a safety net.

medium Suggestion validation fans out many TMDB lookups per request — latency and rate-limit degradation the team has already observed performance Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/routes/suggestions.ts:2363-2366, 805-820 unverified
Description

Non-pool Claude picks are each resolved against TMDB /search via tmdbLookup, and legacy bare-id rows are backfilled via resolveTitles. There is single-flight coalescing, short result caches, and a pool fast-path that avoids the lookup for pool hits — all good — but the per-request caches are in-process and do NOT help concurrent DISTINCT users, and the code itself documents the failure mode: at the TMDB free-tier ~40 req/10s limit, 'call 2 (cache cold for lookups) gets 429-throttled and returns 1-4 items instead of 20.' At App-Store scale with many households the same popular title is re-looked-up per user and TMDB rate limits will routinely produce short strips. There is no persistent cross-user TMDB metadata cache.

Evidence
suggestions.ts:2364-2366 `Promise.all(survivors.map(({pick}) => tmdbLookup(type, pick.title, pick.year)...))`; suggestions.ts:654 comment 'TMDB rate limit is ~40 req / 10s ... call 2 ... gets 429-throttled and returns 1-4 items instead of 20.'
Recommendation

Add a persistent (SQLite or shared LRU with TTL) TMDB detail cache keyed by (kind,tmdbId)/(kind,title,year) shared across users, plus a server-side TMDB request budgeter. The local-recommender fast path largely avoids this, but the BYO-Claude path is exposed.

medium TMDB credits proxy does not validate tmdbId/tvdbId before spending an upstream call security Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/routes/tmdb.ts:46-77 unverified
Description

tmdb.get('/credits') reads tvdbId/tmdbId from the query string and interpolates them into the upstream path via encodeURIComponent (which prevents path traversal) but with NO numeric validation, unlike the strict Number.isSafeInteger guards used in feedback.ts and recommenderEvents.ts and the enum guard on /trending/:type. A client can send arbitrary/garbage ids that each turn into an outbound TMDB call, an amplification toward api.themoviedb.org that burns the server's shared TMDB rate budget on nonsense and adds an SSRF-adjacent unauthenticated-fan-out surface.

Evidence
tmdb.ts:47-48 reads raw query; :53 `tmdbFetch(/find/${encodeURIComponent(tvdbId)}...)` and :71 `/movie/${encodeURIComponent(tmdbId)}/credits` with no Number.isSafeInteger/positive-int check.
Recommendation

Validate tmdbId/tvdbId as positive safe integers and return 400 otherwise, mirroring the validation already standard elsewhere in this subsystem.

medium Internal-principal HMAC comparison in the Rust verifier should use a constant-time compare security Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge crates/emerald-contracts/src/internal_principal.rs:~44 (verification path) unverified
Description

The Python receiver uses constant-time hmac.compare_digest. The Rust verifier (used by media-core and the napi verify path that backs the server->media-core proxy auth minted in routes/media.ts) should likewise use the hmac crate's constant-time verify_slice rather than a PartialEq byte comparison of the provided vs expected signature. routes/media.ts mints one of these tokens for every media-core request, so the verifying side is on the hot internal-auth path. Severity is medium because exploitation requires network position and the secret is shared internally; note I verified the verifier returns Ok(claims) on success and Err on mismatch (via grep) but could not re-read the exact comparison line at final pass, so the team should confirm whether verify_slice is already used before actioning.

Evidence
Earlier grep of internal_principal.rs showed a signature-mismatch return (`return Err(\"signature mismatch\")`) on a direct slice comparison rather than mac.verify_slice; contrast recommender/app/internal_principal.py which uses hmac.compare_digest. Final re-read was blocked by an unresponsive shell, so confirm the exact operator.
Recommendation

Ensure verification uses mac.verify_slice(&provided_sig) (constant-time). Additionally validate the `service` claim against an expected allowlist per endpoint so a token minted for one service cannot be replayed against another.

medium @runwayml/sdk is a declared but completely unused dependency dependency Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/package.json:48 unverified
Description

@runwayml/sdk ^3.21.0 (a commercial video-generation SDK) is declared in devDependencies but is imported by ZERO source files — a repo-wide search for 'runwayml' returns matches ONLY in package.json and package-lock.json, never in src/**, server/**, or any .ts/.tsx/.mjs. It is dead weight in the dependency tree: every declared dep is install surface, audit noise, and a potential supply-chain vector, and an LLM/generative-media SDK that can make outbound network calls is exactly the kind of dependency you do not want sitting unused in a product bound for App-Store review.

Evidence
git grep for 'runwayml' across the repo returns only: 'package-lock.json' and 'package.json'. No importer exists in any source tree.
Recommendation

Remove @runwayml/sdk from package.json and regenerate the lockfile. If it was added for an experiment, keep that on a feature branch, not on the integration branch bound for release.

medium internal-principal time check has no nbf/iat bounds, uses strict-greater expiry, and has no req_id replay guard security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/internal_principal.rs:77-85 unverified
Description

Internal principals cross the trust boundary from Hono to the Rust/Python services and assert the requestor's identity/role so those services skip their own auth. enforce_time_window only checks `now > exp` (strict >), with no nbf, no iat-floor (forward clock-skew), and no req_id replay cache. A captured internal-principal JWE can be replayed against media-core/transcoder/recommender for the full 60s window (and is accepted at exactly now==exp due to the strict >). The doc comment claims the principal 'must be fresh' but nothing enforces single-use.

Evidence
internal_principal.rs:81 `if now > claims.exp { return Err(InternalPrincipalError::Expired); }` is the entire time check — no nbf, no iat lower-bound, no req_id dedup. Comment at internal_principal.rs:75-76 asserts single-in-flight freshness that the code does not enforce.
Recommendation

Add a req_id replay guard (single-use per req_id within the TTL) and/or bind the principal to the specific request (method+path+body hash), reject iat too far in the future, and use `>=` against exp.

medium Internal Rust error detail is leaked verbatim across the FFI boundary, including the attacker-supplied kid security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs:107-220 unverified
Description

Every napi/pyo3 verify/decrypt error formats the internal Rust error enum directly into the message returned to the caller via format!("{:?}", e). This distinguishes BadSignature vs BadPayload vs Expired vs UnknownKid(kid) to the caller — including echoing the attacker-supplied kid back in the UnknownKid message. Surfacing whether a token failed on signature vs structure vs expiry is a classic oracle aiding forgery/enumeration, and echoing an attacker-controlled kid into a returned message (which frequently lands in logs) invites log injection/noise.

Evidence
napi src/lib.rs:108 `.map_err(|e| Error::from_reason(format!("verify failed: {:?}", e)))`; napi src/lib.rs:220 device decrypt `.map_err(|e| Error::from_reason(format!("{:?}", e)))` renders `UnknownKid("<attacker kid>")`; pyo3 src/lib.rs:124 `PyKeyError::new_err(format!("unknown kid: {}", kid))`.
Recommendation

Return a coarse, stable error code at the public FFI boundary (a single 'token invalid' for all signature/structure/expiry failures) while keeping the detailed enum for internal structured logging only. Never echo the attacker-supplied kid into a returned message.

medium Telemetry scrub replacement sentinel diverges from the vector ("[Filtered]" vs "REDACTED") and is entirely untested correctness Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/telemetry.rs:82-125 unverified
Description

telemetry-pii-scrub.json specifies the expected replacement as "REDACTED" (plexAuthToken -> "REDACTED", url ...t=REDACTED..., authorization -> "REDACTED", and a JWE_CIPHERTEXT case yielding "REDACTED."), but telemetry.rs emits "[Filtered]" for denied keys and "[Filtered:token]"/"[Filtered:bearer]"/"[Filtered:cookie]" for value-regex hits. Because vectors.rs never loads telemetry-pii-scrub.json, this divergence is completely UNTESTED. Either the vector is dead (authored against a different TS/Sentry-style impl) or there is a genuine cross-language scrub-output divergence that would silently break any consumer asserting on the redaction sentinel. Both are problems for a 'single source of truth' crate the contract relies on for MANDATORY Glitchtip PII safety (§15).

Evidence
telemetry.rs:87 `*v = Value::String("[Filtered]".to_string());`; telemetry.rs:115-123 replace_all with "[Filtered:token]"/"[Filtered:bearer]"/"[Filtered:cookie]". telemetry-pii-scrub.json `expected` blocks use "REDACTED" throughout. No load_vector("telemetry-pii-scrub.json") in tests/vectors.rs.
Recommendation

Reconcile the replacement sentinel to a single contract-fixed value across Rust/TS/Python, add a Rust loader for telemetry-pii-scrub.json, and assert exact scrubbed output so the constant cannot drift again.

medium Telemetry token-redaction value regexes are narrow (eyJ prefix + only literal ?t=/&t= param) security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/telemetry.rs:53-71 unverified
Description

The PII value-regex for compact tokens requires the JWT-style `eyJ` prefix, and the stream-grant URL branch matches only the exact query param named `t` (`?t=`/`&t=`). A raw HMAC stream token captured mid-string without the `?t=` prefix, or a token under a differently-named param (`?token=`, `?grant=`, or path-embedded), is not redacted by the value regexes — only the key-match path (matches_pii_key) would catch it, and only when the secret sits under a denied key. Given that the contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15), the scrubber is the last line of defense and its freeform coverage is assumption-bound. (matches_pii_key itself is solid: substring, case-insensitive.)

Evidence
telemetry.rs:58-66 token_regex matches `(?:\?t=|&t=)[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+` (only param literally named t) OR `(?:eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.){2,4}[A-Za-z0-9_-]+` (only eyJ-prefixed). matches_pii_key (telemetry.rs:107-110) lowercases the key and checks `contains` against PII_KEYS.
Recommendation

Broaden the URL token regex to any `[?&][a-z_]*t(oken)?=`/`grant=` param and to path-embedded grant tokens; add a generic two-segment HMAC-token pattern; add scrub vectors covering a bare stream token and a token under `?grant=` and assert redaction across all bindings.

medium PyO3 binding omits stream_token and device_token surfaces yet its doc comment claims it mirrors the N-API binding maintainability Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts-pyo3/src/lib.rs:9-218 unverified
Description

The PyO3 module exports HKDF, parse_sub, PII scrub, and internal_principal (encrypt/decrypt/enforce). It does NOT export stream-token sign/verify or device-token encrypt/decrypt — those exist only in the N-API binding. The module doc comment claims the surface 'mirrors crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs so Hono + recommender call byte-identical code paths', which is false: the two binding surfaces are materially different. If the recommender ever needs to validate a stream or device token it must re-implement it in Python — precisely the cross-language drift this crate exists to prevent. This may be intentional scoping, but the comment overstates parity and the asymmetry is a latent trap.

Evidence
pyo3 src/lib.rs:9 comment 'Surface mirrors crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs so Hono + recommender call byte-identical code paths.' The #[pymodule] (pyo3 src/lib.rs:206-218) registers only hkdf_*, parse_sub, pii_scrub_*, internal_principal_* — no stream_token_* or device_token_*. The N-API binding exports stream_token_sign/verify/verify_dual_key and device_token_encrypt/decrypt.
Recommendation

Either add the missing surfaces to PyO3 or correct the doc comment to state the deliberate scope difference, and maintain a single table of which contract functions each binding must expose so drift is visible.

medium Git merge-conflict markers were committed to stream_token.rs on the integration branch earlier in the audit (since resolved); no CI gate prevents recurrence production-readiness Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/stream_token.rs:176-194 unverified
Description

At the start of this audit the committed HEAD (44ff715) of stream_token.rs contained raw unresolved git conflict markers inside enforce_time_window, which rustc rejects — making the canonical security crate and every downstream (Hono via N-API, recommender via PyO3, media-core, transcoder) fail to build. A concurrent session resolved it during the audit (HEAD advanced to 1e34db02; markers gone, crate builds RC=0, MAX_TTL_SECS=91d / BadTtl correctly defined and unit-tested). The residual finding is process, not current code: a security-foundation crate carried conflict markers on a shared integration branch, and there is no evidence of a CI gate that would have caught it. Reported MEDIUM because the current tree is clean, but the QA-gate gap remains real given multiple sessions commit concurrently.

Evidence
Early-session: `git grep -nE '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)'` matched stream_token.rs:176,177,194; `git show HEAD:...` (HEAD=44ff715) showed `<<<<<<< HEAD`/`=======`/`>>>>>>> c640b04 (fix(stream-token): scope BadTtl forged-window check ...)`; first `cargo build` failed `error: encountered diff marker --> stream_token.rs:176:1`. Later: HEAD=1e34db02, marker count 0 in worktree and HEAD blob, build RC=0.
Recommendation

Add a required pre-merge CI gate running `cargo build --workspace` AND `git grep -nE '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)'` (fail on any match) so conflict markers can never reach a shared branch again.

medium GET clients do `res.json() as T` with no empty-body or parse-error guard — a 204/non-JSON 2xx throws an opaque, unnormalized error correctness Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/api/media.ts:12-21 unverified
Description

Every GET helper returns `res.json() as Promise<T>` gating only on !res.ok. A 200/204 with an empty or non-JSON body reaches res.json() and throws a raw SyntaxError that is NOT an ApiError and bypasses throwApiError's normalization, so hooks surface an opaque error (and with no error boundary, potentially a white screen if it reaches render). The iptv POST helper handles this correctly (`const text = await res.text(); return text ? JSON.parse(text) : undefined`) but the GET helpers across media/sab/sonarr/radarr/tmdb/grabs do not. There is also zero runtime schema validation (no zod) on any response, so a backend shape change surfaces as a render-time crash rather than a handled error.

Evidence
media.ts:20 `return res.json() as Promise<T>`; iptv.ts:22, sab.ts:10, sonarr.ts:11, radarr.ts:11, grabs.ts:37, tmdb.ts:44 same pattern; contrast iptv.ts:37-38 POST which DOES guard empty bodies.
Recommendation

Centralize reads through one apiFetch that tolerates empty/204 bodies and wraps JSON.parse failures as ApiError. For high-value responses (auth, library, suggestions) validate shape with zod so drift fails loudly and safely.

medium useCast is a TMDB cast-list hook, not a Chromecast controller — casting to a TV does not exist in this layer feature-gap Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/hooks/useCast.ts:1-25 unverified
Description

Despite the name, useCast is a TanStack Query hook for TMDB cast/crew (useCast(args) -> fetchCast). The 'cast to TV' capability a Plex replacement needs is absent from the entire reviewed shell/hooks layer: no Cast Application Framework integration (no SDK load, no requestSession, no media load) and no AirPlay path for the Apple targets. For a 'full Plex replacement' bound for Apple TV/iOS, casting from the web client is a baseline expectation.

Evidence
useCast.ts body is entirely `export function useCast(args: Args) { ... fetchCast(...) }` returning CastMember[]; no window.cast / chrome.cast reference anywhere in reviewed files.
Recommendation

If casting is in scope, implement a real Cast/AirPlay hook separately; if not, ensure no UI presents a dead cast control. Rename useCast to useTmdbCast to remove the misleading name.

medium Hash router has no guard layer — route protection is hand-rolled in the shell and duplicated maintainability Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/App.tsx:66-76 unverified
Description

lib/router.ts is a minimal hash store with no concept of guarded routes. Protection is hand-rolled in Shell: a useEffect that navigate('home')s for users/live/media when not permitted, PLUS a parallel `blocked` boolean and `effectiveRoute` fallback to avoid a flash. It works but is fragile — guard logic is duplicated (effect + blocked computation), every new gated route must update BOTH, and the gating contract lives in component code rather than the router. (Server-side authZ is correctly the source of truth via requireAdmin etc., so this is a maintainability risk, not an authZ bypass.)

Evidence
App.tsx:66-70 effect `if (route === 'users' && !isAdmin) navigate('home')...` duplicated by :71-74 `const blocked = (route === 'users' && !isAdmin) || ...` and :75 `const effectiveRoute = blocked ? 'home' : route`.
Recommendation

Centralize route metadata (requiredRole, requiredFlag) and derive both the redirect and the render fallback from one table so a new gated route can't be half-protected.

medium /api/health is a static literal and compose has zero healthchecks — no readiness gating before the Cloudflare tunnel routes traffic production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/app.ts:68; docker-compose.yml 267-268, 317-319, 365-367 unverified
Description

`app.get('/api/health', (c) => c.json({ ok: true }))` always returns 200 regardless of DB-open state, migration success, or sidecar reachability — pure liveness, no readiness. (Contrast the Python recommender's /health, which actually queries the DB.) docker-compose declares NO healthcheck: block on any service and uses bare depends_on (cloudflared depends_on backend 267-268; glitchtip depends_on db/redis 317-319), which waits only for container START, not health. The Cloudflare tunnel can therefore route to a backend that booted but whose migration failed, and Glitchtip web can start before Postgres finished migrating.

Evidence
app.ts 68 static health. docker-compose grep: zero `healthcheck:` keys; depends_on appears only as start-ordering at 267-268, 317-319, 365-367.
Recommendation

Add /readyz that runs SELECT 1 on each open SQLite handle, confirms migrations at head, and optionally pings sidecars; keep /api/health as liveness. Add healthcheck: blocks and convert depends_on to `condition: service_healthy`.

medium Heavy reliance on unstructured console.* logging — ~150+ sites, no levels/correlation IDs/JSON; becomes the de-facto only signal given the Sentry gaps production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/services/upstream.ts:52, 84 (representative; ~150 sites repo-wide) unverified
Description

Logging is unstructured console.error/log/warn throughout (per-file counts: suggestions.ts 25, migrator.ts 10, sonarr.ts 10, radarr.ts 10, iptvScheduler.ts 6, plex.ts 4, and dozens more). No JSON logger, no levels, no request-correlation id; Hono's default logger() emits human text. Combined with Sentry not capturing handler errors and 3 runtimes having no telemetry at all, console output is effectively the only failure signal for large error classes — with no aggregation or alerting at App-Store scale.

Evidence
upstream.ts 52 `console.error(`[upstream] ${label} ${reason}: ${message}`)`; 84 same. Per-file non-test console counts: suggestions.ts 25, migrator.ts 10, sonarr.ts 10, radarr.ts 10, iptvScheduler.ts 6, etc.
Recommendation

Introduce a thin structured logger (level + JSON + optional request id) and route console sites through it; feed Sentry breadcrumbs from it so error context survives. Keep the PII-scrubbing posture in the logger.

medium DESIGN.md flatly forbids WebGL / Three.js, contradicting the shipped WebGL brand mark - the approved exception is NOT documented docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty DESIGN.md:528,748-749 unverified
Description

DESIGN.md refuses WebGL twice in absolute terms and never records the user-approved exception. The product DOES use Three.js: package.json:42 '"three": "^0.184.0"', and src/lib/gemScene.ts + src/components/atmosphere/EmeraldMark.tsx import three (confirmed via grep). Per project memory ('Brand mark uses WebGL (DESIGN.md override) - user-approved exception'), this WebGL is sanctioned - but because DESIGN.md is the locked design contract and does not record the exception, a new engineer could delete the brand mark to 'fix' the violation, or flag the atmosphere components as off-contract in review.

Evidence
DESIGN.md:528 'What we don't take: WebGL hero scenes ...'; DESIGN.md:748-749 'WebGL hero scenes. The constellation overlay is a static SVG ... Three.js stays out.' vs package.json:42 '"three": "^0.184.0"'; grep: src/lib/gemScene.ts + src/components/atmosphere/EmeraldMark.tsx import three. No exception clause anywhere in DESIGN.md.
Recommendation

Add an explicit exception clause to DESIGN.md: 'EXCEPTION (user-approved): the 3-emerald brand mark uses live Three.js/WebGL; the no-WebGL rule applies to hero scenes and decorative overlays only.' Reference the approval so it is not re-litigated.

medium Stream-byte proxies (live .ts, catchup, vod/series progressive) call raw fetch() with NO SSRF guard, relying on an unasserted 'host is operator creds' invariant security Cross-Cutting: Security server/routes/iptv.ts:653,720,859 unverified
Description

Unlike the segment/sub-playlist paths, the live (.ts at iptv.ts:720), catchup (iptv.ts:859), and VOD/series progressive (proxyRangeable at iptv.ts:646-668) proxies build upstreamUrl from credsFromEnv() and fetch directly with no isPublicHttpsUpstream check. credsFromEnv() (xtream.ts:17-26) only strips trailing slashes — it never validates XTREAM_HOST is https or public. The safety rests entirely on an invariant asserted nowhere; the catchup URL is plain interpolation of streamId/start/duration into timeshift.php (iptv.ts:853-855), and the client-supplied Range header is forwarded verbatim to the upstream (iptv.ts:651,1034).

Evidence
iptv.ts:720 `const upstream = await fetch(upstreamUrl, { signal..., headers: { 'User-Agent': 'IPTVSmarters' } })` (no guard). iptv.ts:653 proxyRangeable raw fetch (no guard, forwards client Range). xtream.ts:22 `host: env.XTREAM_HOST.replace(/\/+$/, '')` (no scheme/public validation).
Recommendation

Validate XTREAM_HOST at boot (require https + public host) and run every stream-byte upstream through isPublicHttpsUpstream (with the IP-pinning fix) even when built from operator creds. Validate/cap the forwarded Range header to a sane byte-range form.

medium STREAM_TOKEN verifier accepts SESSION_SECRET as a fallback signing key for up to 90 days, coupling two deliberately-separated key classes security Cross-Cutting: Security server/routes/iptv.ts + server/env.ts:iptv.ts:288,571,798,997; env.ts:215-221 unverified
Description

Every stream-token verification calls verifyStreamTokenDualKey(env.streamTokenSecret, env.sessionSecret, t) — SESSION_SECRET is an accepted fallback signing key for IPTV stream tokens. This re-introduces exactly the coupling that separate STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET + assertSecretsDistinct exist to prevent: a SESSION_SECRET compromise also forges stream tokens and grants stream access. env.ts:215-221 frames it as a time-bounded D2a migration shim, but playlist tokens have a 90-day TTL (iptv.ts:256), so the cross-key acceptance is live for up to a quarter year, not the 300s of short-lived tokens. The crate computes both HMACs unconditionally (no timing leak), but the fallback itself is the risk.

Evidence
iptv.ts:571 `const claims = verifyStreamTokenDualKey(env.streamTokenSecret, env.sessionSecret, t)`. env.ts:218-221 'verifier tries it first and falls back to SESSION_SECRET'. Playlist TTL iptv.ts:256 `const ttl = 90 * 24 * 3600`.
Recommendation

Bound the SESSION_SECRET fallback by token iat < deploy-time and auto-disable after the max relevant TTL elapses past deploy. For 300s tokens it should be dead within minutes; for 90-day playlist tokens, mint replacements and drop the fallback rather than accepting SESSION_SECRET-signed stream tokens for 90 days. Track and verify a hard removal date (mirror the index.ts:61-65 deadline-guard pattern already used for the session legacy key).

medium No application-layer rate limiting on unauthenticated stream-byte, playlist, and auth surfaces (brute-force + resource-exhaustion DoS) production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Security server/app.ts + server/routes/iptv.ts:app.ts:36-66; iptv.ts:284,708,917,979,993 unverified
Description

The global middleware chain is logger -> cors -> requireSafeOrigin only (app.ts:36-66); no limiter mounted. Token-in-URL stream-byte endpoints are intentionally cookie-auth-free (iptv.ts:282-283,701-707) and each proxies an upstream byte stream or spawns a full ffmpeg remux process (iptv.ts:760 -> iptvRemux.ts:134). Per-session concurrency is tracked but there is no server-wide ffmpeg ceiling and no per-IP rate limit, so high-volume hammering of grant+stream/remux can drive unbounded concurrent upstream fetches and ffmpeg processes. The device-PIN poll, passkey, and Plex PIN auth surfaces (app.ts:104-111) are likewise unthrottled against brute force.

Evidence
app.ts:36-66 — the complete set of global app.use() calls, no rate-limit middleware. iptv.ts:284 `iptv.get('/playlist.m3u', (c) => {...})` (no requireAuth, no rate limit). iptv.ts:760 startRemuxSession per manifest request -> iptvRemux.ts:134 `spawn('ffmpeg', args, ...)`.
Recommendation

Add IP/identity-scoped rate limiting to the unauthenticated stream/playlist/auth surfaces, a global request ceiling, and a hard server-wide cap on concurrent remux/transcode ffmpeg processes with queue/reject above it. (The transcoder crate has a concurrency.rs; ensure the server's iptvRemux path has an equivalent global cap.)

medium Internal-principal enforcement defaults to 'off' on both media-core and recommender — caller identity is unverified until an env flip security Cross-Cutting: Security crates/media-core/src/auth.rs + recommender/app/internal_principal.py:auth.rs:38-41; internal_principal.py:8-21,137-139 unverified
Description

Both internal services default the principal mode to off/skip: media-core's principal_layer returns next.run(req) immediately when mode==Off (auth.rs:39-41), and the recommender dependency returns None without inspecting the header in 'off' mode (internal_principal.py:138-139). In off/log mode the receiver TRUSTS whatever acting sub it is told (or proxies anonymously) without cryptographically verifying the 60s JWE — and in those modes acting_sub() even honors a client-supplied ?sub= (routes.rs:648-671 only rejects ?sub= OUTSIDE Off mode). media-core's /api/media/* is only reachable via the Hono proxy which fails closed if minting fails (routes/media.ts:43-53), so this is defense-in-depth today, but shipping the receiver off-by-default means a misrouted or directly-reachable media-core/recommender container performs per-user actions with unauthenticated, caller-asserted identity. Latent IDOR/per-user-isolation gap that becomes live the moment network topology assumptions break.

Evidence
media-core auth.rs:39-41 `if mode == PrincipalMode::Off { return Ok(next.run(req).await); }`. recommender internal_principal.py:138-139 `if mode == 'off': return None` ('off (default): ignore the header entirely'). routes.rs:648-655 acting_sub honors ?sub= only in Off mode.
Recommendation

Default to enforce in production (or hard-require the secret + enforce when NODE_ENV/equivalent is production), and ensure media-core/recommender are never directly network-reachable (compose internal network only, no published ports — verify in docker-compose.yml). Make the off->enforce cutover a required pre-prod gate, not optional (the m3-internal-principal-rollout doc exists; enforce it in CI/boot).

medium Recommender CI runs only 3 hand-picked pytest files — optimizer promotion, db migrator, and eval harness are not gated testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor .github/workflows/ci.yml:183-185 unverified
Description

The recommender job's pytest step is `pytest tests/test_emerald_contracts_parity.py tests/test_sub_validation.py tests/test_internal_principal.py` — three files, explicitly enumerated. The repo also has recommender/tests/test_db_migrator.py (17 tests) and recommender/eval/test_build_holdout.py, plus the optimizer worker (recommender/workers/optimizer.py) and the recipe modules (baseline_cosine, cold_start_trending, mmr_diverse) — NONE of which run in CI. So the recommender's actual recommendation logic, its DB migrations, and the optimizer-promotion path (which decides which recipe wins) are not gated by any green check. A migration that corrupts the recommender DB or an optimizer that promotes a broken recipe would pass CI.

Evidence
ci.yml:185 `run: pytest tests/test_emerald_contracts_parity.py tests/test_sub_validation.py tests/test_internal_principal.py -v`. recommender/tests/test_db_migrator.py and recommender/eval/test_build_holdout.py exist but are referenced nowhere in ci.yml; workers/optimizer.py has no test.
Recommendation

Change the step to `pytest recommender/tests/` (collect the whole directory) and add the eval harness as a separate reported job. Explicitly add an optimizer-promotion test (given candidate recipes with known offline metrics, assert the correct one is promoted) since that is the recommender's core decision and is currently untested.

medium No test exercises missing-secret / misconfigured-boot failure paths — config injects every secret so the fail-closed posture is unverified at suite level production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor vitest.config.ts:23-37 unverified
Description

vitest.config.ts injects all required secrets globally for every test. Convenient, but it means the import-time env validator and the §4 enforce-mode posture (transcoder/routes.rs:73 requires INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET when principal_mode=enforce) are never tested in their FAILURE direction at the suite level. There is a server/env.test.ts, but no test that boots with a missing STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET / DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET and asserts the process refuses to start. For an invite-only platform whose security model rests on these secrets being present and correct, the 'we fail closed if misconfigured' guarantee for the token secrets is asserted by humans, not by tests.

Evidence
vitest.config.ts:23-37 sets PLEX_CLIENT_ID, SESSION_SECRET, SONARR/RADARR/SAB keys, STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET, DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET, INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET for all tests; no test boots env-less to assert a hard failure on the token secrets.
Recommendation

Extend env.test.ts (or a separate empty-env vitest project) to assert the validator throws on each missing critical secret, and that enforce-mode rejects when INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET is absent. Converts the fail-closed promise from comment to test.

medium @sentry/node (Glitchtip, contract §15 MANDATORY) — wiring is test-unverified; no Sentry init / beforeSend-scrub test exists production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor package.json:28 unverified
Description

Telemetry via Glitchtip is contractually MANDATORY (§15) and @sentry/node is a declared runtime dependency. The testing-lens concern: there is NO test asserting Sentry/Glitchtip is initialized at boot or that thrown errors are captured and PII-scrubbed before send — despite the project shipping a dedicated telemetryPiiScrub service WITH 19 unit tests. So the scrubber is tested in isolation, but nothing proves it sits on the path between a thrown error and the Glitchtip DSN. A regression that disconnects the scrubber from Sentry's beforeSend hook, or that skips Sentry.init entirely, would not fail any test, and CI has no telemetry smoke check. (I flag this as declared-but-test-unverified; I did not complete the init-site grep due to a tool-output failure late in the session, so I am not asserting it is unwired.)

Evidence
package.json:28 '@sentry/node': '^9.47.1'; server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.test.ts has 19 tests for the scrub logic, but no test asserts Sentry.init is called or that beforeSend runs the scrubber; ci.yml has no telemetry step.
Recommendation

Add a boot test asserting Sentry/Glitchtip initializes when the DSN env is present and that a captured exception passes through the PII scrubber (mock the transport, throw, assert the outgoing event is scrubbed). Closes the gap between 'scrubber tested' and 'scrubber wired to telemetry'.

medium Coverage is collected but no threshold is enforced and the SPA is excluded — the coverage gate is decorative testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor vitest.config.ts:38-49 unverified
Description

CI runs npm run test:coverage and uploads an HTML report (ci.yml:44-45, 68-74), but there is NO thresholds block (lines/branches/functions) in the coverage config, so coverage can drop to any level and CI stays green. coverage.include is scoped to server/**/*.ts + only src/lib/api/errors.ts, so the entire SPA — 97 non-test src modules, 27 hooks, the player — is not measured. The coverage step produces an artifact nobody is forced to look at and cannot fail the build.

Evidence
vitest.config.ts:38-49 coverage block has provider/reporter/include/exclude but no `thresholds`. coverage.include = ['server/**/*.ts', 'src/lib/api/errors.ts'] — the SPA is effectively uncovered.
Recommendation

Add coverage thresholds (ratchet from current measured numbers so they can only rise). Either expand coverage.include to the SPA, or document that the SPA is covered by Playwright — noting that per the E2E finding it largely is not against the real backend.

medium SPA test coverage is thin and skips the entire hooks layer and player error-recovery — the client data plane is largely untested testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor src/components/player/IptvPlayer.test.tsx:1-30 unverified
Description

Only 11 SPA test files exist for 97 non-test src TS/TSX modules. All 27 data hooks (useIptvLive/useIptvVod/useIptvSeries/useIptvEpg/useMediaLibrary/useDownloadQueue/useCast/useSonarr*/useRadarrLibrary/useSuggested/useTrending, etc.) — which own loading/error/retry/cache state via TanStack Query — have ZERO dedicated unit tests (the only hook test is usePlexLinks). IptvPlayer.test.tsx has just 2 cases and both only `renderToStaticMarkup` and assert the HTML `toContain('<video')` — it never mounts the player or fires an hls.js/mpegts.js event. So HLS/mpegts failure-and-recovery (segment 404, token expiry mid-play, source switch) — the Netflix-grade resilience the product promises — is wholly unasserted.

Evidence
11 SPA test files vs 97 src modules; only src/lib/hooks/usePlexLinks.test.ts among 27 hooks; IptvPlayer.test.tsx uses renderToStaticMarkup and asserts html.toContain('<video') with no event simulation (2 it() blocks).
Recommendation

Add hook tests (renderHook + a mocked QueryClient) for the critical data hooks asserting loading/error/retry transitions, and replace the static-markup player test with a jsdom mount that drives hls.js/mpegts.js error events and asserts the recovery/teardown path. These are the flows users hit on bad networks — the differentiator vs a demo.

low DESTRUCTIVE-migration auto-backup copies the full DB beside the live file with no free-space precheck or .bak retention production-readiness Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/app/db.py:186-256 (_auto_backup / _check_backup_gate) unverified
Description

_check_backup_gate's primary path calls _auto_backup, which sqlite3.backup()s the entire DB to db_path.with_name(...bak) on every DESTRUCTIVE migration (e.g. 0007_drop_iptv_kinds.sql). There is no os.statvfs free-space precheck and no cleanup of prior .bak files — repeated destructive migrations accumulate full-size copies on the same NAS /data volume, and a backup that fills the disk could itself wedge the DB it is trying to protect.

Evidence
db.py _auto_backup: 'src.backup(dst)' to f'{db_path.name}.pre{stem}-{ts}.bak'; no statvfs check and no removal of old .bak files anywhere in the module.
Recommendation

Add a free-space precheck before _auto_backup and a retention policy (keep N most recent .bak, prune older). Log the backup size.

low Migrations 0005 and 0007 disable foreign_keys and rebuild titles via DROP/RENAME without re-validating referential integrity data-integrity Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/migrations/0007_drop_iptv_kinds.sql:11, 96-103 (PRAGMA foreign_keys=OFF; DROP TABLE titles; RENAME) unverified
Description

0005 and 0007 set 'PRAGMA foreign_keys = OFF', rebuild titles via CREATE titles_new / INSERT SELECT / DROP titles / RENAME, then set foreign_keys=ON. The dependent tables (title_features/title_genres/etc.) have no FK to titles in this schema (0001 declares only rec_outcomes->rec_log), so this does not orphan via FK, and 0007 manually deletes iptv feature/genre/vec rows first. However the PRAGMA foreign_keys toggle inside a migration is a no-op-with-a-catch: SQLite ignores foreign_keys changes inside a transaction, and the migrator wraps each file in BEGIN IMMEDIATE (db.py 457), so these PRAGMAs do not actually take effect during the rebuild and no 'PRAGMA foreign_key_check' is run afterward to confirm integrity.

Evidence
0007 line 11 'PRAGMA foreign_keys = OFF;' then BEGIN IMMEDIATE wraps it (db.py _migrate '_migration_statements' executed inside 'with transaction(conn)'). SQLite docs: foreign_keys is a no-op within a transaction. No 'PRAGMA foreign_key_check' anywhere.
Recommendation

Drop the misleading in-transaction PRAGMA toggles and add an explicit 'PRAGMA foreign_key_check' assertion (outside the wrapping transaction, or as a post-migrate step) after any table rebuild so a future FK addition cannot silently orphan rows.

low build_holdout.py opens the production DB read-write and approximates pre-outcome library state by subtracting current positives data-integrity Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/eval/build_holdout.py:54 (sqlite3.connect without mode=ro); 91-109 (positives subtraction approximation) unverified
Description

build_holdout opens the live DB with sqlite3.connect(p) (read-write, no mode=ro URI) for a pure read job, and reconstructs the pre-outcome library by subtracting current positives from the current library because historical library snapshots are not kept (documented in-comment). The recall-signal approximation is reasonable and honestly disclosed, but the read-write open of a production DB from an operator script is needless risk, and the approximation means the holdout the auto-promotion gate trusts is a lossy reconstruction, not ground truth.

Evidence
build_holdout.py: 'conn = sqlite3.connect(p)' (no '?mode=ro'); comment: 'A more faithful version would replay timestamped library_items ... we don't keep historical library state today; subtracting current positives is the cheapest fix that restores the recall signal.'
Recommendation

Open the holdout-builder connection read-only. Longer term, persist timestamped library snapshots so the eval set reflects true pre-outcome state rather than a subtraction heuristic, since this artifact is the sole promotion gate.

low main.rs panics on any config/bind error instead of failing gracefully production-readiness Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/main.rs:30, 60, 66 unverified
Description

TRANSCODER_BIND parse, listener bind, and axum::serve all use .expect(), so a malformed env var or a port already in use produces a bare panic. Acceptable for fail-fast boot, but combined with no crash telemetry (§15) the operator gets nothing actionable. Also note: the same `expect` style means there is no graceful shutdown handler (no SIGTERM trap), so on container stop in-flight ffmpeg children are not cleanly killed/reaped — they are orphaned by the runtime tear-down (compounds the zombie/reaping finding).

Evidence
main.rs:30 `.expect("TRANSCODER_BIND must be a valid socket address")`, :60 `.expect("failed to bind transcoder listener")`, :66 `.expect("transcoder server crashed")`. No tokio signal handler / axum graceful shutdown in the file.
Recommendation

Add axum::serve(...).with_graceful_shutdown(...) wired to SIGTERM/SIGINT that kills + reaps all live sessions and cleans work_dir on shutdown.

low Unused import warning indicates the crate is not built clean / not linted in CI maintainability Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole) crates/transcoder/src/routes.rs:10 unverified
Description

`use std::sync::Arc;` is unused and produces a compiler warning on every build. Minor on its own, but it signals the crate is not gated by `cargo clippy -D warnings` (or even `cargo build` warning-checks) in CI — consistent with the non-compiling tests slipping through. A transcoder handling untrusted input needs a clean lint gate.

Evidence
`cargo build --package transcoder` emits `warning: unused import: 'Arc' --> crates/transcoder/src/routes.rs:10:5`.
Recommendation

Remove the import, add `cargo clippy -D warnings` and `cargo test -p transcoder` to .github/workflows/ci.yml so the dead tests and warnings cannot regress.

low Library grid poster images lack width/height (layout shift) and broken-image fallback performance Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/search/MediaCard.tsx:24-36 unverified
Description

MediaCard posters have loading="lazy" decoding="async" (good) but no intrinsic width/height and no onError handler. The CSS .media-card__poster sets aspect-ratio so CLS is largely contained, but a 404/expired TMDB or Xtream poster URL renders a broken-image glyph rather than swapping to the existing letter fallback (which only shows when poster is falsy, not when it fails to load). IPTV grids (LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab <img>) have the same no-onError gap.

Evidence
MediaCard: `<img src={poster} alt="" loading="lazy" decoding="async" referrerPolicy="no-referrer" />` — no width/height, no onError. LiveTab.tsx:139 `<img src={c.stream_icon} ... loading="lazy" />` likewise.
Recommendation

Add an onError handler that hides the broken img and reveals the letter fallback; set explicit width/height attributes to fully eliminate CLS.

low TabPlaceholder is dead code, and no tab degrades gracefully when its upstream integration is unconfigured maintainability Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/tabs/TabPlaceholder.tsx:whole file unverified
Description

TabPlaceholder still contains a 'Phase 2 wiring proof' that console.logs on confirm and is not imported by any of the nine content tabs (all render real query-backed UI). It is dead code shipping in the bundle. Separately, unconfigured integrations surface as raw error text ('Couldn't reach Sonarr. Check that the dev server has SONARR_API_KEY in .env.local.' — TvTab.tsx:509) which is developer-facing copy, not a first-run 'connect this service' state — exactly what a TabPlaceholder-style component should provide for a fresh NAS install.

Evidence
TabPlaceholder.tsx comment: 'Phase 2 wiring proof... Removed in Phase 3+ when real flows replace this content' and `console.log(`[probe] ${title} confirmed`)`; no consumer imports it. TvTab error copy references '.env.local', a dev-only concept.
Recommendation

Delete TabPlaceholder, and replace the dev-oriented integration-error copy with a user-facing 'This service isn't connected yet' empty state for fresh installs.

low LiveTab Export-M3U uses window.alert and an unguarded clipboard write for a primary action production-readiness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/tabs/LiveTab.tsx:209-213 unverified
Description

The Export M3U button awaits generatePlaylist(), writes to navigator.clipboard (catch-swallowed), then surfaces the URL via a blocking window.alert(). alert() is non-stylable, blocks the main thread, is unavailable in some embedded/WebView contexts, and is inconsistent with the app's Toast/ConfirmProvider system used everywhere else. A failed generatePlaylist() is unhandled (no try/catch) so a backend error throws an unhandled rejection with no user feedback.

Evidence
`onClick={async () => { const { url, expiresAt } = await iptvApi.generatePlaylist(); await navigator.clipboard.writeText(url).catch(() => undefined); alert(`M3U URL copied. Expires ${new Date(expiresAt).toLocaleString()}.\n\n${url}`) }}` — no try/catch around generatePlaylist; alert() for output.
Recommendation

Wrap in try/catch and surface success/failure via the existing Toast; replace alert() with a small modal or toast that shows the copyable URL.

low DownloadsTab/MediaTab error copy leaks dev-environment instructions to end users maintainability Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/tabs/DownloadsTab.tsx:85-86 unverified
Description

Production error states tell the user to edit .env.local: 'Couldn't reach SABnzbd. Check that the dev server has SAB_API_KEY in .env.local.' End users (the household / App-Store viewers) have no dev server and no .env.local. This is developer copy shipped to production and additionally renders String(error) which can expose internal URLs/stack detail.

Evidence
DownloadsTab.tsx:85 `<p>Couldn't reach SABnzbd. Check that the dev server has SAB_API_KEY in .env.local.</p>` and `<p className="downloads-tab__error-detail">{String(queue.error)}</p>`; same .env.local copy in TvTab.tsx:509 and MoviesTab.tsx:490.
Recommendation

Replace with user-appropriate copy ('Downloads are temporarily unavailable — the server can't reach the downloader.') and gate the raw String(error) detail behind an admin-only / dev-only flag.

low db.ts legacy backfill + fresh inserts store empty checksums and the two runners' schema_migrations DDL disagree, so unifying them later will mis-fire data-integrity Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/services/db.ts:56,59,73,76,122 unverified
Description

Even if db.ts were wired to verify checksums later, every row it writes today is uncheckable: fresh applies store '' (line 122) and both legacy backfills store '' (lines 59, 76). migrator.ts's verify skips only rows equal to '(file-not-found)' (line 258), NOT '' — so a '' row compared against a real sha256 would become a permanent false 'mismatch' WARN if the runners were ever unified. The two runners also disagree on the column DDL: db.ts declares `checksum TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''` (lines 56, 73) while migrator.ts declares `checksum TEXT NOT NULL` with no default (lines 139, 180) — a DB bootstrapped by one and extended by the other can hit a NOT NULL violation.

Evidence
db.ts:122/59/76 write `''`; db.ts:56/73 `checksum TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''` vs migrator.ts:139/180 `checksum TEXT NOT NULL`. migrator.ts:258 `if (stored !== checksum && stored !== '(file-not-found)')` — '' not excluded.
Recommendation

When consolidating to one runner (see the high finding), compute real sha256 on apply and have verify treat '' as 'unknown — skip'. Align the schema_migrations DDL exactly so a DB is never bootstrapped by one shape and extended by another.

low Hardcoded D18 boot self-destruct on 2026-06-25 will refuse to boot every server on/after that date production-readiness Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/index.ts:61-65 unverified
Description

index.ts throws (exit 1) unconditionally if Date.now() >= 2026-06-25, to force removal of the legacy SHA-256 session-key path (session.ts legacyKey, line 349). This is a calendar bomb: today is 2026-05-30. On 2026-06-25 ANY container that restarts — or any fresh deploy where the legacy path hasn't been removed — refuses to boot, regardless of whether legacy cookies are still in circulation. For an App-Store-bound product on an unattended NAS, a routine reboot after that date is a hard outage with no operator override. Intent (force the migration) is sound; mechanism (brick all boots) is operationally dangerous.

Evidence
index.ts:61 `if (Date.now() >= new Date('2026-06-25').getTime()) { throw new Error('HKDF legacy-SHA256 grace window expired; remove legacyKey path per D18') }`. session.ts:348-349 still defines the legacyKey it's guarding.
Recommendation

Gate the throw on whether the legacy path is actually still present (a build-time const flipped when legacyKey is deleted), or downgrade to a loud startup WARN plus a CI check that fails the build after the deadline. A running production service must never self-brick on a wall-clock date with no override.

low telemetryPiiScrub substring key denylist over-redacts benign diagnostic keys; t= and JWE regexes are conservative but have known gaps maintainability Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.ts:78-86,129,138 unverified
Description

isRedactedKey is case-insensitive SUBSTRING match against a set including 'key','auth','token','hash','session' — so legitimate crash-context fields like 'keyboard', 'authority', 'hashRoute', 'sessionCount', 'monkey' are blanket-REDACTED, degrading telemetry usefulness (the contract wants telemetry USEFUL, not only safe). STREAM_TOKEN_RE `/\bt=([^&\s"']+)/g` also clobbers any unrelated `t=` param (e.g. ?t=1 cache-busters), and JWE_CIPHERTEXT_RE only catches compact-serialized tokens contiguous in one string — a JWE split across object fields, or a raw base64url device token not starting with the literal `eyJ` header, is not caught (those rely on the field-key denylist). Acceptable conservatism for safety, but the key false-positives in particular will hide useful debugging context.

Evidence
Line 81 `if (lower.includes(pattern.toLowerCase()))` over a set with 'key','auth','token','hash','session'. Line 129 STREAM_TOKEN_RE. Line 138 `JWE_CIPHERTEXT_RE = /eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]{8,}\.[^"'\s;,]*/g`.
Recommendation

Anchor/exact-match the generic terms (keep substring only for clearly-secret roots) or maintain a known-safe-key allowlist; scope STREAM_TOKEN_RE to URL contexts; add a tests/vectors/telemetry-pii-scrub.json case for a token-split-across-fields scenario and document the non-eyJ raw-token reliance on the key denylist.

low Migration runner derives version with parseInt and silently continues on non-.sql/odd names; gaps and duplicate-version files are not detected correctness Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/services/db.ts:102,108-110 unverified
Description

db.ts's runner enumerates *.sql, sorts lexically, and parses the version from file.split('_')[0]. There is no check for (a) duplicate version numbers across two files, (b) gaps in the sequence, or (c) a file whose numeric prefix collides with an already-applied version from a different filename. Lexical sort also breaks ordering if the project ever exceeds 9999 migrations or mixes widths (e.g. 0010 vs 010) — unlikely at 4 files today but a latent ordering hazard. migrator.ts's versionFromFilename at least throws on a non-numeric prefix (line 189); db.ts's parseInt would yield NaN and then applied.has(NaN) is always false, so a malformed filename would attempt to apply on every boot.

Evidence
db.ts:102 `fs.readdirSync(migrationsDir).filter(f => f.endsWith('.sql')).sort()`, db.ts:109 `const version = parseInt(file.split('_')[0], 10)` with no NaN guard and no duplicate/gap detection.
Recommendation

Validate that parsed versions are unique, strictly increasing, and non-NaN; throw on a malformed filename rather than silently retrying it every boot. (Consolidating onto migrator.ts, which already throws on non-numeric prefixes, addresses most of this.)

low SAB read forwarder relays SAB's HTML error page verbatim on a rejected apikey production-readiness Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/sab.ts:36-46, 92-98 unverified
Description

sabCall forces output=json, but the service comment notes 'a non-JSON 200 from SAB usually means the apikey was rejected and SAB returned its HTML error page.' forward() blindly relays r.status + body with the upstream Content-Type, so a misconfigured/rotated SAB_API_KEY surfaces to the SPA as a 200 with an HTML body the client fails to JSON-parse — an opaque client error rather than a clear 502. There is no JSON-shape validation of the SAB response.

Evidence
sab.ts:92-98 forward() relays body+status+upstream Content-Type with no JSON check; sab.ts service comment:3-5 acknowledges the HTML-on-bad-key behavior.
Recommendation

Detect a non-JSON 200 from SAB in forward()/sabCall and map it to 502 {error:'sab_auth_failed'} so a bad key is diagnosable.

low Movie 'upgrade' selects by qualityWeight sort with no verified delta against the current file correctness Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/radarr.ts:546-562 unverified
Description

The upgrade route relies entirely on Radarr setting rejected:true for equal-or-worse releases, then grabs eligible[0] by qualityWeight. If Radarr's rejection semantics differ (custom formats, propers, indexer quirks), a non-strict-upgrade can pass the filter and Radarr's import 'then decides,' so the dashboard may grab+import bandwidth for a lateral move. This mirrors the ROADMAP-noted M3 matcher concern (results.first() with no confidence filter) in the downloads path: selection is upstream-trust plus a single sort key, not a verified improvement over the current quality.

Evidence
radarr.ts:546-554 filters only on !rejected && size<=cap && fits-free-space then '.sort((a,b)=>b.qualityWeight-a.qualityWeight)'; comment 495-501 admits it reuses the same filter chain and 'Radarr's import logic then decides whether to replace'.
Recommendation

Compare best.qualityWeight against the current movieFile quality before grabbing and skip when it isn't a strict improvement, so the upgrade endpoint can't burn a grab on a lateral release.

low Discord webhook is host-regex-validated only; egress is delegated to *arr so ssrfGuard never applies security Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads server/routes/notifications.ts:201-203 unverified
Description

The webhook is validated only by ^https://(discord.com|discordapp.com)/api/webhooks/. That host allowlist is reasonable and the route is admin-only, so this is low severity, but the actual outbound POST is performed by Sonarr/Radarr (the dashboard only configures them), so server/services/ssrfGuard.ts isPublicHttpsUpstream is never applied to this URL — there is no defense-in-depth if the regex is ever loosened to support generic webhooks.

Evidence
notifications.ts:201 'if (!/^https:\/\/(discord\.com|discordapp\.com)\/api\/webhooks\//.test(url))'; isPublicHttpsUpstream from ssrfGuard.ts is not imported in notifications.ts.
Recommendation

Keep the host allowlist; if generic webhooks are ever added, route the URL through isPublicHttpsUpstream first and document that egress is delegated to *arr.

low device/poll and device/start mint/exchange the 180-day device token with NO rate limiting (unlike the cookie /plex/check path) security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/routes/device.ts:86-177 unverified
Description

device.post('/start') and device.post('/poll') reuse Plex createPin/checkPin but, unlike auth.ts's /plex/pin and /plex/check (which call enforceAuthRateLimit), apply NO rate limiting. /poll exchanges a PIN, runs the membership gate, and mints a 180-day Bearer JWE persisted as a device_tokens row. An attacker who can drive a PIN to authorization can hammer /poll/start without the per-client/global throttle the cookie path has. The prod membership gate (device.ts:135-142, PLEX_SERVER_ID) bounds this, but the asymmetry matters for the longest-lived credential the system issues.

Evidence
device.ts:86-97 `/start` and device.ts:99-177 `/poll` contain no enforceAuthRateLimit call; contrast auth.ts:252-253 and 262-274. Device tokens are the 180-day class (session.ts:155).
Recommendation

Apply enforceAuthRateLimit (a dedicated 'device' kind) to /start and /poll, keyed by client identity and globally, since /poll mints the longest-lived token.

low Passkey register/login endpoints have no rate limiting, allowing webauthn_challenges / local:-sub churn security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/routes/passkey.ts:47-125 unverified
Description

The passkey endpoints are correctly public (they ARE the login) and Origin-CSRF-gated globally. register/options mints a fresh local:<ulid> sub + challenge row on every call with no rate limit; login/options mints a challenge per call. authorizeOrRedeem correctly precedes persistCredential (no orphan credential), and challenges are TTL-swept on each put, so impact is bounded — but the other auth routes have a limiter and these do not.

Evidence
passkey.ts:47-54 register/options and 97-100 login/options have no enforceAuthRateLimit; webauthn.ts:115-133 beginRegistration mints a sub + challenge unconditionally; mitigations: sweepExpiredChallenges (webauthn.ts:75), authZ-before-persist (passkey.ts:79-87).
Recommendation

Add rate limiting to the passkey options/verify endpoints consistent with auth.ts, keyed by client identity, to blunt challenge-table flooding and ulid churn.

low UserMenu logout/sign-out uses window.location hard navigation and swallows failures correctness Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System src/components/auth/UserMenu.tsx:(sign-out handler) unverified
Description

The sign-out handler calls signOut() then catches and ignores any error, and the broader logout path discards SPA state via a full navigation. If the logout request fails (e.g. the 403 that would come from the unwired CSRF, or a network error), the user can be left appearing signed out client-side while the server session persists — a confusing half-logout — and the hard navigation defeats the SPA model. signOutError is surfaced in one place but the catch-and-ignore branch can still hide failures.

Evidence
UserMenu.tsx sign-out: `void signOut().then(() => setOpen(false)).catch(() => {})` — the .catch swallows errors; the logout flow relies on a hard reload rather than router navigation.
Recommendation

Surface logout failures to the user (signOutError is available), and after a confirmed successful logout use router navigation instead of window.location to stay within the SPA.

low ApiKeySettings and admin app links rely on cross-subdomain endpoints/links with no in-app feedback when absent production-readiness Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System src/components/auth/UserMenu.tsx:(APP_LINKS) / ApiKeySettings.tsx fetchUsageMe unverified
Description
