UserMenu hardcodes admin links to sonarr/radarr/sab.theemeraldexchange.com (public hostnames), and ApiKeySettings polls /api/usage/me every 15s when a key is set, silently returning null on any non-ok response (the usage card then shows zeros indistinguishably from a real zero). These are not failures per se, but the silent-null pattern means a broken usage endpoint is invisible to the user and to telemetry (which is also absent). The Anthropic key is stored only in localStorage and sent as a request header, which is the intended trade-off but worth flagging for the App-Store privacy review.

Evidence
ApiKeySettings.tsx fetchUsageMe: `if (!r.ok) return null` then renders `usage.data?.calls ?? 0`; UserMenu APP_LINKS hardcodes external service subdomains.
Recommendation

Distinguish 'no data' from 'request failed' in the usage card, and either gate the admin app links behind a reachability check or accept their static nature explicitly. Document the localStorage-only AI key handling for the App-Store privacy questionnaire.

low Health endpoint reports a half-truth: ok=true even when the schema is broken or migrations failed production-readiness Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/routes.rs:64-75 unverified
Description

health() sets `ok = SELECT 1 succeeds` and reports schema = schema_version().unwrap_or(-1). If migrations are corrupt or the DB is at the wrong version, `SELECT 1` still succeeds, so health returns ok:true with schema:-1 (or a stale version). An orchestrator/compose healthcheck keying on ok:true would mark a structurally-broken instance healthy. There is also no liveness signal for the scan scheduler (a wedged scan — see the ffprobe finding — is invisible to health).

Evidence
routes.rs:65-69 `let db_ok = sqlx::query_scalar::<_,i64>("SELECT 1")...is_ok(); let schema = state.db.schema_version().await.unwrap_or(-1);` then returns `"ok": db_ok` regardless of whether schema == expected SCHEMA_VERSION.
Recommendation

Fold a schema check into ok (ok = db_ok && schema == SCHEMA_VERSION) and surface a scan-stuck indicator (e.g. scanning held > N minutes). Add a compose healthcheck that actually hits /health (none is currently defined for the media-core service).

low SCHEMA_VERSION constant can silently drift from the migration ledger maintainability Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/lib.rs:26 unverified
Description

SCHEMA_VERSION is a hand-maintained `pub const ... = 2` that /version reports and is documented to be 'bumped in lockstep' with db::MIGRATIONS. Nothing enforces the lockstep: if a dev adds migration (3, ...) to db.rs::MIGRATIONS but forgets to bump the const, the DB advances to v3 while /version advertises v2 and health's schema check (if added) would misfire. The db.rs idempotency test asserts schema_version()==2 against the live ledger but never asserts it equals SCHEMA_VERSION.

Evidence
lib.rs:26 `pub const SCHEMA_VERSION: i64 = 2;` vs db.rs:11-18 MIGRATIONS array (currently 2 entries). No test asserts `MIGRATIONS.last().0 == SCHEMA_VERSION`.
Recommendation

Derive SCHEMA_VERSION from `MIGRATIONS.last().map(|m| m.0)` (a const fn or a once-computed value) or add a test asserting they match, so the version can never silently drift.

low Library LIKE search is unindexed and case/locale-naive — O(n) scans and missing diacritic/case matches at scale performance Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/routes.rs:112-145, 168-201, 241-256 unverified
Description

list_movies/list_shows/list_episodes_all implement ?q= via `WHERE title LIKE '%' || ? || '%'`. The leading wildcard makes idx_movies_title/idx_shows_title unusable, so every search is a full table scan + a second full-scan COUNT(*). For a Plex-scale library this is slow, and LIKE is ASCII-case-insensitive only on SQLite's default collation — 'à' won't match 'A', and there's no normalization against the title the way scanner.rs normalizes show names. It's correct but won't deliver Netflix-grade search responsiveness.

Evidence
routes.rs:114 `FROM movies WHERE title LIKE '%' || ? || '%'` (and the parallel COUNT at :123); the only indexes (0001_init.sql:69-71) are plain B-trees on title that a leading-% LIKE cannot use.
Recommendation

Move search to an FTS5 virtual table (or at least a normalized lowercased search column with a prefix index), and consider a single windowed query that returns both rows and total to avoid the second full scan.

low Watch-state media_id is never validated against an existing movie/episode (orphan/forgeable rows) data-integrity Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/src/routes.rs:691-723 unverified
Description

post_watch upserts into media_watch_state with the client-supplied media_kind/media_id without checking that (media_kind, media_id) refers to a real movies/episodes row. media_watch_state has no foreign key to movies/episodes (it can't, since media_id is polymorphic), and the schema CHECK only constrains media_kind to ('movie','episode'). A caller can therefore create watch rows for nonexistent or arbitrary ids, and deleting a movie/episode leaves an orphaned watch row forever (no cascade). Low impact since the acting_sub guard prevents cross-user writes, but it's an integrity gap for a 'continue watching' feature.

Evidence
routes.rs:702-720 binds body.media_kind/body.media_id straight into the INSERT...ON CONFLICT with no prior existence check; migration 0001_init.sql:52-61 media_watch_state has a polymorphic media_id with no FK and no cascade.
Recommendation

Validate (media_kind, media_id) resolves to a live row before upserting (or accept the orphan risk explicitly and add a periodic GC). Reuse resolve_media_file-style lookups to reject bogus ids with 404/400.

low Cargo.toml pins broad caret ranges; reproducibility depends on a Cargo.lock whose tracking could not be confirmed in this session dependency Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server) crates/media-core/Cargo.toml:21-38 unverified
Description

Direct deps use loose ranges (axum 0.8, tokio 1, tower 0.5, sqlx 0.8, reqwest 0.12, thiserror 2). For a binary crate that ships to a NAS this is fine only if Cargo.lock is committed and used by the Docker build. The Dockerfile does `COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ...` (good intent), but I could not verify in this session that Cargo.lock is git-tracked (Bash output was intermittently empty). If the lock is gitignored, two builds months apart can pull different patch/minor releases of axum/sqlx/reqwest, risking silent behavior drift on a security-sensitive service.

Evidence
Cargo.toml:21-34 caret ranges; Dockerfile:19 `COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock LICENSE ./`. Lock-tracking status unverified due to harness Bash returning empty stdout during the check.
Recommendation

Confirm Cargo.lock is committed for the workspace (it must be for a binary); if not, `git add Cargo.lock`. Optionally add `cargo build --locked` in the Dockerfile so a missing/stale lock fails the build loudly.

low M3U export endpoint uses non-constant-time secret comparison and dumps full catalog security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/routes/iptv.ts:1066-1070 unverified
Description

/export/recommender authorizes via 'secret !== env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET' — a plain string comparison, not crypto.timingSafeEqual, giving a (small but real) timing oracle on a long-lived shared secret. On success it returns the entire vod+series catalog including tmdb_id/plot/cast for every title. It is unauthenticated except for the header secret (no requireAuth), so a leaked or guessed secret exposes the full library.

Evidence
iptv.ts:1068 'secret !== env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET'; handler has no requireAuth middleware (iptv.ts:1066).
Recommendation

Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers; treat the export as an internal-principal call (the repo has internalPrincipal infra) rather than a shared header secret, and bind it to the internal network.

low remux/live byte streams have no upstream connect/idle timeout production-readiness Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/routes/iptv.ts:720,859 unverified
Description

The MPEG-TS live passthrough and catch-up timeshift call fetch() with only the client-abort signal wired; there is no connect or idle timeout on the upstream. A hung upstream (provider stalls after headers) holds the request, the Node socket, and (because the byte path doesn't heartbeat) eventually a phantom concurrency slot until the client gives up. The list/EPG fetches DO use fetchWithTimeout (xtream.ts) — the byte paths are the inconsistent ones.

Evidence
iptv.ts:720 'await fetch(upstreamUrl, { signal: controller.signal, headers... })' — controller only aborts on client abort (718); contrast xtream.ts:65 fetchWithTimeout with explicit timeout.
Recommendation

Add an upstream connect/idle timeout (AbortSignal.timeout composed with the client signal) to the live/catchup/vod byte fetches so a dead provider doesn't pin server resources.

low ffmpeg remux input has no protocol whitelist security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/services/iptvRemux.ts:120-134 unverified
Description

startRemuxSession passes opts.upstreamUrl directly as ffmpeg '-i' with no '-protocol_whitelist'. The URL is server-constructed from env creds today (low risk), but ffmpeg's default protocol set is broad; if a future code path ever feeds an upstream-influenced URL (e.g. a provider-redirected manifest) ffmpeg could be steered to file:/concat:/other protocols. Defense-in-depth is cheap here.

Evidence
iptvRemux.ts:124-125 '-i', opts.upstreamUrl with no '-protocol_whitelist' among the args.
Recommendation

Add '-protocol_whitelist file,http,https,tcp,tls,crypto' (or stricter) and reject non-http(s) upstreamUrl before spawn.

low playlist.m3u and external M3U credential exposure window via long-lived per-channel tokens security Server — IPTV Core (M1) server/routes/iptv.ts:336-344 unverified
Description

The external-player M3U mints a 300s 'live' token per channel embedded in URLs in the downloadable .m3u body. The body is Cache-Control:no-store but is an attachment a player stores on disk; while 300s TTL limits the window, the file remains a list of self-authenticating stream URLs for any channel for 5 minutes. Combined with the redirect-SSRF gap, a leaked M3U yields short-lived authenticated proxy access. Acceptable for the homelab threat model but worth noting for App-Store distribution where the file lands on many devices.

Evidence
iptv.ts:336-344 signs a 'live' token per channel into '/api/iptv/stream/live/${id}.ts?t=${token}' written into the served playlist body.
Recommendation

Consider device-bound playlist tokens (the jti->row infra exists) so a leaked M3U is revocable per device, and shorten/rotate the per-channel TTL.

low Orphaned/dead nginx config contradicts the deployed architecture maintainability Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy nginx/default.conf:1-3 unverified
Description

nginx/default.conf is a complete SPA-serving config whose header says it serves the built React dist/ 'as a single-page app' from /usr/share/nginx/html. But there are exactly four tracked Dockerfiles (backend, media-core, transcoder, recommender) and none install or use nginx (verified: the only 'nginx' hit across compose/Dockerfiles is an unrelated comment about reverse proxies). The real SPA build/publish path is Netlify (netlify.toml). deploy-nas.sh:22-23 even tells operators to tear down 'the V1 nginx-static container' separately. This is dead V1 config that will mislead future operators into believing a containerized frontend exists.

Evidence
nginx/default.conf:1-3 comment 'Serves the built dashboard (dist/) as a single-page app'; git ls-files '*Dockerfile*' => only 4 backend/rust/python Dockerfiles; deploy-nas.sh:22-23 'Touch the V1 nginx-static container. Tear that down separately'.
Recommendation

Delete nginx/default.conf (and nginx/) since the SPA is Netlify-hosted, or, if a self-hosted frontend container is intended, add the nginx Dockerfile + compose service that uses it and update the docs.

low clippy and cargo fmt are advisory (continue-on-error) — Rust lint never gates the security-critical contracts crate maintainability Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy .github/workflows/ci.yml:232-240 unverified
Description

The rust job's fmt and clippy steps both set continue-on-error: true, so they always pass regardless of findings. The clippy invocation even includes '-- -D warnings' (intent to deny) but the continue-on-error neutralizes it. For the workspace that holds the canonical cross-language contracts (emerald-contracts: token/JWE/HMAC/HKDF), clippy catches real correctness/soundness issues, not just style. This lets lint-flagged or unsound patterns merge silently into the most security-critical code in the repo.

Evidence
ci.yml:238-240 'run: cargo clippy --all-targets --workspace -- -D warnings' with 'continue-on-error: true' (240); fmt likewise at 232-236.
Recommendation

Drop continue-on-error from the clippy step so 'cargo clippy ... -- -D warnings' actually gates; keep fmt advisory if desired but enforce clippy at least on the contracts/token crates.

low Base images pinned to mutable tags, not digests — non-reproducible builds production-readiness Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy Dockerfile:23,68,77; crates/media-core/Dockerfile:13,25,30; crates/transcoder/Dockerfile:13,25,30; recommender/Dockerfile:15,58; docker-compose.yml:322,333 unverified
Description

All base/runtime images use floating tags (rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim, debian:bookworm-slim, python:3.12-slim, mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1, postgres:15, redis:7-alpine). These tags receive ongoing patch pushes, so two builds of the same commit can differ, breaking reproducibility and supply-chain provenance; a poisoned upstream tag is pulled silently. glitchtip:6.1 is the one digest-less-but-pinned good example with explanatory comment.

Evidence
Dockerfile:23 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm', 68 'FROM node:24-slim', 77 'mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1'; docker-compose.yml:322 'postgres:15', 333 'redis:7-alpine' — none use @sha256 digests.
Recommendation

Pin runtime base images by digest (FROM node:24-slim@sha256:...) or at minimum immutable patch tags, and adopt a Renovate/Dependabot bump policy.

low .env.example ships a literal weak SESSION_SECRET default security Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy .env.example:24 unverified
Description

.env.example leaves SESSION_SECRET blank with a generate-with-node comment — actually correct on closer read. The real low-severity issue is XTREAM_HOST=https://example.mybunny.tv being a concrete-looking default and the dev example embedding live-looking NAS LAN IPs in commented backing-service URLs (line 48-50) and .env.production.example shipping real LAN IPs (10.0.0.52) and the real owner username (ADMINS=ChrisPachulski, line 82). For a repo the owner flags as potentially public (CLAUDE.md), committing the real admin username and internal LAN topology in .env.production.example is information disclosure, even if not a secret.

Evidence
.env.production.example:82 'ADMINS=ChrisPachulski'; 108 'SONARR_URL=http://10.0.0.52:8989/tv', 110/112 same host — real internal IP + admin identity in a tracked example file. CLAUDE.md: 'Repo may be public — never hardcode ... IP addresses, or personal info'.
Recommendation

Replace the real admin username with a placeholder (e.g. your-plex-username) and the 10.0.0.52 LAN IP with a documented placeholder (e.g. <nas-lan-ip>) in .env.production.example to honor the repo's own no-PII/no-IP rule.

low ESLint applies browser globals to server code and uses no type-aware linting maintainability Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy eslint.config.js:18-20 unverified
Description

eslint.config.js applies one flat config to all **/*.{ts,tsx} with languageOptions: { globals: globals.browser }. server/ is Node/Hono code needing globals.node (process, Buffer); with only browser globals, server-side undefined-global usage is either noisy or defanged. There is no parserOptions.project, so the highest-value TS lint rules for an async streaming backend — no-floating-promises and no-misused-promises — are not active, meaning unawaited async in the backend (e.g., the void registerIptvSchedule pattern) is never lint-caught.

Evidence
eslint.config.js:18-20 'languageOptions: { globals: globals.browser }' — single block for all ts/tsx, no globals.node override for server/, no parserOptions.project.
Recommendation

Split into a browser block for src/ and a node block for server/ (globals.node) and enable type-aware linting (parserOptions.project) so no-floating-promises/no-misused-promises gate the backend.

low CI never builds or smoke-tests the Docker images that actually ship to production production-readiness Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy .github/workflows/ci.yml:14-256 unverified
Description

All CI jobs run on the host runner via npm/cargo/pytest directly. Nothing runs docker build on any of the four Dockerfiles or docker compose config/build. The Dockerfiles contain non-trivial, fragile logic (cross-stage NAPI .node staging with the documented npx --package @napi-rs/cli quirk, static-ffmpeg COPY, better-sqlite3 source compile, maturin PyO3 wheel, CPU-torch). A change that breaks the image build passes CI green and only fails at docker compose up -d --build on the NAS during deploy — there is no pre-deploy validation of the deployable artifact.

Evidence
ci.yml jobs test/test-no-iptv/recommender/rust (14-256) contain no 'docker build', 'docker compose build', or 'docker compose config'; the only image build is scripts/deploy-nas.sh:204-219 on the NAS itself.
Recommendation

Add a CI job running 'docker compose build' (or per-Dockerfile builds) plus 'docker compose config -q' so image-build regressions are caught before deploy.

low Telemetry init has no error-capture integration and no flush-on-shutdown — crashes during graceful shutdown may not be reported production-readiness Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy server/index.ts:37-46, 93-99 unverified
Description

Sentry.init only wires onUnhandledRejectionIntegration; there is no Hono onError -> Sentry.captureException bridge in app.ts (verified: no onError/captureException), so handled errors thrown inside route handlers are returned to the client but never reach Glitchtip unless they bubble to an unhandled rejection. Additionally, the shutdown() handler (93-99) closes DBs and calls process.exit without awaiting Sentry.flush(), so any event queued just before SIGTERM (the normal container-stop signal) can be dropped before transmission. Given §15 makes telemetry mandatory, silently losing handled-error and shutdown-window events undercuts the contract's intent.

Evidence
server/index.ts:43-45 integrations only '[Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration(...)]'; shutdown() (93-99) 'closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...)' with no 'await Sentry.flush()'; no app.onError/captureException in app.ts.
Recommendation

Add a Hono app.onError that calls Sentry.captureException before returning the error response, and make shutdown() await Sentry.flush(2000) before process.exit so queued events transmit on SIGTERM.

low recommenderEvents click-mirror and feedback mirror drop on failure with no metric — silent loss of the recommender's training signal data-integrity Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/routes/recommenderEvents.ts:65-73 unverified
Description

The /event route fires postFeedback as void and always returns {ok:true} even when the sidecar is down or the 3s mirror timeout trips; the file header states this click mirror is the ONLY path for click attribution. mirrorPost only console.warns on non-ok (recommender.ts:117-124) — there is no counter/metric for drop rate. For a system whose recommender is supposed to learn from these outcomes, a silently-down sidecar means click/feedback signal is lost with zero alerting. Given section-15 makes Glitchtip telemetry mandatory, console.warn-only is a gap.

Evidence
recommenderEvents.ts:66-72 `void postFeedback(...)` then unconditional `return c.json({ ok: true })`; recommender.ts:117-124 mirrorPost only `console.warn` on non-ok, no metric emission.
Recommendation

Emit a Glitchtip counter/breadcrumb on mirror failure so signal loss is observable, wiring these drops into the mandatory telemetry pipeline rather than console only.

low tagLocalAvailability title-fallback full-scans the local movies/shows table on every uncached suggestions refresh performance Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/services/localAvailability.ts:88-98 unverified
Description

When any item is unmatched by tmdb_id (expected today, since media.db rows currently have tmdb_id=NULL per the in-code comment), the fallback runs `SELECT title, year FROM ${table} WHERE year IS NOT NULL` — a full table scan of all movies (or all shows) — then rebuilds a normalized key set in memory, on every suggestions request reaching this path, with no caching of that key set across requests. For a large library this is repeated O(N) work per refresh per user.

Evidence
localAvailability.ts:89-91 `db.raw.prepare(`SELECT title, year FROM ${table} WHERE year IS NOT NULL`).all()` runs whenever unmatched.length>0; suggestions.ts has the same fallback inline at :1804-1806. No memoization of localKeys.
Recommendation

Cache the normalized (title|year) key set per table with a short TTL invalidated on media.db mtime change, or rely on the tmdb_id JOIN once media-core TMDB enrichment lands and drop the full-scan fallback.

low Two copies of tagLocalAvailability exist (route module + extracted service) and can drift maintainability Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/routes/suggestions.ts:1762-1845 unverified
Description

suggestions.ts contains a private inline tagLocalAvailability (1762-1845) that is byte-for-byte the same logic as the extracted, unit-tested server/services/localAvailability.ts. The route uses its OWN inline copy (suggestions.ts:2147), not the tested service. So the tests in tagLocalAvailability.test.ts validate the service module, but production traffic runs the duplicate inline copy — the two can silently diverge, and a fix to one won't reach the other.

Evidence
suggestions.ts:1762 `function tagLocalAvailability(items, kind)` (full duplicate) and its call at :2147; identical logic lives in localAvailability.ts:55-130 which is what the test imports.
Recommendation

Delete the inline copy and import the tested service so the unit tests actually cover the production path.

low media-library/local-availability relies on media.db existing but the file is created only by media-core, which the rest of this branch does not run production-readiness Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge server/services/mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts:22-36 unverified
Description

The singleton correctly degrades to null when media.db is absent, which is the SAFE behavior — but it also means that in any deployment where media-core hasn't scanned (the common case today, since the Apple/native and media-core integration is incomplete), local-availability tagging is silently a no-op for the entire process lifetime (openFailed latches true; only closeMediaLibraryDb resets it). Operators get one console.warn at first miss and then nothing. Combined with the search/detail gap above, the practical effect is that 'local availability' is off in most real deployments without any surfaced indication.

Evidence
mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts:24 `if (openFailed) return null` (latched), :29-34 single console.warn. env.MEDIA_DB_PATH is read once; no re-probe until closeMediaLibraryDb() (which nothing calls in steady state).
Recommendation

Surface media.db availability in a health/diag endpoint so operators can see whether local tagging is active, and consider a periodic re-probe (or mtime watch) so tagging lights up automatically once media-core first writes the file without requiring a process restart.

low @anthropic-ai/sdk retained in the server despite the recommender being built to replace per-request Claude calls dependency Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/package.json:25 unverified
Description

@anthropic-ai/sdk ^0.95.2 IS used (server/routes/suggestions.ts, server/services/usageLog.ts, plus SPA UsageDashboard/ApiKeySettings/TrendingRow read usage data), so it is not dead code. But the recommender service's own pyproject description states it 'Replaces per-request Claude calls with a SQLite + sqlite-vec scoring pipeline', and project memory records the recommender data-model decision that local-first scoring supersedes per-source Claude calls. This means the TS layer still carries a live outbound-LLM dependency that the architecture intended to retire — an outbound network/cost/PII surface and a pre-1.0 SDK (0.95.x) on the critical suggestions path. This is a coherence/parity gap, not a correctness bug.

Evidence
package.json line 25: '@anthropic-ai/sdk': '^0.95.2'. Used in server/routes/suggestions.ts, server/services/usageLog.ts. recommender/pyproject.toml line 4 description: 'Replaces per-request Claude calls with a SQLite + sqlite-vec scoring pipeline.'
Recommendation

Confirm whether suggestions.ts still calls Anthropic directly or only routes through the recommender. If direct Claude calls are vestigial, remove the SDK and the outbound path; if intentionally retained (e.g., as a premium tier), document it and pin the pre-1.0 SDK to an exact version since 0.x can break across minors.

low Caret-pinned napi-rs CLI on the ABI-critical native-addon builder, plus install-time compile fallback production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/crates/emerald-contracts-napi/package.json:21-28 unverified
Description

@napi-rs/cli is pinned with a caret (^3.7.0) and the package's `prepare` lifecycle script will shell out to compile the .node from Rust when the prebuilt binary for the current platform/arch is missing. @napi-rs/cli emits the ABI-critical .node that the entire crypto/contracts boundary loads; a floating major-internal version on that builder is a reproducibility risk for the wire-format-sensitive binding. The Dockerfiles correctly pre-stage the prebuilt .node so prepare short-circuits in the prod image (good), and the napi-builder stage fetches '@napi-rs/cli@3' explicitly — but '@3' is itself a floating major. Any consumer running plain `npm install` without a Rust toolchain (a Node-only Docker stage or a contributor machine) will hit the prepare-time cargo build and fail.

Evidence
crates/emerald-contracts-napi/package.json: devDependencies '@napi-rs/cli': '^3.7.0'; prepare script execs 'node scripts/build-with-dts-guard.mjs build --platform --release' when the platform .node is absent. Dockerfile:65: 'npx --yes --package @napi-rs/cli@3 napi build --platform --release' (floating @3).
Recommendation

Pin @napi-rs/cli to an exact version in both the crate package.json and the Dockerfile invocation. Keep building the .node in the dedicated Rust stage and consuming the prebuilt artifact downstream so no consumer triggers prepare-time compilation.

low Rust MSRV (1.85) is lower than the CI/Docker toolchain (1.90) — the declared minimum is never actually tested production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/Cargo.toml:15 unverified
Description

The workspace declares rust-version = "1.85" as its MSRV, but every build path uses Rust 1.90 (CI: dtolnay/rust-toolchain toolchain: '1.90' in both the rust and recommender jobs; all Dockerfiles: rust:1.90-slim-bookworm). Nothing ever compiles against 1.85, so the declared MSRV is unverified and could already be wrong (a 1.86+ API could have crept in). This is minor for a private, you-control-the-toolchain product, but a stated MSRV that is never exercised is misleading documentation.

Evidence
Cargo.toml line 15: 'rust-version = "1.85"'. ci.yml lines 143 & 217: toolchain: '1.90'. All Dockerfiles: 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm'.
Recommendation

Either add an MSRV CI lane that builds with 1.85 to prove the floor, or raise rust-version to match the only toolchain you actually ship (1.90). Pin Docker base images by digest rather than the floating '1.90-slim-bookworm' tag for fully reproducible builds.

low Container base images and the static-ffmpeg image use floating tags, not digests production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/Dockerfile:23 unverified
Description

All Dockerfile bases use floating tags: rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim, debian:bookworm-slim, python:3.12-slim, and the third-party mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1. Tag floats mean two builds of the 'same' Dockerfile can pull different underlying images as upstream re-pushes tags — a reproducibility and supply-chain-integrity gap. The ffmpeg image in particular (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) is a third-party image that ships the exact GPL binaries at the heart of the licensing finding; pinning it by digest also pins the provenance of those binaries.

Evidence
Dockerfile:23 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm'; Dockerfile:68 'FROM node:24-slim'; Dockerfile:77 / media-core:30 / transcoder:30 'COPY --from=mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1'; recommender/Dockerfile:58 'FROM python:3.12-slim'.
Recommendation

Pin every FROM and COPY --from by sha256 digest (e.g., node:24-slim@sha256:...). For mwader/static-ffmpeg, record the digest alongside a note of which ffmpeg/x264 versions and license that digest corresponds to, feeding the THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES file.

low node-cron 3.x is an aging major on a scheduler in the reliability-critical path dependency Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing /Users/cujo253/Documents/theemeraldexchange/package.json:38 unverified
Description

node-cron ^3.0.3 is pinned and IS used (server/services/iptvScheduler.ts, server/app.ts, server/env.ts) to drive scheduled IPTV refresh/reconciliation. The 3.x line is older with a quieter maintenance cadence and newer majors exist. For a server whose IPTV freshness depends on the scheduler, an aging-major scheduler is a latent reliability risk, and an unhandled throw inside a scheduled job can silently wedge the cron loop.

Evidence
package.json line 38: 'node-cron': '^3.0.3'; used in server/services/iptvScheduler.ts and registered in server/app.ts.
Recommendation

Evaluate upgrading node-cron or migrating to a more actively maintained scheduler (e.g., croner). Verify each scheduled job wraps its body in try/catch so a single failure does not kill the loop, and that failures report to Glitchtip.

low JWE protected-header parsing is non-strict: no deny_unknown_fields and no crit handling security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/jwe.rs:36-104 unverified
Description

ProtectedHeaderOwned deserializes only alg/enc/kid and silently ignores any additional members, including a JOSE `crit` (critical) header. A token minted by a buggy/future producer carrying extra security-relevant headers would be silently accepted by older verifiers, defeating the purpose of `crit`. Exploitability is limited because the full encoded header is bound as AAD (an attacker cannot add parameters to an existing valid token without breaking the tag), but for a crate that aims to be the canonical multi-language verifier, header strictness should be explicit, not incidental.

Evidence
jwe.rs:36-41 ProtectedHeaderOwned { alg, enc, kid } with default serde (ignores unknown fields); jwe.rs:100 only checks `hdr.alg != "dir" || hdr.enc != "A256GCM"`. No #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] and no crit handling.
Recommendation

Add #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to the header struct (or explicitly reject any header containing crit/zip/epk) and add a vector with an extra header parameter asserting rejection across languages.

low parse_sub runs regexes on unbounded FFI-supplied input with no length cap security Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/sub.rs:65-106 unverified
Description

parse_sub is exposed across both N-API and PyO3 and receives caller/attacker-controlled strings. The regex crate is linear (no catastrophic backtracking), so ReDoS is not the concern, but there is no max-length guard before evaluation: a multi-megabyte input runs the linear regex over the whole string and heap-allocates copies (to_string) on every auth call. A security-boundary parser exposed to FFI should cap input length defensively.

Evidence
sub.rs:65 parse_sub immediately does `s.contains(':')` then `plex_re().is_match(s)` on the full untrusted `s` with no length cap; rest.to_string()/s.to_string() allocate the full input. PLEX_REGEX `^plex:(0|[1-9][0-9]*)$` scans an arbitrarily long digit run.
Recommendation

Add an early length cap (e.g. `if s.len() > 256 { return Err(SubError::InvalidFormat) }`) before regex evaluation, plus an oversized-input vector.

low encrypt()/sign() use .expect() panics reachable from the FFI boundary production-readiness Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts/src/jwe.rs:53-69 unverified
Description

jwe::encrypt calls serde_json::to_vec(&header).expect(...) and cipher.encrypt(...).expect(...); device_token::encrypt and internal_principal::encrypt call serde_json::to_vec(claims).expect(...); stream_token::sign and compute_and_compare call Hmac::new_from_slice(...).expect(...). Most are genuinely unreachable, but all are reachable from #[napi]/#[pyfunction] exports. A panic in this context unwinds into the Node/Python runtime; napi-rs/pyo3 generally convert panics into thrown errors, but crypto paths reachable from foreign runtimes should return Result rather than panic.

Evidence
jwe.rs:53 .expect("static header serializes"); jwe.rs:69 .expect("aes-gcm encrypt with random nonce should not fail"); device_token.rs:64 .expect("DeviceClaims serializes"); stream_token.rs:147 and stream_token.rs:233 .expect("HMAC-SHA256 accepts any-length keys"). All reachable via the binding exports (e.g. device_token_encrypt -> device_token::encrypt -> jwe::encrypt).
Recommendation

Convert the encrypt/sign paths to return Result and map errors at the FFI layer; avoid .expect on any function reachable from FFI.

low device_token_decrypt rebuilds the kid->key HashMap on every call with no key-count cap performance Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto) crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs:208-222 unverified
Description

device_token_decrypt accepts a Vec<KidKey> from JS and rebuilds a HashMap on every single token verification (allocation + hashing per call) instead of reusing a cached verifier, and there is no bound on the number of keys a caller may pass. Minor at homelab scale, but the stated target is App-Store scale with many concurrent streams; per-request HashMap reconstruction on the hot device-auth path is avoidable overhead.

Evidence
napi src/lib.rs:209-218 `let mut map: HashMap<String,[u8;32]> = HashMap::new(); for kk in keys { ... map.insert(kk.kid, arr); }` executed inside the per-call device_token_decrypt export.
Recommendation

Expose a verifier-handle object (build the key map once, reuse across verifies) or document that the JS side must memoize; bound the number of kids accepted.

low Discover/Home rail hooks throw bare Errors that bypass the ApiError envelope correctness Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/hooks/useSuggested.ts:125-132 unverified
Description

useSuggested (via SuggestionsError), useTrending, useRecentlyAdded, useUserFeedback, and useLimits use inline fetch and throw plain Errors (or a custom SuggestionsError) instead of throwApiError, so the rich ApiError mapping (session-expired copy, code/details) is lost for these surfaces — the user gets a raw 'suggestions movie: 500'. With no error boundary and no cache onError, a persistent 500 on the Home rails renders whatever the consuming component does with isError. (Note: useSuggested DOES correctly disable retry on 4xx, which is good.)

Evidence
useSuggested.ts:131 `throw new SuggestionsError(r.status, body)`; useUserFeedback.ts:32 `throw new Error(\`feedback ${r.status}: ...\`)`; useTrending/useRecentlyAdded inline same pattern — unlike the iptv/media/sonarr clients which call throwApiError.
Recommendation

Route these inline fetches through the same apiFetch/throwApiError path the typed clients use so error copy and typing are consistent, and ensure each rail renders a retryable ErrorState.

low auth.test.ts covers only pure helpers + admin fetches — the security-critical React auth flows are untested testing Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/auth.test.ts:13-16 unverified
Description

The test file explicitly scopes itself to authModeFromUser, deniedMessage, and the admin invite/member fetches, noting 'The React context (signIn/appleSignIn) is exercised indirectly via the component tests.' The most security-sensitive logic — the Plex PIN poll lifecycle (deadline, popup-closed, 403-denied, interval ownership), the Apple SIWA POST branches, and the WebAuthn login/register ceremonies including @simplewebauthn/browser cancellation paths — has no direct unit coverage in this layer. These are the gate to the whole platform, so indirect-only coverage is thin.

Evidence
auth.test.ts:14-16 comment 'The React context (signIn/appleSignIn) is exercised indirectly via the component tests.'; the 8 it() blocks cover only helpers + admin API.
Recommendation

Add focused tests for signIn poll state transitions (fake timers + mocked popup), appleSignIn 403/401/ok branches, and passkeyLogin/passkeyRegister with a mocked @simplewebauthn/browser.

low navTransition home-reset effect suppresses exhaustive-deps and recreates its context value unmemoized maintainability Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth src/lib/navTransition.tsx:71-80 unverified
Description

The mount effect reads `route` to decide whether to clear the nav-transition-played gate but uses `[]` deps with an eslint-disable for react-hooks/exhaustive-deps. The intent (reset only on a real page load while on home) is documented, but suppressing the rule instead of capturing the mount-time route is fragile; additionally the provider's context value `{ transitionTo, navigate, replay }` is a fresh object every render, forcing all consumers to re-render on any provider state change. Minor, but indicative of lint being suppressed rather than satisfied.

Evidence
navTransition.tsx:79 `// eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` on a `}, [])` effect that reads `route`; context value object literal recreated each render at :128.
Recommendation

Read the route once at mount (ref or parseHash) instead of disabling the rule, and useMemo the context value.

low No global request-timeout or body-size limit middleware — inbound slow/large requests share the single Node event loop with stream proxying performance Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/app.ts:36-66 unverified
Description

The only global middleware is logger() and requireSafeOrigin. Outbound calls are well-bounded by upstream.ts, but nothing bounds INBOUND request lifetime or body size. A slow-loris client or oversized POST (invites/feedback/device-pair) is processed without a deadline on a single-process Node server that is also proxying many concurrent streams, so a pathological control-plane request can starve stream handling.

Evidence
app.ts 36 `app.use('*', logger())`; 66 `app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin)`. No bodyLimit/timeout middleware registered.
Recommendation

Add Hono bodyLimit and a per-request timeout wrapper; isolate heavy stream/remux work so control-plane requests cannot starve it and vice versa.

low Unhandled-rejection mode is 'warn' (non-fatal) with no uncaughtException integration — process can continue in an inconsistent state instead of restarting cleanly production-readiness Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness server/index.ts:43-45 unverified
Description

onUnhandledRejectionIntegration is mode:'warn', so a rejection logs and the process keeps running rather than crashing for restart:unless-stopped to recover. No uncaughtException integration and (per finding 1) no request-error capture. tracesSampleRate is unset, so no latency data for latency-sensitive streams. The fire-and-forget IPTV bootstrap/cron syncs (.catch -> console.error in iptvScheduler 21,30) are the kind of rejection this 'warn' mode silently absorbs.

Evidence
index.ts 43-45 only `Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration({ mode: 'warn' })`; iptvScheduler.ts 21 `void syncOnce(db).catch((err) => console.error(...))`, 30 same.
Recommendation

Decide the rejection policy deliberately (strict crash + unless-stopped restart, or keep warn but ensure captureException fires) and add an uncaughtException path that flushes Sentry before exit.

low ROADMAP-STATUS understates M3 client progress: a web media-core consumer now EXISTS, contradicting its 'no client consumer' claim docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty docs/ROADMAP-STATUS.md:16,72-73 unverified
Description

Rare reverse drift (under-claiming, not over-claiming): ROADMAP-STATUS says of M3 'no client consumer' (ROADMAP:16) and 'web mediaApi exposes only movies/shows/episodes/scan - no grant/stream/watch consumer' (ROADMAP:72), and the prior audit's M10 was 'Entire /api/media/* vertical has no frontend consumer'. But the repo now ships a real Media Library tab consuming media-core: src/lib/api/media.ts (BASE='/api/media'), src/lib/hooks/useMediaLibrary.ts, src/components/tabs/MediaTab.tsx, src/components/nav/TopNav.tsx (tab gated on /api/media being mounted), and src/lib/api/media.test.ts. The browse consumer exists; only the grant/stream/watch playback consumer is still missing. ROADMAP's blanket 'no client consumer' is now stale and undersells real progress - a positive-direction inaccuracy but still drift a new engineer would be confused by.

Evidence
ROADMAP-STATUS.md:16 '...no client consumer'; ROADMAP-STATUS.md:72 'web mediaApi exposes only movies/shows/episodes/scan - no grant/stream/watch consumer.' vs grep 'api/media' src/: src/lib/api/media.ts:10 'const BASE = "/api/media"', src/components/tabs/MediaTab.tsx, src/lib/hooks/useMediaLibrary.ts, src/components/nav/TopNav.tsx, src/lib/api/media.test.ts.
Recommendation

Update ROADMAP M3 to 'browse consumer shipped (Media Library tab); grant/stream/watch playback consumer still missing' so the doc credits the SPA browse integration that already exists.

low A pre-fix backup of the canonical (now RATIFIED) M1.5 contract is committed alongside the real spec docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-cross-service-contract.md.pre-fix-bak unverified
Description

git ls-files shows BOTH the canonical cross-service-contract.md (now RATIFIED/LOCKED) AND a sibling '.pre-fix-bak' copy tracked in version control. Committing a pre-fix backup of the FROZEN cross-service contract is a hygiene drift: a new engineer cannot tell which is authoritative, and a stale backup of a locked contract is exactly the file that gets read by mistake and re-introduces a reverted decision. Git history already provides the backup.

Evidence
git ls-files docs/ includes both 'docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-cross-service-contract.md' and '...md.pre-fix-bak'. The canonical file's header (line 3) reads 'RATIFIED for M2 (2026-05-28)', making the .bak redundant.
Recommendation

git rm the .pre-fix-bak file; rely on git history. If a diff must be preserved, capture it in the contract's own change log.

low README and PRODUCT.md point to a machine-local, untracked plan file as the spec for deferred scope docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty README.md:70 unverified
Description

Both README and PRODUCT.md terminate their out-of-scope sections by pointing the reader to a plan file at an absolute home path that exists only on the original author's machine and is not in the repo. For any other contributor this is a dangling reference to content they can never open - a concrete instance of docs assuming a single-author environment, which contradicts the App-Store-team trajectory.

Evidence
README.md:70 'Plan file at `~/.claude/plans/instead-could-we-robust-journal.md`.'; PRODUCT.md:54 'Listed in the plan file at `~/.claude/plans/instead-could-we-robust-journal.md`.' - absolute home path, outside the repo, untracked.
Recommendation

Remove the references or move the relevant plan content into docs/ so it is versioned and reachable by all contributors.

low Prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit fixed its code findings but never addressed the stale README/PRODUCT - the docs-honesty process has a blind spot docs-drift Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty docs/AUDIT-2026-05-28-honesty-and-best-practice.md:176-197 unverified
Description

The 2026-05-28 audit's CODE findings were genuinely remediated (section-7 remediation log with commit hashes; corroborated this run: media-core SPA consumer now exists; contract now RATIFIED; Sentry wired) - a strength. But the audit scoped itself to code/test/security claims and never flagged the stale README or PRODUCT.md, both of which remain V1 today. So the honesty discipline the team demonstrably applies to code/test claims has NOT been extended to the product narrative - the largest doc-drift in the repo went unexamined by the very process meant to catch dishonest docs.

Evidence
AUDIT-2026-05-28:13 'the status reporting is not honest'; AUDIT section 7 (lines 180-192) lists fmt/backup-gate/TMDB/IDOR/path-traversal/transcode-503 fixes - NO README/PRODUCT entry; README.md and PRODUCT.md remain V1 (full reads). Corroborated fix: src/lib/api/media.ts exists (audit M10); contract header now RATIFIED (audit still-open item line 195).
Recommendation

Add product-doc accuracy (README/PRODUCT/DESIGN) to the next audit pass and to a lightweight docs-lint checklist; this is the one place the otherwise-strong honesty process has a gap.

low version.ts registers a /health route AFTER 'export default app' - dead/fragile code in the contract-governed file maintainability Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty server/routes/version.ts:33 unverified
Description

Minor code-cleanliness drift in the file that implements the M1.5 sec-7/sec-12.3 /api/version contract. The module ends at the version.get('/') handler closing on line 33; the /api/version body it returns is correct (server_id, release, auth_modes, accepting_device_pairs) and accurately omits the sec-7.2 schemas block ROADMAP flags as unwired. The export naming (named export 'version' rather than the default-export pattern other route modules use) is consistent here, so this is a low-severity note: confirm route-mount conventions are uniform across server/routes so the /api/version contract surface stays unambiguous for M2 client integration. (No behavioral defect observed in version.ts itself.)

Evidence
server/routes/version.ts:15 'export const version = new Hono()' then version.get('/') closing at line 33; returns { server_id, release, auth_modes, accepting_device_pairs } with no schemas field (matches ROADMAP:46 honest flag).
Recommendation

No action required for correctness; optionally add the sec-7.2 schemas:{iptv,exchange,media} block to close the M1.5 REMAINING item ROADMAP already documents, before M2 Apple clients consume /api/version.

low Secret-strength + placeholder validation is production-only; dev/staging accept trivially weak cookie-encryption keys security Cross-Cutting: Security server/services/secrets.ts + server/env.ts:secrets.ts:38-39; env.ts:176,229-234,243-248 unverified
Description

validateSecretStrength returns immediately when !isProd (secrets.ts:38-39); the SESSION_SECRET length/placeholder block is gated `if (isProd && rawSessionSecret)` (env.ts:176); DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET/INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET default to '' in non-prod (env.ts:231,245). A NODE_ENV!=production box that becomes network-reachable (staging, a dev tunnel) boots with a 1-char or 'test' SESSION_SECRET deriving the A256GCM key protecting the Plex token in every cookie. assertSecretsDistinct runs everywhere (catches reuse) but not weakness.

Evidence
secrets.ts:38-39 `): void { if (!isProd) return`. env.ts:176 `if (isProd && rawSessionSecret) {`. env.ts:231 `: process.env.DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET || ''`.
Recommendation

Apply a minimal length floor + placeholder rejection to SESSION_SECRET and STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET in ALL environments (the distinctness check already runs everywhere). Keep the strict 32-byte rule prod-only if desired, but never accept test/changeme as a cookie-encryption key on any network-exposable instance.

low Non-constant-time secret comparison on the recommender-export endpoint security Cross-Cutting: Security server/routes/iptv.ts:1067-1069 unverified
Description

/api/iptv/export/recommender authenticates a caller header with plain `!==` against IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET (iptv.ts:1068). String !== short-circuits on the first differing byte, a timing oracle that can recover the secret byte-by-byte over many requests. It fails closed when the secret is unset (403) and exposes only the local VOD/series catalog (no PII/creds), bounding impact — but it is a network-reachable route gated solely by this secret, inconsistent with the project's otherwise-careful constant-time posture (the stream-token crate explicitly avoids timing leaks).

Evidence
iptv.ts:1067-1069 `const secret = c.req.header('x-iptv-export-secret') ?? ''; if (!env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET || secret !== env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET) { return c.json({ error: 'forbidden' }, 403) }`.
Recommendation

Compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual over equal-length buffers (hash both sides first). Audit other header/secret equality checks for the same pattern.

low CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' on Netlify, and nginx serves no CSP at all — weakened XSS defense-in-depth security Cross-Cutting: Security netlify.toml + nginx/default.conf:netlify.toml CSP line; nginx/default.conf:18-27 unverified
Description

The Netlify CSP includes script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. No XSS sink exists in src/ today (no dangerouslySetInnerHTML/innerHTML/eval), but 'unsafe-inline' on script-src removes CSP's value as a second line of defense if a future injection lands. Separately, nginx/default.conf serves the SPA with NO Content-Security-Policy header at all (it sets only SPA caching directives at lines 18-27; the security headers I credited are conditionally present depending on the build — verify), so the NAS-served SPA path has weaker headers than the Netlify-served path. (NOTE: I observed the security-header set on the Netlify side and SPA caching on the nginx side; confirm nginx also emits nosniff/X-Frame-Options in the deployed config — the file as read shows only cache headers.)

Evidence
netlify.toml carries a Content-Security-Policy with 'unsafe-inline' in script-src (per the redirects/headers block). nginx/default.conf:14-27 shows only `try_files` + Cache-Control headers; no add_header for CSP/X-Frame-Options/nosniff in the location blocks read.
Recommendation

Drop 'unsafe-inline' from script-src (Vite output is hashable; use hashes/nonces for any inline script). Add a matching CSP + nosniff + X-Frame-Options DENY + Referrer-Policy to nginx/default.conf so the NAS-served path matches the Netlify path. Confirm both edges emit identical security headers.

low IPTV sync / reconcile failure modes are under-tested relative to their blast radius testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor server/services/iptvSync.test.ts:n/a unverified
Description

iptvSync.test.ts has only 2 tests, iptvEpg 2, iptvCatalog 4, despite IPTV sync being a large, network-dependent, partial-failure-prone subsystem (Xtream upstream timeouts, malformed M3U/EPG, mid-sync abort, dedup against the existing lineup). The reconcile logic that decides what channels appear is comparatively lightly covered. By contrast iptvStreamToken (26) and iptv routes (28) are well covered — so the token/security surface is solid but the resilience-of-sync surface is thin. A partial sync that drops half the lineup or duplicates channels would likely not be caught.

Evidence
Per-file test counts: iptvSync 2, iptvEpg 2, iptvCatalog 4 vs iptvStreamToken 26, iptv routes 28.
Recommendation

Add sync failure-mode tests: upstream 5xx/timeout mid-sync (assert no partial-clobber of the existing lineup), malformed M3U/EPG (assert graceful skip + telemetry), and idempotent re-sync (assert no duplicate channels).

low Apple Sign-In and passkey flows are unit-tested in pieces but never tested end-to-end through the assembled auth surface testing Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor server/services/appleAuth.test.ts:n/a unverified
Description

appleAuth.test.ts (19), the passkey route (9), sessionGate (19), and members (14) each cover their slice with mocks, and the SPA has AppleSignInButton/appleSdk tests. But because the Playwright layer mocks all APIs, no test drives a full Apple-token -> server-verify -> session-cookie -> member-gate -> authorized-request chain, nor the passkey register/authenticate round-trip against the real WebAuthn server verifier. The pieces are green; the assembled flow (the thing that actually logs a user in and admits them past the invite gate) is never exercised together. Given §4 calls the gate a one-way door, the absence of an assembled-flow test is a real risk.

Evidence
appleAuth.test.ts 19, passkey route 9, sessionGate 19, members 14 — all isolated/mocked; tests/e2e/auth.spec.ts runs against mockApi.ts, not the real verifier; no test composes token-verify + session + member-gate.
Recommendation

Add an integration test (vitest, real Hono app + in-memory SQLite, mocking only Apple's public-key endpoint) that runs the full Apple and passkey flows through to an authorized request and a denied non-member, so the assembled gate is regression-protected.

info featurize genre/keyword perturbation uses additive +/-1 hash bumps without re-checking the post-add norm before normalize correctness Recommender — Python FastAPI recommender/workers/featurize.py:42-69, 147 (_normalize(txt_emb + g_pert + k_pert)) unverified
Description

Minor numerical note, not a live bug given current inputs: the feature vector is _normalize(txt_emb + GENRE_WEIGHT*g_pert + KEYWORD_WEIGHT*k_pert). txt_emb is L2-normalized by sentence-transformers, so the sum's norm is ~>=1 minus pathological cancellation and _normalize guards n>0, so a zero vector is extremely unlikely. But the additive perturbation is applied AFTER text normalization and only the final vector is renormalized, so the genre/keyword contribution's effective weight varies with the text-embedding magnitude rather than being a fixed fraction. Combined with MMR's separate norm filtering downstream (which would drop a degenerate zero-norm row), correctness holds, but the perturbation weighting is not as principled as the GENRE_WEIGHT/KEYWORD_WEIGHT constants imply.

Evidence
featurize.py 147: 'vec = _normalize(txt_emb + g_pert + k_pert)' where g_pert/k_pert are sums of +/-1 bumps scaled by 0.30/0.15; _normalize returns v unchanged when norm==0.
Recommendation

If genre/keyword weighting is meant to be a fixed fraction of the final vector, normalize txt_emb and the perturbation separately and blend with explicit weights, then renormalize. Low priority — document the current behavior as intended if so.

info ConcurrencyLimitModal/ConnectionsWidget error-on-upstream handling could not be fully verified (read interrupted) production-readiness Web SPA — Feature Components & Player src/components/iptv/ConnectionsWidget.tsx:whole file unverified
Description

The connections widget and concurrency modal are confirmed to exist and are wired into IptvTab and the play flows (the concurrency retry/stash logic in LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab is verified). However, the internal error/unknown-state handling of ConnectionsWidget (what it renders when the Xtream upstream is unreachable vs reports 0 active) could not be read in full because the harness tool-output channel degraded during the follow-up reads. Flagged as info, not a confirmed defect: a concurrency gate that silently reads upstream failure as '0 active' would mislead, so this is worth confirming.

Evidence
Verified wiring: LiveTab.tsx:80-97 catches concurrencyPayloadFromError and shows ConcurrencyLimitModal; IptvTab.tsx:33 renders <ConnectionsWidget/>. The widget's own error branch was not readable in this session.
Recommendation

Confirm ConnectionsWidget renders an explicit 'connections unknown' state on upstream failure rather than 0, so the count is never silently misleading.

info Default Hono logger logs full request paths including stream-grant t= tokens — an unscrubbed side channel parallel to the Sentry scrub security Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing server/app.ts:36 unverified
Description

app.use('*', logger()) is Hono's default logger, which prints method + full path (including query string). IPTV/stream proxy URLs carry t=<token> query params — exactly the value telemetryPiiScrub strips from Sentry. Those land in container stdout/access logs unredacted, which on a NAS may be shipped to log aggregation. The PII scrub effort is thorough for Sentry but the plain access log is an unscrubbed channel for the same token class, making the two policies inconsistent.

Evidence
app.ts:36 `app.use('*', logger())` (Hono default logs the full request path with query). telemetryPiiScrub.ts:129 treats `t=<token>` as sensitive.
Recommendation

Replace the default logger with a custom one that strips the query string (or just the t= param) from logged paths, or disable path logging on the /api/iptv proxy routes, to keep the access log consistent with the Sentry scrub policy.

info Plex membership is no longer the login authZ gate; revocation timing now depends on sessionGate's fail-open probe + allowlist-row removal (Plex-parity de-provisioning gap) security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/services/sessionGate.ts:149-207 unverified
Description

By design (auth.ts:306 'Discovery aid only — no longer an authZ gate'), PLEX_SERVER_ID no longer gates the COOKIE login; the members allowlist does. (Note the inconsistency: the DEVICE-pair path device.ts:135-142 STILL enforces live Plex-server membership at mint time — so the cookie and device login paths apply different gates.) sessionGate demotes the plex.tv probe to advisory: a probe 'not_member' or outage NEVER overrides a present members row (sessionGate.ts:176-205); only a definitive 401/403 drops the session. Consequence: a member removed from the Plex SHARE but still holding an allowlist row keeps dashboard access until an admin revokes the row — unlike Plex, which cuts access immediately on un-share. env.ts:116-118 still describes PLEX_SERVER_ID as 'scoping sign-ins', now only true via this advisory loop.

Evidence
auth.ts:306 `// Discovery aid only (no longer an authZ gate)`; sessionGate.ts:154-157 'a plex.tv not_member or outage must NEVER override a present members row'; device.ts:135-142 still hard-checks server membership; env.ts:116-118 still says PLEX_SERVER_ID scopes sign-ins.
Recommendation

Confirm intent and reconcile the two login paths (cookie = allowlist-only, device = Plex-membership-required is inconsistent). Update env.ts's PLEX_SERVER_ID rationale. If Plex-parity auto-de-provisioning is desired, treat a sustained probe 'not_member' as grounds to auto-revoke the row.

info memberStatus un-bootstrapped fall-through fails OPEN on a fresh install; the gate does an uncaught per-request DB read security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/services/membership.ts:46-95 unverified
Description

On a fresh install with NO gate configured (no PLEX_SERVER_ID, no ADMIN_SUBS, Apple unconfigured) AND zero members rows, memberStatus returns 'allowed' for any verified identity (membership.ts:77-78) so the operator isn't locked out — a sound 'one-way door' (any gate or first row closes it permanently). But the bootstrap check runs `SELECT 1 FROM members LIMIT 1` on every memberStatus call reaching the fall-through, with no try/catch — if serverDb() were unavailable here it would throw (becoming a silent Hono 500, see the onError finding) rather than fail closed. The defensive `env.adminSubs ?? []` at membership.ts:53 hints env can be partial in some path.

Evidence
membership.ts:77-78 `if (isAuthzBootstrapped()) return 'not_member'; return 'allowed'`; membership.ts:91-94 `SELECT 1 FROM members LIMIT 1`; membership.ts:53 `(env.adminSubs ?? [])`.
Recommendation

Set ADMIN_SUBS (owner bootstrap) on every deploy so the fall-through never fires (the MEMORY note about plex:494190801 confirms this is intended). Cache isAuthzBootstrapped()=true once true (one-way door) to drop the per-request DB read, and wrap it so a DB error fails closed.

info Stream-token replay cache and Apple JWKS cache are per-process and empty after restart — short-TTL replay defense has a restart gap security Server — Auth & AuthZ server/services/tokenReplayCache.ts:21-30, 51 unverified
Description

tokenReplayCache is correctly wired into routes/iptv.ts (live/remux/segment), but it is an in-process Map cleared on restart (acknowledged in the module comment). For 60s segment tokens a restart opens a brief window where a previously-presented segment jti is forgotten and could be replayed once. Same per-process limitation as the auth rate limiter — acceptable for one NAS, a gap at multi-replica scale (a segment token presented to replica A is replayable on replica B). Flagged for the App-Store-scale goal, not the current single-NAS deploy.

Evidence
tokenReplayCache.ts:25-26 'On process restart the cache is empty; this is accepted for short-TTL segment tokens (60s)'; tokenReplayCache.ts:51 `const cache = new Map<...>()`.
Recommendation

For multi-replica scale, back the replay cache with a shared store (Redis with per-jti TTL) so single-use is enforced across instances and restarts. No change needed for single-NAS.

info Non-spec JWE tag layout and residual unread route bodies — follow-up verification needed security Cross-Cutting: Security crates/emerald-contracts/src/jwe.rs + multiple route files:jwe.rs:7-11,77-87; see description unverified
Description

Two items for follow-up. (1) jwe.rs produces the 5-segment compact form b64u(header).b64u('').b64u(iv).b64u(ciphertext).b64u(tag) — this IS RFC-7516 compliant (it splits aes-gcm's combined output into ciphertext+tag, jwe.rs:71-86), and AAD-binds the header, so it is safe between this crate's own ends; no action needed unless an external/RFC-strict JWE verifier is ever introduced, in which case re-verify interop. (2) Some route bodies were not read in full and could add per-route authZ/IDOR findings: the complete media-core series/episode handlers and transcoder routes.rs/session.rs authZ; every server/routes/*.ts not opened (radarr, sab, users, devices, adminInvites, plex-admin, plex-links, grabs, feedback, suggestions, usage, notifications); recommender main.py for which event routes actually mount the (off-by-default) principal dep. Representative samples that WERE read were sound (sonarr: list/lookup open, add permission-rewrites non-admin bodies, delete+season-monitor requireAdmin with safe-integer id gates — sonarr.ts:16,488-519,743,836).

Evidence
jwe.rs:71-86 splits ciphertext||tag correctly into separate segments (this is RFC-compliant, contrary to a non-standard-layout concern). sonarr.ts:16 `sonarr.use('*', requireAuth)`, :502 `const adminSuppliedPolicy = session.role === 'admin' && rawBody.rootFolderPath !== undefined`, :743/:836 requireAdmin gates.
Recommendation

In a focused follow-up, read every server/routes/*.ts and the full media-core/transcoder route tables to confirm per-route authZ/IDOR and that the recommender's principal dep is mounted on each event route before the off->enforce cutover. No JWE change required for internal-only use; document an interop check if a third-party JWE verifier is ever added.

Subsystem Dossiers

Recommender — Python FastAPI

functional

A FastAPI sidecar that replaces per-request Claude calls with a local SQLite + sqlite-vec content-based scoring pipeline. /score does KNN retrieval over title embeddings re-ranked by pluggable recipes (baseline_cosine, cold_start_trending, mmr_diverse); event-ingest endpoints (feedback, shown, impressions, rejection, library/sync, clears) feed an outcome-attribution log. A nightly optimizer makes exactly one Claude call per run to propose a config patch, clamps numeric changes to +/-20% drift, and auto-promotes only if a held-out eval beats baseline by epsilon. TMDB workers (tmdb_ingest/tmdb_client/featurize) populate the catalog and embeddings. Auth is a shared HMAC event-secret plus an optional internal-principal JWE (contract 4). Hono is the sole caller; the service is Docker-network-internal with no public tunnel.

Maturity: Every file is well above prototype quality (parameterized SQL, WAL per-request connections, IMMEDIATE transactions, finite/NaN param guards, MMR with explicit non-finite filtering, tenacity retry + sliding-window rate limit on TMDB, resumable ingest queue with lock-backoff, clamp+eval+epsilon promotion gate). It is NOT production-grade because the auto-promotion eval gate is a no-op without an operator-curated holdout that the deploy path deliberately omits, internal-principal auth defaults to mode=off, the optimizer feeds untrusted TMDB title text into a Claude prompt with no neutralization, and there is no rate limit / request timeout / concurrency cap on /score.

33 files reviewed · 11 findings
What works
  • Per-request SQLite connections (main.py get_db 96-109) avoid cross-thread cursor races; connect() sets WAL + busy_timeout=5000 + foreign_keys=ON + mmap + temp_store=MEMORY (db.py 283-299) — a sound concurrency posture for FastAPI's sync-handler threadpool.
  • All SQL is parameterized; the only dynamic SQL is IN-clause '?'.join placeholder generation (context.py, retrieval.py, main.py clear_feedback). sub is additionally regex-validated via the Rust contract binding before any query (schemas.py 36 -> sub_validation.py). The one f-string LIMIT in featurize._load_pending wraps int(limit) so it is not injectable.
  • Transactions are explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE with rollback-on-exception (db.py 302-313), used consistently across every write path.
  • Model-param sanitization is genuinely defensive: rejects bool-as-int, requires math.isfinite, clamps to MODEL_PARAM_BOUNDS, falls back to default recipe on bad JSON / wrong type / unknown recipe (context.py 385-472). NaN/inf params cannot reach a recipe.
  • Recipe math handles the degenerate cases: empty candidate pool returns RecipeResult([]) (baseline_cosine 72, mmr_diverse 115); cosine sim from sqlite-vec distance is computed safely; pop_max uses 'or 1.0' to avoid div-by-zero; _mmr explicitly filters non-finite embeddings and zero-norm rows (mmr_diverse 50) before normalizing and returns [] if none valid.
  • neighbors_for and _stack_embeddings defend the embedding/id alignment bug class with strict=True zips and embedding-aligned id lists, with comments documenting why sorted(ids)+zip would misalign (context.py 186-208, reasons.py 57-91).
  • vec rowid kind-collision (movie vs tv sharing a tmdb_id) is handled by encoding kind into bit 32, with a one-shot rebuild migration and decode-time kind-mismatch detection (db.py 337-358, 483-510).
  • TMDB client is resilient: tenacity AsyncRetrying with stop_after_attempt(5) + exponential backoff retrying only 429/5xx/RequestError (tmdb_client 44-50, 118-126); sliding-window rate limiter; api_key redaction in error messages (_redacted_url, _sanitize_http_status_error).
  • tmdb_ingest is operationally robust: resumable ingest_queue, _requeue_errors with a max-attempts cap to stop permanently-broken rows from churning, database-locked detection with exponential backoff and a hard stop after MAX_LOCKED_NO_PROGRESS_BATCHES, and asyncio.gather(return_exceptions=True) so one worker crash doesn't abort the batch.
  • Optimizer promotion is gated: numeric drift clamped to +/-20% with careful zero-base handling (clamp_patch), a >10% holdout-failure-rate guard (evaluate_pair), a baseline+epsilon improvement requirement, and a post-promote invariant assert that exactly one active row with the new version exists (promote()).
  • load_holdout() validates every JSONL line (must be dict, non-empty str sub, kind in {movie,tv}, library/positives/negatives int-only rejecting bool, non-empty positives) so one malformed line cannot poison the eval run.
  • Event-secret check uses hmac.compare_digest and fails closed (503) when unconfigured (main.py 112-119); config.py enforces placeholder + min-length checks for both secrets in NODE_ENV=production.
  • Migrator is careful: checksum verification of applied migrations, legacy schema_migrations table reshape, a DROP TABLE guard requiring an explicit '-- DESTRUCTIVE' annotation + a fresh backup gate, and statement-by-statement completion parsing.
  • Retention is actively swept (background task + inline deletes); rec_log rows with outcomes are preserved from the sweep so training signal survives (main.py 47-71). Batch endpoints are size-capped (200 -> 413; 5000 max_length on list fields).
  • Dockerfile builds and installs the emerald_contracts PyO3 wheel that sub_validation and internal_principal depend on, runs as a non-root uid 10001, installs CPU-only torch to avoid the CUDA payload, and has a /health HEALTHCHECK.
Plex parity notes

Plex has no self-hosted equivalent to this service — Plex Discover/recommendations are centralized Plex-cloud features, whereas this is a per-deployment, content-based recommender with a local embedding store and a privacy-preserving design (all traffic Docker-internal, ONE Claude call per night rather than per request, no third-party data processor for scoring). For a Plex REPLACEMENT this is a genuine differentiator and is real, working code: sqlite-vec KNN retrieval, household-aware exclusion (library/rejections/recently-shown/dislikes), cold-start handling, MMR diversity, templated no-LLM reason pills, and a self-tuning loop all exist and are sound. Parity gaps for a Netflix-grade product: (1) the learning loop is effectively inert out of the box — no holdout shipped (finding #1) and the optimizer needs >=50 daily attributed outcomes which the 10-minute attribution window (finding #6) makes hard to reach on a small household — so day-1 recommendation quality rests entirely on the fixed recipe defaults, not on adaptation; (2) recommendation QUALITY rides on a single 384-dim MiniLM overview embedding plus small hashed genre/keyword perturbations — adequate for content-based 'more like this' but weaker than collaborative signals Netflix/Plex-scale systems use, and there is no online quality metric or A/B beyond the offline holdout; (3) only movie/tv kinds are supported — IPTV kinds were added then dropped (migrations 0005/0007), so live-TV/IPTV discovery is explicitly out of scope for the recommender. The assignment's 'matcher takes results.first() with no confidence/lang filter' concern is a media-core/M3 matching issue, not this service; within the recommender the analogous concern is that retrieve_candidates applies no original_language filter and the personalized_threshold (tau=0.45) only relabels provenance rather than dropping low-confidence picks — a low-similarity item still ships labeled 'discover', which is a deliberate fill behavior but means the recommender will surface weak matches rather than return fewer-but-confident results.

Crate — transcoder (M4, the long pole)

prototype

A standalone Rust/axum HTTP service intended to be the platform's on-the-fly media transcoder: probe a source file + the client's declared playback capabilities, plan the smallest per-stream transform (copy when possible; re-encode video to H.264 with optional downscale / HDR->SDR tonemap; re-encode audio to Apple-safe stereo AAC), spawn a managed ffmpeg HLS session, and reap stale/idle sessions. This is the Plex-equivalent "Convert and stream automatically" engine that makes arbitrary library files playable on iOS/tvOS/Apple App Store clients.

Maturity: The library compiles and the planner/arg logic is clean, but the test suite does not compile (plan.rs calls nonexistent EncoderRegistry::from_names), the Docker image cannot build (workspace members not COPY'd), no code anywhere calls the service (orphan), and the ffmpeg child's piped stderr is never drained — guaranteeing a deadlock the first time a real transcode runs longer than a few seconds. "No real transcode has ever run" is accurate, and several latent bugs would surface immediately if one did.

14 files reviewed · 16 findings
What works
  • Clean module separation (plan/args/encoders/session/concurrency/routes) makes arg assembly unit-testable without spawning ffmpeg — the right architecture even if the tests are currently broken.
  • ffmpeg is invoked via Command::new("ffmpeg").args(&args) with argv (no shell), so the source_path -i input is not a classic shell command-injection vector (the filtergraph subtitle path is a separate, currently-dead concern).
  • The copy-first planner is correct in spirit: video is copied unless codec/resolution/HDR force a re-encode (plan.rs:111), minimizing CPU — the core Plex-parity instinct.
  • scaled_dimensions preserves aspect ratio and forces even dimensions via `& !1` (plan.rs:181), which is genuinely required by H.264 encoders.
  • Concurrency limiter is a correct non-blocking try_acquire_owned semaphore that maps cleanly to HTTP 503 (concurrency.rs:27, routes.rs:57-60), and the permit is held for the session lifetime via _permit in SessionRecord (session.rs:42) so it releases on stop/reap.
  • Encoder probe degrades safely: if `ffmpeg -encoders` fails, the registry still reports the libx264 software baseline so planning never panics (encoders.rs:44-46).
  • Dockerfile runtime stage runs as a non-root uid 10001 user and the media mount in compose is read-only (:ro), limiting blast radius (Dockerfile:27-28, docker-compose.yml:53).
  • Heartbeat-based reaper design is sound in concept: 30s timeout, 10s scan interval, kill + cleanup (session.rs:23-25, 126-148).
Plex parity notes

This crate is the Plex "transcode-on-the-fly" engine and is currently the weakest pillar of the Plex-replacement story. (1) End-to-end playback does not exist: the advertised playlist_url 404s and segment serving is explicitly unimplemented (routes.rs:121), so there is no Plex-equivalent "press play, get a stream" path. (2) Multi-track audio, surround passthrough, and selectable/forced subtitles — table stakes for Plex — are absent: a single scalar audio stream, forced stereo AAC downmix, no -map, and dead subtitle burn-in (plan.rs:129-142, 151; args.rs:101). (3) HDR->SDR tonemapping is implemented as a filtergraph string but has never run and silently loses to the double-vf bug if subs are ever enabled (args.rs:60-64, 67-103). (4) Hardware acceleration (VideoToolbox/NVENC/VAAPI/QSV) is detected by substring only and never validated — Plex gates HW transcoding on actual device capability; here a GPU-less host can pick a HW encoder that dies at spawn (encoders.rs:54-77). (5) Adaptive bitrate / multi-rendition HLS ladders (Plex's bandwidth adaptation) are not modeled at all — single rendition, bitrate_kbps always None. (6) No seek support despite the seek=kill+respawn comment: start_seconds is hardcoded None and there is no seek route (plan.rs:79,155; args.rs:22). Net: the transcoder is a prototype skeleton with correct instincts but no proven transcode, no auth, an unbuildable image, broken tests, and a guaranteed stderr-deadlock — it is far from Plex-equivalent and is the literal long pole the roadmap calls it.

Web SPA — Feature Components & Player

functional

Core feature UI of the React 19 + Vite + TS SPA: the IPTV player (hls.js/mpegts.js/native-HLS lifecycle, buffer tuning, error recovery, audio/subtitle track switching, unmount cleanup, resume seeking + position reporting), the search-as-you-type stack (SearchInput, ResultGrid, MediaCard, AiToggle, ModeToggle, FeedbackDots, TrendingRow), library browsing (LibraryAlphabet A-Z bucketing, LibraryFilters sort/status), the nine content tabs (Home/Live/Vod/Movies/Tv/Media/Downloads/Users/IptvSeries + IptvTab shell + TabPlaceholder), the detail modal, add-movie/add-series modals, media SourceToggle, queue rows, downloads panels (grab activity + AI usage), and IPTV concurrency-limit handling. Assessed for XSS, accessibility, loading/error/empty states, list performance at scale, and which tabs are real vs placeholder.

Maturity: All nine content tabs render real query-backed data (TabPlaceholder is dead/unused), the player has genuine 3-engine playback with deliberate buffer tuning and bounded recovery, search-as-you-type is debounced, and detail/add modals correctly use the native <dialog> element with Escape + focus handling; but it falls short of production-grade due to a player listener-churn loop on every render, unbounded recovery counters, unvirtualized library grids, and IPTV-specific modals that lack focus traps.

57 files reviewed · 12 findings
What works
  • No XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML anywhere in src/ — a full repo-wide grep returned zero hits. All dynamic text (titles, overviews, EPG programme names, usernames) renders as JSX text nodes and image URLs go through src attributes, so React's default escaping covers the render path. Cast links use rel="noopener noreferrer" and posters use referrerPolicy="no-referrer".
  • IptvPlayer.tsx branches correctly across three engines: native HLS via canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl') for Safari/iOS, hls.js for desktop MSE, and mpegts.js for live MPEG-TS — all behind dynamic import() so the heavy media libs are code-split out of the initial bundle (IptvPlayer.tsx:200-313).
  • Player buffer tuning is deliberate and well-reasoned for the proxy->cloudflared->edge transport, with extensive comments documenting real debugging: hls.js uses lowLatencyMode:false, liveSyncDurationCount:4, maxBufferLength:30, fragLoadingMaxRetry:8 (lines 221-232); mpegts.js enables worker demux, a 1MB stash buffer, liveBufferLatencyChasing, autoCleanupSourceBuffer to bound long-session memory, and fixAudioTimestampGap (lines 295-313). The latest commit 44ff715 is exactly this fix.
  • The engine-setup effect cleanup is correct in shape: sets cancelled=true, invokes cleanupEngine (hls.destroy() or mpegts unload+detach+destroy), nulls hlsRef, and calls resetVideo() which pauses, removes src, and reloads the element — the right teardown for MSE/worker resources on unmount and grant change (IptvPlayer.tsx:341-358).
  • DetailModal uses the native <dialog> element with showModal(), an onCancel handler that intercepts Escape, focus moved to the close button on open, and aria-labelledby/aria-describedby — a genuinely accessible modal, not a div-overlay hack (DetailModal.tsx:128-157). AddMovieModal and AddSeriesModal also use native <dialog> and guard onCancel against closing while a mutation is in flight (AddSeriesModal.tsx:202-206).
  • Search-as-you-type is debounced everywhere via the shared useDebounced hook: Movies/TV/Media at 300ms, IPTV Live/Vod/Series at 250ms, with TanStack Query keying providing request dedup/cancellation (TvTab.tsx:106, VodTab.tsx:45). My initial concern about un-debounced search is disproven by the container code.
  • Loading, error, and empty states are consistently handled.
