TrendingRow is exemplary: it has a dedicated describeError() that maps 401/402/429/5xx to actionable copy, a dominantDropReason() that explains WHY a suggestion strip is empty, and distinct skeleton/loading/degraded-source hints (TrendingRow.tsx:58-149). MoviesTab/TvTab DiscoverResults and LibraryResults branch loading/error/empty explicitly with tailored messages.
  • IPTV concurrency limiting (ConnectionsWidget + ConcurrencyLimitModal + concurrencyLimit.ts) is a real Plex-style 'active streams / max connections' gate: a failed grant due to the limit is caught, the modal is shown, and the original play attempt is stashed in pendingPlay and retried after the user kicks a session (LiveTab.tsx:80-97, 252-266).
  • Keyboard accessibility on card grids is implemented: IPTV channel/VOD/series cards are role="button" tabIndex={0} with onKeyDown handlers honoring Enter/Space and event.target===currentTarget guards so the favorite/guide sub-buttons don't double-fire (LiveTab.tsx:99-104, 119-126). FeedbackDots, AiToggle, ModeToggle, SourceToggle, and LibraryAlphabet all carry correct ARIA (role=switch/tab/tablist, aria-checked/aria-selected/aria-pressed) and focus-visible outlines.
  • DownloadsTab is a substantial, real component: it joins SAB queue + Sonarr/Radarr queues + library season stats to compute season/episode size math, shows an indeterminate 'indexer searching' shuttle when SAB is idle but upstream queues are pending, gates pause/resume/cancel on isAdmin and per-item isPending, and routes destructive cancels through the ConfirmProvider (DownloadsTab.tsx:108-453).
  • Components are unit-tested where it matters: SourceToggle.test.tsx exercises ARIA + onClick by walking the element tree (no jsdom), and IptvPlayer.test.tsx asserts the video element renders for both progressive and HLS grants.
  • VOD/series resume bars and completion tracking are real: resumePercent/resumePosition compute a progress bar from history, startPositionSecs is passed to the player, and onPositionUpdate marks completed when within 30s of the end (VodTab.tsx:22-31,180-183).
Plex parity notes

Player engine coverage is genuinely strong for a Plex replacement: native HLS (iOS/Safari), hls.js (desktop MSE), and mpegts.js (live MPEG-TS) with real buffer tuning and bounded recovery — the hardest part, and it is functional, not stubbed. The IPTV surface (Live channels with EPG now/next, channel guide with catchup/archive, VOD with resume bars, series with seasons/episodes, favorites, M3U export, and a Plex-style active-streams concurrency gate) is broad and real. The Sonarr/Radarr discover+library side has Plex-grade detail (A-Z bucketing with article stripping, sort/status filters, season disclosure, cast from TMDB, ratings, 'Play in Plex' deep links). Parity gaps: (1) continue-watching/resume is fragile on the HLS-VOD transport where Plex shines (best-effort seek, no verification); (2) Movies/TV library grids are unvirtualized, so they will not browse a large library as smoothly as Plex; (3) the live-TV viewing modals lack the focus-trap/Escape accessibility that DetailModal already gets right, and the player shows no buffering/connecting/retry state (Plex always surfaces connection status); (4) no in-app playback of the Radarr/Sonarr local library — those route out to Plex via deep link, so 'Play in Plex' is a bridge, not a native player, for that content. All nine content tabs are real (TabPlaceholder is unused dead code), so feature breadth toward parity is present; the remaining work is scale-hardening, resume fidelity, player-state UX, and accessibility consistency. NOTE: all 52 in-scope component files plus the consuming tab containers, App.tsx, and package.json were read in full; three iptv/ helper files were only partially re-read because the harness tool-output channel intermittently returned empty during follow-up reads (the same transient issue seen at session start) — the one finding depending on that unread detail is marked info/unverified.

Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing

functional

App bootstrap and the shared security/data plumbing of the Hono backend: app.ts (route mounting, middleware order, CORS, body limits, global error handling), index.ts (Sentry init, ffmpeg/boot guards, listener, SIGTERM/SIGINT shutdown), the better-sqlite3 layer (db.ts pragmas/WAL/migration runner, serverDb.ts singleton + server_id), the migrator (migrator.ts sha256 drift guard + DESTRUCTIVE backup gate), the outbound fetch wrapper (upstream.ts), the SSRF guard, sanitize, telemetry PII scrub, ulid, the §4 internal-principal JWE minter, and the telemetry + version routes.

Maturity: The plumbing boots, opens WAL DBs, runs transactional migrations, mounts routes with real CORS/CSRF/timeout/PII-scrub code, and the §4 JWE boundary has a genuine N-API->PyO3 cross-binding CI gate. But the hardened migrator (sha256 drift guard + DESTRUCTIVE backup gate in migrator.ts) is DEAD CODE — the live DBs run db.ts's separate runner that stores empty checksums and skips every guard — and the SSRF guard is string-only (DNS-rebinding/redirect bypassable). Solid bones, two material safety gaps inside an otherwise functional layer.

22 files reviewed · 11 findings
What works
  • WAL + busy_timeout(5000) + synchronous=NORMAL + foreign_keys=ON are set on every DB at open (db.ts:19-22) — the correct pragma set for a single-process better-sqlite3 (synchronous, in-process) app on a NAS.
  • ulid.ts is genuinely correct: CSPRNG via node:crypto randomBytes, byte & 31 over a 32-symbol Crockford alphabet is provably unbiased (256 is an exact multiple of 32), and the alphabet matches the contract's /^[0-9A-HJKMNP-TV-Z]{26}$/ class. The header's unbias claim holds.
  • upstream.ts wraps fetch with an AbortController timeout, ALWAYS clears the timer in finally, and synthesizes a 504 on abort/network error so route handlers never face an unhandled rejection (upstream.ts:30-62); it also drains the body and correctly nulls it for 101/204/205/304 (lines 35-38).
  • telemetryPiiScrub.ts is the strongest module in scope: non-mutating recursive walk, case-insensitive substring key denylist that folds in Sentry's full default denylist as defense-in-depth, regex strip of stream-grant t=<token> and JWE eyJ... ciphertext in BOTH events and breadcrumb data.url (the Mozilla 2021 token-in-breadcrumb bug class), Authorization/Cookie header scrubbing, and a setSentryUser() wrapper that THROWS on any non-anonymous id/username to enforce the App Store linked=No label at dev time.
  • Telemetry is actually wired, not just declared: index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init with beforeSend=piiScrub and beforeBreadcrumb=piiBreadcrumbScrub, and loudly warns (not silently no-ops) when EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is unset.
  • The §4 internal-principal boundary has a REAL cross-language gate: internalPrincipal.crossBinding.test.ts mints via the prod N-API path and decrypts via the recommender's PyO3 binding, asserting claim-for-claim equality and correct deviceId-omitted->None handling, with CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1 turning a missing/drifted extension into a red gate instead of a green-by-skip. media.ts (line 47-52) and recommender.ts fail CLOSED (502) when minting fails rather than proxying anonymously.
  • CSRF middleware (csrf.ts) is sound: state-changing methods require an Origin in env.allowedOrigins; missing Origin in prod is rejected; an empty allow-list in prod fails closed ('csrf_misconfigured'); GET/HEAD are exempt by design and side-effecting GETs opt back in via requireTrustedOrigin. Cookies are SameSite=None only in prod (session.ts:429) and HttpOnly+Secure, making Origin the correct sole CSRF distinguisher.
  • Migrations are transactional (BEGIN/exec/COMMIT with ROLLBACK on error) in both runners (db.ts:119-127, migrator.ts:292-300), applied in lexically-sorted version order with an applied-set skip, and CRLF-normalized — idempotent re-runs are safe.
  • ssrfGuard's range table is correct: isPrivateIPv4/IPv6 covers 10/8, 127, 0/8, 169.254 metadata, 172.16/12, 192.168/16, 100.64/10 CGNAT, 224+ multicast, ::1/::, fc00::/7, fe80::/10 and ::ffff: mapped, treating octets >255 as unsafe; it IS invoked on the two IPTV proxy fetch paths (iptv.ts:680, iptv.ts:1024).
  • index.ts fails fast at boot on missing/old ffmpeg (validateFfmpegOrExit) and runs ensureServerId() before serving; serverDb.ts uses INSERT OR IGNORE so server_id is generated exactly once and stable across reboots. version.ts correctly re-uses the same ensureServerId (re-exported from session.ts:339 -> serverDb.ts), so /api/version, device-token serverId claims, and Keychain keying all share ONE identity source.
Plex parity notes

This subsystem is infrastructure, not a user-facing Plex feature, but several items bear on operating a Plex replacement safely at household + App-Store scale: (1) Plex depends on a robust, drift-checked library-DB migration story across years of upgrades; here the hardened migrator with sha256/backup gates EXISTS but is NOT on the live path (db.ts runs instead), so long-term schema evolution on a self-hoster's NAS is riskier than Plex's, and the §7.4 backup-before-destructive guarantee is unmet in running code. (2) Plex Media Server proxies remote content and is itself a hardened SSRF target; the current string-only ssrfGuard is DNS-rebinding- and redirect-bypassable, which is below the bar for a server that proxies provider-controlled HLS manifest URLs. (3) The §4 internal-principal boundary (Hono->recommender/media-core) is the analog of Plex's internal service trust; the cross-binding round-trip is genuinely tested, but expiry/tamper REJECTION is not, and key rotation is comment-only — acceptable for now, must harden before multi-service M3/M4 scale. (4) A 24/7 Plex substitute must survive NAS reboots/updates without corrupting its DB or cutting active streams; the current shutdown closes DBs before draining requests and never stops the listener, weaker than expected for an always-on server. None of these block a demo; the migrator dead-code, SSRF rebinding, and shutdown ordering should all be closed before this is trusted as an always-on Plex substitute. Telemetry/version/CSRF/PII plumbing is at or above typical self-hosted-app quality.

Server — *arr / SAB Bridge & Downloads

functional

A Hono backend that proxies a curated, allow-listed subset of Sonarr/Radarr/SABnzbd APIs to the SPA so household members can browse, add, and (for admins) manage downloads without ever seeing the upstream API keys. It enforces a hard disk-space gate (MIN_FREE_GB), a per-title size cap (env.maxMovieBytes / maxTvBytesPerEpisode) that intercepts Radarr/Sonarr auto-search and drives its own release selection, an append-only JSONL grab-event log for observability, a per-user Claude usage/cost log, and admin-only Discord webhook provisioning across both *arr apps.

Maturity: The security-critical invariants (server-side key injection, fail-closed disk gate, global Origin/CSRF gate, path-traversal defense on DELETE) are real and correct, and telemetry/usage-logging ARE actually wired; the gaps that keep it below production-grade are no rate limiting, in-memory-only disk-reservation accounting that races across requests/replicas, JSONL stores that lose history at scale, unfiltered upstream query forwarding, and thin per-route test coverage.

29 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • API keys never reach the browser: sonarrFetch/radarrFetch inject X-Api-Key as a server-held header (services/sonarr.ts:29, radarr.ts:29) and sabCall splices apikey as a server-side query param (services/sab.ts:16); none are sourced from client input.
  • Disk-space gate is genuinely fail-closed: the Sonarr/Radarr add paths reject with 507 on missing path, unknown path, non-finite freeSpace, or freeSpace<minFreeBytes (sonarr.ts:525-558, radarr.ts validateRadarrRootFolderSpace:34-69), and env.ts:72-82 throws at boot on a non-numeric MIN_FREE_GB instead of letting NaN silently disable the gate. Tests confirm admins are gated too (sonarr.test.ts:210).
  • Path-traversal on DELETE/upgrade/monitor is closed by Number.isSafeInteger(id)&&id>0 guards (sonarr.ts:843-846, radarr.ts:592-595, 508-514) with an accurate comment explaining the encoded-slash + WHATWG URL normalization attack they defeat; the encoded-slash attack is regression-tested (sonarr.test.ts:152, radarr.test.ts:112).
  • CSRF defense is mounted globally — app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin) (app.ts:66) gates every POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE on Origin∈allowedOrigins, fails closed in prod (csrf.ts:35-38), and env.ts:103-108 refuses to boot in prod without ALLOWED_ORIGINS.
  • SAB read/mutate split is sound: reads require ?mode= and reject any ?name= param (sab.ts:32-34) to kill the legacy GET-name attack (regression-tested at sab.test.ts:115); pause/resume/delete are POST/DELETE + ensureAdmin (sab.ts:59-90); nzoId flows into value= via URLSearchParams.set so it is auto-encoded and name= is hardcoded server-side (no injection).
  • Non-admin policy laundering is correctly defended: materializeNonAdminSeriesBody/MovieBody discard everything outside a descriptive-metadata allowlist and re-derive rootFolderPath/qualityProfileId/monitored from server config (sonarr.ts:330-486, radarr.ts:197-332); tests assert a hostile rootFolderPath/qualityProfileId/seasons[] are scrubbed and replaced with server defaults (sonarr.test.ts:313, radarr.test.ts:234).
  • Upstream calls are uniformly time-bounded via fetchWithTimeout (LAN_TIMEOUT_MS=15s) which synthesizes a 504 on abort/network error (upstream.ts:24-63) so no route hangs on a dead Sonarr/Radarr/SAB; the body is fully drained into an arrayBuffer before constructing the replay Response, avoiding stream-reuse bugs.
  • Telemetry IS wired: index.ts:15,37 imports @sentry/node (package.json:28, ^9.47.1) and calls Sentry.init({ dsn, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, onUnhandledRejectionIntegration }) gated on env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, with env.ts:267 hard-failing prod boot if the DSN is absent — the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract is satisfied at the SDK-init level.
  • Usage/cost logging IS wired: suggestions.ts:2434-2436/2472/2590-2593 calls appendUsageEvent with type 'claude_call'/'claude_error' on the real Claude path, so /api/usage/me|admin|log surface live per-user spend; computeCostCents (usageLog.ts:265) is unit-tested against Haiku 4.5 pricing.
  • Notifications connector management is unusually careful: a serialized mutation lock (withDiscordMutationLock), fail-closed-on-list-error to avoid stacking duplicate webhooks, full reverse-order rollback of partial mutations (notifications.ts:32-44,96-119,205-287), an https://(discord.com|discordapp.com)/api/webhooks/ allowlist (notifications.ts:201), and thorough tests for create/update/dup-suppression/partial-delete (notifications.test.ts).
  • Radarr add rolls back the created movie when the capped grab fails or finds nothing and surfaces a 424 with phase detail (radarr.ts:439-485) rather than leaving an orphaned unmonitored entry.
  • grabLog/usageLog serialize appends through a single in-flight write queue to prevent interleaved JSONL lines, rotate at 5MB, tail-read backward in 64KB chunks, and summarizeUsage uses a scan-until-cutoff with a SUMMARY_HARD_CAP memory bound (usageLog.ts:118-175,216-258); chunk-spanning tail reads and rotation are tested (grabLog.test.ts:66,107).
Plex parity notes

This subsystem is NOT a Plex-equivalent capability — Plex itself has no built-in usenet/torrent acquisition layer; this *arr/SAB bridge is closer to Ombi/Overseerr (request + acquire) bolted onto the household's existing Sonarr/Radarr/SAB. For the stated 'full Plex replacement' goal the relevant points are: (1) the size-cap + curated-profile policy is a thoughtful household-safety feature Plex/Overseerr lack, and is a genuine strength; (2) there is no per-user request quota, approval workflow, or request visibility that Overseerr provides — any authenticated member can add directly, gated only by disk space, which the owner should confirm is intended for a multi-user App-Store deployment; (3) grab events have no user attribution (grabLog.ts:31-51), so unlike Overseerr you cannot answer 'who requested this' for moderation, and /api/grabs/by-item is enumerable. None of these block Plex playback parity (that lives in media-core/transcoder/IPTV). NOTE / correction to an earlier draft: telemetry (Sentry/Glitchtip via index.ts:37) and per-user Claude cost logging (suggestions.ts → appendUsageEvent) ARE actually wired and functional — the observability table-stakes for opening this to scale exist; the remaining scale risks are rate limiting, the in-memory disk-reservation races, and the JSONL stores.

Server — Auth & AuthZ

functional

Authentication and authorization for the invite-only platform. Four identity providers converge on one shared allowlist gate: Plex PIN OAuth (plex.ts + auth.ts), Sign-in-with-Apple identity-token verification (appleAuth.ts), device-token JWE bearer auth for native clients (session.ts mint/verify + middleware/deviceTokenAuth.ts + reconcileDeviceToken.ts), and WebAuthn passkeys (webauthn.ts + routes/passkey.ts). Sessions are stateless JWE cookies (session.ts). AuthZ is enforced via requireAuth/requireAdmin (middleware/auth.ts) backed by per-request reconcileSession (sessionGate.ts: role recompute + periodic Plex liveness probe + cascade device revocation). CSRF is an Origin-header gate (middleware/csrf.ts) mounted globally in app.ts. The members/invites allowlist (members.ts/membership.ts/invites.ts) plus ADMIN_SUBS owner-bootstrap and PLEX_SERVER_ID scoping (env.ts) decide access. sub.ts is the canonical namespace parser; secrets.ts/keyDerivation.ts handle secret strength + HKDF; tokenReplayCache.ts backs the stream-token replay defense consumed by routes/iptv.ts.

Maturity: The crypto/identity core is genuinely strong and mostly production-grade (JWE sessions, Apple RS256+aud+iss+nonce pinning, HKDF key separation, atomic race-safe invite redemption, fail-closed prod env, globally-mounted CSRF, Sentry telemetry IS initialized, replay cache IS wired into iptv). Held at 'functional' not 'production-grade' because of real per-process-scale limits (rate limiter and revocation/membership caches are single-replica only, contradicting the App-Store-scale goal) plus latent correctness gaps (guest-role laundering, device-token role never re-derived so admin demotion lags 180 days on paired devices, and Hono handler errors are not forwarded to Sentry).

30 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • env.ts fails CLOSED in prod on every dangerous default: throws if ALLOWED_ORIGINS empty (env.ts:103-108), if PLEX_SERVER_ID unset without explicit ALLOW_UNSCOPED_PLEX_LOGIN=1 (env.ts:121-131), if SESSION_SECRET is a known placeholder or <32 chars (env.ts:176-197), and if EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN missing (env.ts:267-274). No `?? 'devsecret'` signing-secret fallback reaches prod.
  • Secret hygiene: STREAM/DEVICE/INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL secrets each strength-validated and asserted pairwise-distinct at boot in ALL envs (env.ts:221-260, secrets.ts:72-102) so copy-paste reuse is caught before CI.
  • Apple SIWA verifier is textbook: jose jwtVerify with algorithms pinned to ['RS256'] (blocks alg=none/HS-RS confusion), issuer pinned to appleid.apple.com, audience pinned to APPLE_CLIENT_ID, 60s clock tolerance, constant-time nonce compare, sub taken ONLY from the verified payload and re-validated via parseSub, fail-closed when aud unconfigured, JWKS URL hardcoded (no SSRF), jwks_unavailable mapped to 5xx not 401 (appleAuth.ts:125-217).
  • Telemetry IS wired: index.ts:15,37 imports @sentry/node and calls Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, release, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, onUnhandledRejectionIntegration }). Prod boot hard-fails without the DSN; a PII-scrub beforeSend is attached — satisfying the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract at the SDK-init level.
  • Stream-token replay defense IS wired: routes/iptv.ts:26 imports checkReplay and calls it on live (578), remux (803), and segment (1003) tokens; tokenReplayCache.ts enforces segment strict-single-use and multi-use-until-exp with a self-starting GC sweep.
  • Legacy SHA-256 session-key fallback has a CODE-ENFORCED sunset: index.ts:60-68 refuses to boot on/after 2026-06-25 (process.exit(1)), so the deprecated-crypto path cannot silently outlive its grace window — not merely a TODO.
  • Session cookie is a real JWE (A256GCM/dir) not signed-only, so the embedded plexAuthToken is encrypted at rest (session.ts:35,351-361); HKDF key with info-string domain separation (keyDerivation.ts:56-88).
  • Device-token verify is defense-in-depth: decrypt via the Rust contracts crate, aud/iss/role/authMode shape checks, DB-row-exists-and-not-expired check AND revocation-table check (session.ts:285-309); supports revocation despite stateless JWE.
  • Bearer auth deliberately does NOT fall back to cookie when a Bearer header is present-but-invalid (deviceTokenAuth.ts:57-60, middleware/auth.ts:42-47) — a stolen cookie can't bypass a freshly-revoked device token. Explicit nbf/exp skew re-check (deviceTokenAuth.ts:65-69).
  • AuthZ decoupled from Plex identity: all four login paths converge on the single authorizeOrRedeem/memberStatus allowlist gate (auth.ts:237-249; passkey.ts:79; sessionGate.ts:132-139). Passkey registration runs authZ BETWEEN attestation-verify and persistCredential so a bad invite leaves no orphan credential (passkey.ts:75-87).
  • Invite redemption is atomic and race-safe: IMMEDIATE sqlite transaction with a `used_count < max_uses` guarded UPDATE and an ExhaustedRace rollback so two concurrent redeems can't over-spend a single-use code (invites.ts:145-241); 128-bit codes, only sha256(code) persisted, plaintext shown once.
  • WebAuthn challenges are single-use via a fetch+delete transaction with TTL (webauthn.ts:90-108), signCount is bumped on every login (webauthn.ts:245-247), RP ID + expectedOrigin bound to env, simplewebauthn enforces counter regression.
  • CSRF Origin gate is mounted GLOBALLY before all routes (app.ts:66) and fails closed in prod when allowedOrigins is empty (csrf.ts:35-37); CORS reflects only allow-listed origins with credentials (app.ts:41-60). plexLinks (requireAuth) is correctly mounted BEFORE plexAdmin (requireAdmin) so first-match-wins routing doesn't 403 household members (app.ts:143-148). Every admin/self route mounts requireAdmin/requireAuth (users.ts:26, devices.ts:102-103/177, adminInvites.ts:34/100, plex-admin.ts:20, plex-links.ts:26).
  • Per-request reconcile keeps state fresh: role recomputed from env.admins every call, Plex liveness probe fails-open on 5xx/network but drops the session on a definitive 401/403, and a denial cascades device-token revocation so paired tvOS/iOS tokens drop on next request instead of at 180-day TTL (sessionGate.ts:123-207, reconcileDeviceToken.ts:83-99).
  • Self vs admin device routes are correctly sub-scoped: self deletes/renames are WHERE jti=? AND sub=? so a user cannot revoke/rename another user's device (devices.ts:118-168); adminMembers refuses to revoke an ADMIN_SUBS owner sub (adminInvites.ts:138-145).
Plex parity notes

The auth posture is a deliberate divergence from Plex, not a clone: cookie-login access is invite-only by a local members/invites allowlist (auth.ts:294-304, membership.ts), with Plex as one of four identity providers alongside Apple, passkey, and device-token. This is arguably stronger than Plex's share model for a closed household and is the only way to support the non-Plex providers Plex lacks. Parity GAPS to weigh: (1) Plex AUTO-REVOCATION — Plex instantly cuts a removed share recipient; here a removed share-recipient who still has an allowlist row keeps cookie access until an admin revokes the row or plex.tv returns 401/403 for the stored token (sessionGate.ts fails open on the probe). (2) INCONSISTENT LOGIN GATES — the cookie path is allowlist-only but the device-pair path (device.ts:135-142) still requires live Plex-server membership at mint, so the two paths de-provision differently. (3) Plex Home / managed-user / per-user library restrictions have NO analog in the session model, which carries only sub+role, not per-library entitlements — fine for whole-library household access, short of Plex's parental/managed-user scoping. plex-links.ts does correctly scope its library-link cache per-sub (plex-links.ts:51-64) to avoid leaking item presence across Home profiles with different visibility. (4) Device-token admin demotion lags up to 180 days (reconcileDeviceToken does not re-derive role) whereas Plex permission changes propagate quickly. (5) The non-Plex trust roots (Apple JWKS verifier, WebAuthn) are well-implemented and give this system multi-provider login Plex cannot match.

Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System

functional

Client-side auth/onboarding UI (Sign in with Apple, passkeys/WebAuthn, invite + member management, paired-device management, per-user AI API key, Discord webhook config), the app chrome (top nav, home nav, user menu, toast, confirm dialog, onboarding walkthrough, IPTV concurrency/connections UI), the WebGL/Canvas/video brand atmosphere (EmeraldMark gem, Kraken/resting video backdrop, Constellation, Beacon), and the OKLCH design-token system that encodes the project's maritime-luxe / Netflix-grade UI contract.

Maturity: The auth/chrome/atmosphere components are individually well-built, fully styled (every component has co-located CSS consuming the OKLCH tokens), error-handled, and partially unit-tested; but production-grade gaps remain — the passkey client depends on a placeholder npm version, CSRF middleware is unenforced while CORS reflects any credentialed origin, the mandatory §15 crash telemetry is absent, and several modals/overlays miss focus-trap/Enter-suppression accessibility requirements stated in DESIGN.md.

62 files reviewed · 9 findings
What works
  • tokens.css faithfully encodes the DESIGN.md contract: correct OKLCH palette, no #000/#fff in the token layer, motion easing cubic-bezier(0.16,1,0.3,1), z-scale, and a prefers-reduced-motion block that collapses all --dur-* tokens to 0.01ms plus a mobile breakpoint override
  • Every audited component ships co-located CSS (UserMenu.css, AppleSignInButton.css, TopNav.css, ConfirmModal.css, Toast.css, Walkthrough.css, atmosphere/*.css, etc.) — the design system is implemented, not just specified
  • appleSdk.ts is a solid lazy SDK loader: single-flight Promise guard, dedupes an existing script tag, 10s timeout, resets sdkLoad on failure to allow retry, crypto.getRandomValues nonce for replay defense, graceful hide when VITE_APPLE_CLIENT_ID is unset, and dedicated unit tests (appleSdk.test.ts)
  • AppleSignInButton precisely classifies Apple SDK rejections (popup_closed_by_user / user_cancelled / user_trigger_new_signin_flow treated as silent cancels vs real errors) and disables during pending states
  • PasskeyButtons feature-detects via browserSupportsWebAuthn() in a try/catch and hides the whole block on unsupported browsers rather than offering a button that can only fail
  • DevicesPanel is a complete self-service device manager (list, rename with 1-128 validation, revoke single, sign-out-everywhere) using TanStack mutations with onSuccess invalidation and onError user messaging; platform labels and relative-time formatting are clean
  • DiscordNotifications keeps the menu open on partial-delete failure and surfaces the backend's per-connector failure list, and uses an alive-flag cleanup in its mount effect to avoid setState-after-unmount
  • ConfirmModal/ConfirmProvider use a native <dialog> with showModal()/close(), onCancel preventDefault so ESC is controllable, focus restoration to the previously-focused element, and resolve a Promise<boolean> — a clean async confirm pattern
  • Toast component uses an ARIA live region with role/aria-live, manual dismiss with aria-label, auto-dismiss timers, and timer cleanup; LoadingPulse is a lightweight reduced-motion-aware skeleton
  • EmeraldMark (the user-approved WebGL exception) skips boot under navigator.webdriver to avoid crashing headless CI GPU processes, gates animation on prefers-reduced-motion AND document.hidden (visibilitychange), wraps GemScene construction in try/catch with graceful aria-label fallback, and fully disposes geometry/material/envTex/renderer on unmount
  • gemScene.ts GemScene.dispose() disposes geometry, material, env texture, and renderer and cancels RAF; Kraken uses a dual-video crossfade (no remount black-gap) and aria-hidden; Constellation is pure static SVG honoring the DESIGN.md no-WebGL-for-ambient rule
  • apiFetch centralizes auth-cookie credentialed fetch into a typed ApiError carrying status+parsed body and handles 204 No Content correctly
  • TopNav implements proper roving-tabindex keyboard nav (ArrowLeft/ArrowRight wrap) on role=tablist, and feature-gates Live/Media/Users tabs on backend capability flags
Plex parity notes

Auth/onboarding parity is the strongest part of this subsystem and in some respects exceeds Plex's home setup: it offers three sign-in paths (Apple SIWA, passkeys/WebAuthn, Plex), single-use invite codes with expiry and member management (InvitesPanel), and a self-service paired-device manager with rename + revoke + sign-out-everywhere (DevicesPanel) that maps cleanly onto Plex's Authorized Devices. The IPTV ConcurrencyLimitModal/ConnectionsWidget provide the concurrent-stream-limit UX that Plex enforces server-side. Gaps vs a production Plex alternative: (1) the passkey path is non-buildable due to the placeholder dependency, so one advertised login method is currently dead; (2) the cookie-session security model is undermined by unenforced CSRF + reflective credentialed CORS, which a multi-tenant streaming server must get right; (3) mandatory crash telemetry (§15) that an at-scale service needs is absent in this tier; (4) accessibility polish on the confirm dialog (Enter suppression) and the first-run walkthrough (focus trap/ESC) is below the bar for an App-Store client. The design-token system and component styling are well ahead of a typical homelab dashboard and credibly support the stated Netflix-grade aesthetic. Native Apple clients remain the headline blocker but are out of this subsystem's scope.

Crate — media-core (M3 Rust media server)

functional

A Rust/Axum library server (edition 2024) that scans on-disk media roots with ffprobe, owns media.db (sqlx/SQLite), and serves a direct-play library + per-user watch state to the Hono backend behind the §4 internal-principal boundary. Computes the direct-play-vs-transcode decision (capability.rs), hands transcode-required files off to the M4 transcoder, and enriches metadata best-effort from TMDB. This is the Plex-equivalent "library + playback decision" core.

Maturity: Cohesive, idiomatic Rust with no panics/unwrap on request paths, graceful shutdown, an effective IDOR guard, admin-gated scans, and 104 mostly-meaningful tests — but the TMDB matcher blindly takes results[0] with no confidence/language scoring (wrong metadata at scale), there is zero telemetry despite the §15 MANDATORY-Glitchtip contract, no perf/accuracy benchmark exists, and the ServeFile stream path has no HTTP timeout and re-canonicalizes on every request.

19 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • No panics or .unwrap()/.expect() on any request-handling path. Grep confirms every unwrap/expect lives inside #[cfg(test)] modules or the controlled startup path (main.rs IP parse falls back to 127.0.0.1; transcoder_http() uses unwrap_or_default()). Request handlers return AppResult and map cleanly through error.rs IntoResponse.
  • Graceful shutdown is wired: main.rs:53-55 axum::serve(...).with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal()) on tokio ctrl_c. In-flight requests drain on SIGINT.
  • The acting_sub IDOR guard (routes.rs:655-672) is correct and well-tested: ?sub= is honored ONLY in Off mode; in Log/Enforce a verified principal is authoritative and a bare ?sub= is rejected 401. The test acting_sub_rejects_query_sub_outside_off_mode covers the attacker-names-victim case in both Log and Enforce.
  • Scan single-flight is genuinely race-free: a single AtomicBool claimed via compare_exchange(false,true,SeqCst,SeqCst) is shared by both the scheduler (lib.rs:56-63) and the manual endpoint (routes.rs:796-799), so a scheduled tick and POST /scan can never overlap; the skipped path correctly does NOT clear the guard (run_guarded_scan_skips_when_already_running asserts this).
  • Scan partial-failure handling is robust: every per-file failure mode (walk error, non-utf8 path, stat failure, ffprobe failure, index failure, lookup failure) is caught, logged via tracing::warn!, counted in report.errors, and continue's — one bad file never aborts the pass, and scan_once never propagates an error to tear down the scheduler (lib.rs:77-80).
  • Admin gate on the DoS-prone rescan: authorize_scan (routes.rs:760-776) requires role=="admin" outside Off mode, returns 403 'admin role required'. Backed by the pure authorize_scan_admin_gate test plus integration tests that mint real signed principals (scan_rejects_non_admin_with_403, scan_allows_admin_with_202).
  • capability::decide is a clean, pure, exhaustively-tested function (11 cases): container/codec case-insensitive match, height-over-max, HDR-to-SDR tone-map, unknown container/codec, empty-hdr-as-SDR, no-max-height-allows-any.
  • Path containment defense-in-depth (path_within_roots, routes.rs:352-364) canonicalizes both the DB path and each root before a starts_with prefix check, so a poisoned scan row with .. or an escaping symlink cannot be streamed; refuses (404) when canonicalize fails.
  • ffprobe is spawned via tokio::process::Command (no FFI), non-zero exit is surfaced as ProbeError::Failed with stderr, and parse_ffprobe_json is pure and heavily fixture-tested (h264/hevc, HDR10/HLG/Dolby-Vision side-data, multi-audio, forced subs, numeric-or-string fields, empty doc).
  • Corrupt-JSON resilience in models.rs: audio/subtitle track decoders return empty Vec + a tracing::warn! carrying media_file_id on malformed JSON, and expose *_checked() variants so callers can distinguish corruption from a genuinely track-less file — covered by 6 tests including wrong-shape JSON.
  • filename.rs is the strongest module: root-authoritative classification (Shows never yields a movie, Movies never an episode), reversed-basename detection (the live 010E70S corruption), last-plausible-year selection so 1917 isn't eaten, noise-token stripping, and a normalize_show_name dedup key — 16 tests including property-style invariants.
  • TMDB layer is correctly best-effort and never blocks a scan: 5s per-request reqwest timeout, a single Retry-After-honoring 429 retry capped at 10s (MAX_RETRY_AFTER_SECS) so a hostile header can't wedge a scan, and all failures degrade to None with a warn. Steady-state rescans short-circuit before any network call when rows are already enriched.
  • Transcoder handoff degrades safely: unreachable/timeout/non-2xx all map to AppError::TranscoderRequired (503), identical to the M3-only no-transcoder posture, never a 500 — and it mints a FRESH internal-principal for the hop rather than replaying the caller's partially-spent 60s token. Five integration tests cover direct-play-serves-bytes, handoff-success, busy→503, unreachable→503, and no-transcoder→503.
  • Migration ledger mirrors the cross-service convention (schema_migrations version/applied_at/checksum, CRLF-normalized sha256), runs each migration in its own transaction, is idempotent, and surfaces parent-dir-creation failure with an attributed error (two db.rs tests).
Plex parity notes

media-core covers the core Plex library/playback surface competently for M3 scope: ffprobe-driven scanning with skip-unchanged, root-authoritative movie/show/episode classification, dual-version (1080p vs 2160p) quality preference, watch-state with resume position, and a real direct-play-vs-transcode capability decision feeding an M4 transcoder handoff. Gaps vs Plex parity: (1) metadata accuracy — Plex/Jellyfin use multi-candidate scoring with year+language+agent fallbacks and let users fix mismatches; here results[0] is trusted and permanently cached, so wrong posters/titles will accumulate with no re-match path. (2) No FTS/fast search, no genre/cast/collection/artwork-caching, no chapter/intro-marker data — the schema is a thin movies/shows/episodes model, not Plex's rich metadata graph. (3) No per-server stream concurrency limits or stream read timeouts, which Plex enforces. (4) No subtitle/audio track SELECTION endpoint (tracks are probed and returned in the grant, but there's no API to pick one for transcode beyond the caps blob). (5) Scanning is serial and unbenchmarked — Plex parallelizes and incrementally watches the filesystem (inotify); here it's a polling scheduler with a serial probe loop. The internal-principal auth boundary, IDOR guard, and admin-gated rescan are genuinely solid and arguably ahead of Plex's looser internal trust model. The headline parity blocker for this crate specifically is metadata-match quality at scale, which directly degrades the Netflix-grade browsing experience the product targets.

Server — IPTV Core (M1)

functional

Backend for the live-TV / IPTV portion of the Plex replacement. Ingests an Xtream-Codes provider's catalog (live channels, VOD, series, categories) and XMLTV EPG into a dedicated SQLite mirror; exposes catalog/EPG/favorites/history read+write endpoints; proxies stream playback (MPEG-TS passthrough, HLS manifest rewrite, ffmpeg remux-to-HLS for AVPlayer, VOD/series progressive with Range, catch-up timeshift) through the server using short-lived HMAC stream-grant tokens (signed by the Rust emerald-contracts crate) so upstream provider credentials never reach the client. Includes a scheduled sync with an in-process mutex, an SSRF guard on segment egress, a per-process concurrency tracker with a sessions UI, a single-use token replay cache, and an external-player M3U export with revocable playlist tokens.

Maturity: Router is mounted (app.ts:65), scheduler is started at boot (index.ts), secrets are env-validated with no dev fallback, token sign/verify is delegated to the Rust contracts crate with constant-time dual-key verify and a wired single-use replay cache, EPG uses a true streaming sax parser, and catalog sync is transactional with tombstone reconciliation — it is a real, integrated feature, well past prototype. It falls short of production-grade because the SSRF guard is bypassable via fetch redirect-follow on the stream-bytes paths, the concurrency limiter is per-process and HLS-leaky, and there is no integration test that boots the app against the live proxy paths.

33 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • Token sign/verify is delegated to the Rust emerald-contracts crate (iptvStreamToken.ts:173,228) using a canonical fixed-template serializer locked by cross-language vectors; dual-key verify computes both HMACs unconditionally to avoid a timing side channel (iptvStreamToken.ts:221-251), and time-window/skew enforcement is in the crate — far better than a hand-rolled HMAC compare
  • Single-use replay protection IS wired: every stream/segment/remux/catchup handler calls checkReplay(jti, exp, kind) (iptv.ts:578, 803, 1003) backed by tokenReplayCache, so a captured segment/stream token cannot be trivially replayed within its TTL
  • Secrets are hard-required: env.ts validates STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET and SESSION_SECRET and the process refuses to boot without them (no 'dev-secret' fallback anywhere in the IPTV path)
  • EPG parsing is a genuine streaming sax parser (iptvEpg.ts:99) with gzip auto-detect from the first two bytes, idle-timeout + 30-min wall-timeout aborts, event-loop yielding every 500 programmes, and proper stream teardown on error/abort — the multi-MB-EPG-won't-blow-heap claim is real
  • EPG writes are batched in 1,000-row transactions via UPSERT (db.stmts.upsertEpg) with a stale-window DELETE before load (iptvSync.ts:178-208); epg_programs has the dedup key so re-syncs do not duplicate
  • Catalog sync is fully transactional (iptvSync.ts:143-174) with a fetched_at generation marker driving reconcileCatalog deletes, plus soft-delete + nightly hard-sweep of iptv_title_link rows — real tombstoning, not append-only
  • SSRF guard (ssrfGuard.ts) resolves ALL DNS addresses and blocks loopback, RFC1918, link-local 169.254, ULA fc/fd, CGNAT 100.64/10, and v4-mapped IPv6; it is applied at every segment-manifest egress (iptv.ts:680, 1024) with an explanatory threat-model comment naming 169.254.169.254 and recommender:8000
  • HLS rewriter (iptvHlsRewrite.ts) rewrites both bare segment lines and URI="..." attributes inside tags, resolving relative refs against the upstream base, so upstream URLs/creds never appear in the manifest body delivered to the client
  • ffmpeg remux uses spawn('ffmpeg', argsArray) with array args (no shell), redacts Xtream creds from stderr via scrubXtreamCreds, has SIGTERM->SIGKILL escalation, idle-sweep, an 8s manifest-ready deadline with 504 fallback, and unref'd timers (iptvRemux.ts) — solid process lifecycle
  • Sync mutex (iptvSync.ts:93 'if (running) return {busy:true}') plus a scheduler that no-ops overlapping ticks; the M3U export gates VOD/series catalog dump behind a constant-secret header (iptv.ts:1067), and playlist tokens are persisted and revocable (iptv.ts:306-316)
  • Pervasive input validation: numeric id regexes (/^\d+$/), clampLimit/clampOffset, catch-up rid pipe-injection guard (iptv.ts:511), LIKE wildcard escaping with ESCAPE '\' (iptvCatalog.ts:73), and all SQL is parameterized — no SQL-injection surface
Plex parity notes

For Plex-equivalent live TV the IPTV core is meaningfully further along than the roadmap's terse framing suggests: it has a real catalog mirror, streaming EPG with now/next + grid + per-channel window, catch-up/timeshift, MPEG-TS passthrough, HLS manifest rewrite, ffmpeg remux-to-HLS for AVPlayer, VOD/series with Range, favorites, watch history, and a revocable external-player M3U (VLC/TiviMate) — the last is something Plex itself does not expose as cleanly. The principal parity gaps: (1) Concurrency/'too many streams' is Plex's signature enforcement and here it is per-process, advisory-at-grant, and leaks for HLS — it will not reliably cap real playback at scale, and a kicked session keeps streaming. (2) Transcoding is remux-only (-c copy); there is no real transcode/transrate ladder, so a client whose device can't decode the source codec/container has no fallback, whereas Plex transcodes per client capability (this aligns with the M4 transcoder being the separate, incomplete piece). (3) EPG is flat programmes (title/desc/start/stop) with no series linkage, ratings, categories, or artwork, versus Plex's rich guide. (4) Security posture (redirect-SSRF bypass, creds-in-segment-tokens, plaintext provider password) is below the bar for a product proxying a paid subscription to App-Store devices. Net: the live-TV building blocks are genuinely functional and integrated (mounted, scheduled, token-secured, replay-protected) — not the dead code an initial skim might suggest — but session/concurrency semantics, transcoding fallback, and the SSRF/credential hardening need real work before this is a Plex-class, App-Store-safe live experience.

Infra, Docker, CI/CD & Deploy

functional

Defines how theemeraldexchange is built, tested, packaged, and shipped: the multi-service docker-compose stack (Hono backend, Python recommender, Rust media-core + transcoder, cloudflared tunnel, and a full self-hosted Glitchtip telemetry stack), the four multi-stage Dockerfiles, the GitHub Actions CI merge gates, the rsync-based NAS deploy script, the Netlify SPA build config, and the TypeScript/Vite/Vitest/Playwright/ESLint toolchain configs.

Maturity: The stack builds and deploys end-to-end with genuinely sophisticated multi-stage Dockerfiles (cross-language NAPI/PyO3 artifact staging, static ffmpeg, glibc/musl reasoning), thoughtful loopback-only network isolation, and a correctly-pinned mandatory Glitchtip telemetry stack; but it is NOT production-grade: the backend/media-core/transcoder run as root with no healthcheck, the ffmpeg-spawning transcoder has no container CPU/RAM/pids limit, no container hardening (cap_drop/no-new-privileges/read_only), the public-facing cloudflared is :latest, coverage has no enforced floor, 'E2E' runs against a mocked SPA with no backend, and CI never builds the images it ships.

21 files reviewed · 16 findings
What works
  • Network isolation is well-designed: backend binds 127.0.0.1:3001 only, recommender/media-core/transcoder publish to NAS loopback only (8001/8002/8003) and talk over the internal docker network; only cloudflared reaches the public internet, and the internal services are never tunnel-exposed.
  • Multi-stage Dockerfiles are genuinely sophisticated and well-documented: the backend stages the linux-x64-gnu NAPI .node from a rust:1.90 builder; the recommender stages the PyO3 wheel via maturin and installs CPU-only torch from PyTorch's CPU index to avoid the ~6GB CUDA payload; better-sqlite3's python3/make/g++ toolchain is installed and purged in the same layer.
  • ffmpeg version gate handled at build time: static-ffmpeg 7.1 is COPYed in because Debian bookworm apt ships 5.1, which fails server/services/ffmpeg.ts's boot-time >=6.0 assertion (validateFfmpegOrExit, called at server/index.ts:28).
  • Telemetry is actually wired, not just declared: server/index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub }) before serve(), and EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is passed through in compose; a full self-hosted Glitchtip stack (web + worker + postgres:15 + redis:7) is defined with correct v6.0 migration/partition bootstrap, VALKEY_URL, and TRUSTED_PROXIES — this satisfies the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract, and a PII scrub runs on every event.
  • The recommender image is the one fully-hardened container: it creates a non-root system user (uid 10001), chowns /srv and /data to it, and ships a real HEALTHCHECK (curl /health, 30s interval) — a correct model the other three images do not follow.
  • Glitchtip and the only public-facing-adjacent images carry deliberate version pins with comments explaining why `latest` is dangerous (glitchtip 6.1; the compose comments capture the Granian port change, django-vtasks worker role, and migrate-on-web-role gotcha).
  • Strong secret hygiene at the boundary: env.ts production gate requires the three token IKMs and asserts pairwise distinctness (compose comments document it), deploy-nas.sh mirrors the SESSION_SECRET length+placeholder check and the PLEX_SERVER_ID/ALLOW_UNSCOPED gate before shipping, and .env.production.example leaves all secrets blank with openssl-generate instructions.
  • deploy-nas.sh has real preflight: required-key validation, .env shipped separately and chmod 600'd on the NAS, a compose-availability fallback chain that HARD-FAILS rather than silently degrading USE_LOCAL_RECOMMENDER, and a .dockerignore-presence warning.
  • CI gates all three language stacks on PR-to-main: Node (tsc SPA + tsc server + lint + vitest coverage + build + Playwright), recommender (PyO3 maturin wheel + pytest parity + the N-API↔PyO3 cross-binding gate run with CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1 so drift fails red), and Rust (cargo build + cargo test --workspace), plus a test-no-iptv matrix that re-runs the whole suite with IPTV_DISABLED=1.
  • .dockerignore is thorough and tracked: excludes .env*/node_modules/dist/.git/target/**/*.node/tests/data/recommender-db, so secrets and build junk never enter the build context; deploy-nas.sh ships it and the backend Dockerfile relies on it (the .node copy from the builder stage bypasses the **/*.node exclude deliberately).
  • media-core and transcoder mount the media library read-only (:ro) at the SAME host path so ffmpeg input paths resolve identically; the transcoder HLS scratch is a size-capped (2g) tmpfs that auto-clears on restart.
Plex parity notes

Infra is architected for a multi-service Plex-class system (library scanner = media-core, transcode-on-demand = transcoder, recommendations = recommender), and the read-only :media mount shared identically between media-core and transcoder mirrors how Plex separates scanning from transcoding. The gaps that matter for Plex parity are operational, not architectural: (1) Plex transcoding is resource-governed; here the transcoder has NO container CPU/RAM/pids limit (only app-level concurrency knobs), so under many concurrent streams it can OOM the NAS and take down the backend it depends on — 'many concurrent transcodes' is Plex's defining workload and this stack has no container-level backpressure for it. (2) No healthchecks on backend/media-core/transcoder means a crash-looping transcode service is invisible to the orchestrator while cloudflared keeps routing. (3) The transcoder running as root spawning ffmpeg over a mounted library is a privilege posture Plex does not take. (4) CI proves unit-green but never proves a real transcode or even builds the shipping images (consistent with the roadmap's M4 'no real transcode has ever run' note), so there is no infra-level evidence the transcode path works end-to-end. For an App-Store-bound Plex replacement the infra is a credible skeleton, but it is not yet hardened or resource-governed to survive Plex-equivalent concurrent-streaming load. Note: the recommender image is correctly hardened (non-root + healthcheck), so the team has a working template to bring the other three services up to that bar quickly.

Server — Media, Suggestions & Recommender Bridge

functional

Server-side media discovery and recommendation surface for the SPA: a thin authenticated streaming/proxy to media-core (routes/media.ts), a read-only accessor over media-core's media.db for local-availability tagging (mediaLibraryDb.ts + mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts + localAvailability.ts), the Claude-Haiku-backed personalized suggestions engine with a TMDB candidate-pool/discover/trending fallback chain and an alternate local-recommender-sidecar fast path (routes/suggestions.ts), the recommender bridge with internal-principal HMAC auth and bounded timeouts (recommender.ts, recommenderCaller.ts), per-user feedback + household rejection stores (userFeedback.ts, rejections.ts, routes/feedback.ts), a click-mirror endpoint (recommenderEvents.ts), a TMDB credits/trending proxy that keeps the key server-side (routes/tmdb.ts), the section-9 grant-time source-precedence resolver (sourcePrecedence.ts), an ffprobe boot-validation helper (ffmpeg.ts), and the N-API glue that loads the Rust contracts addon under ESM (contractsBinding.ts).

Maturity: The suggestions engine and feedback/rejections stores are genuinely production-minded (bounded timeouts, single-flight coalescing, atomic temp+rename writes, fail-closed corruption handling, cross-store rollback with split-brain logging, retry/fill chains that never serve an empty strip) and the media proxy correctly preserves byte-range streaming and fails closed on mint failure; but the read-only media.db local-tagging path is not consumed by the web client's search/detail, the server->recommender internal-principal is minted-but-not-enforced-by-default on the wire, and the local-media grant/source-selection/playback chain is acknowledged-in-code as deferred to M3+ — so it is solidly functional, not yet production-grade at App-Store scale.

27 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • routes/media.ts is a correctly-built streaming proxy: it forwards range/if-range/if-none-match/if-modified-since and copies back content-range/accept-ranges/etag/last-modified, preserving 206 Partial Content and 304 so HTTP seeking works end-to-end (media.ts:14-31, 80-93). It fails CLOSED on internal-principal mint failure with 502 rather than silently proxying an unauthenticated write (media.ts:47-52) — the right posture. The /api/media mount is correctly gated behind USE_MEDIA_CORE (app.ts:170).
  • mediaLibraryDb.ts opens media-core's media.db with {readonly:true, fileMustExist:true} and deliberately runs NO migrations and NO WAL pragma, with a comment explaining a second writer would race media-core's sqlx; the singleton degrades gracefully (returns null, latches openFailed, never throws) when the file is missing (mediaLibraryDb.ts:39-45, mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts:22-36). Correct ownership model, proven by tests (read-only rejected writes, fileMustExist).
  • Input validation and per-user isolation are sound (no IDOR): feedback/recommenderEvents derive sub from c.get('session').sub (set by requireAuth from a verified token), never from the client; tmdbId is gated by Number.isSafeInteger && >0 on POST and DELETE (feedback.ts:102-104, 233-235; recommenderEvents.ts:49-51); kind is enum-checked; SQL into media.db is parameterized (localAvailability.ts:74-77, suggestions.ts:1783-1788).
  • userFeedback.ts and rejections.ts are well-engineered persistence: atomic temp-file + rename(2) writes (userFeedback.ts:198-213), fail-closed on JSON parse corruption rather than wiping household data (userFeedback.ts:185-194, rejections.ts:107-118), a serialized write queue where the CALLER awaits the real op (route returns 500 on ENOSPC instead of lying {ok:true}) with a separate recovery branch keeping the chain alive — and the tests actually exercise ENOSPC and concurrent-failure ordering (userFeedback.test.ts:308-385).
  • feedback.ts handles the two-store (personal feedback + household rejection) cross-file race with a per-(kind,tmdbId) mutex (itemLocks) and explicit rollback-with-split-brain-logging on partial failure in both the dislike and red-to-green like branches (feedback.ts:48-73, 115-192). Genuinely careful concurrency work, not demo code.
  • The recommender bridge fails safe and is bounded: scoreOnce has a 5s AbortController timeout (recommender.ts:61,139-161), mirror posts a 3s timeout (recommender.ts:68,100-125), all mirror calls are fire-and-forget (void) so a hung sidecar never blocks the user, and recommenderCaller latches ensureServerId failure to avoid hammering disk on every request (recommenderCaller.ts:25-48).
  • The suggestions engine is genuinely sophisticated and defensive: single-flight coalescing on every TMDB GET (titleByIdInFlight/lookupInFlight), settled-result + null caches with key caps, 429 Retry-After honoring bounded by the abort budget, BACKFILL_MAX_PER_CALL so legacy bare-id rows don't fire hundreds of simultaneous TMDB requests, parallel paginated trending with per-page timeouts + allSettled, a Claude 20s deadline + transient-529/503 retry, tool-use-enforced output with a max_tokens-truncation guard, and a never-empty retry->discover->trending fill chain (suggestions.ts:654-820, 1248-1305, 1562-1598, 2305-2427, 2607-2681). It also sanitizes titles on read AND write as prompt-injection defense (userFeedback.ts:85, rejections.ts:65).
  • ffmpeg.ts boot validation is hardened: execFileSync with a hardcoded ['-version'] arg list (no injection surface), 5s timeout, ENOENT vs non-zero-exit handling, and a pass-unconditionally policy for git-dev/master version strings so it doesn't false-negative on nightly builds (ffmpeg.ts:106-171).
  • contractsBinding.ts correctly solves a real ESM/N-API footgun: it uses createRequire to load @emerald/contracts-napi because the addon's dynamic module.exports=loadBinding() resolves to undefined under a bare ESM star-import — the comment documents exactly why a naive import would silently yield undefined functions that throw only at call time (contractsBinding.ts:1-16). internalPrincipal.ts gates minting on INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET and throws a clear error if missing.
  • tmdb.ts keeps the TMDB key server-side and surfaces only /credits and /trending/:type, validating type against the movie|tv enum and returning 503 when unconfigured (tmdb.ts:42-44, 93-96). The suggestions route adds an explicit requireTrustedOrigin CSRF gate on its GET because it has write side effects via the sidecar (suggestions.ts:72-79) — a subtle correct call most teams miss.
Plex parity notes

This subsystem is discovery-and-proxy, and the local-media half of Plex parity is not yet operational. (1) PLAYBACK: routes/media.ts is a competent byte-range-preserving streaming proxy to media-core, but there is no grant/authorization step in front of it, and sourcePrecedence.ts cannot select a local/media-core source at all (buildCandidates only ever returns IPTV; the media-core and Plex slots are explicit comments saying 'not wired'). So even though media.db can be read and the proxy can stream bytes, there is no end-to-end 'pick the local copy and play it' flow — the defining Plex capability. The only working grant path in the SPA is IPTV (iptvApi.grant*). (2) LOCAL-AVAILABILITY: the read-only media.db tagger is real and well-built, but only the Discover suggestion strip consumes it; search/browse never show the 'in your library' badge Plex shows everywhere, and in most current deployments media.db doesn't exist so tagging is a silent no-op. (3) RECOMMENDATIONS / continue-watching analog: the Claude suggestions engine plus the local-recommender sidecar is genuinely sophisticated and arguably exceeds Plex's discovery polish, BUT the server->sidecar internal-principal auth defaults to 'off' (unenforced) and click/feedback mirroring drops silently with no telemetry, so the learning loop is fragile and spoofable on the internal network. Strengths toward parity: a correct media.db ownership boundary (read-only, no migration/WAL races), a real seeking-capable streaming proxy, careful per-user feedback isolation with no IDOR, and a never-empty fallback chain. The gap to a true Plex replacement here is the entire local-playback authorization-and-source-selection path, which the code itself defers to M3+.

Cross-Cutting: Dependencies, Supply Chain & Licensing

prototype

Audit every dependency manifest and lockfile across the polyglot stack (Node/TS SPA + Hono server, Rust workspace of 5 crates, Python FastAPI recommender) for unpinned/loose ranges on security-sensitive deps, hallucinated or impossibly-future major versions, unused declared dependencies, version-pin consistency between CI and Dockerfiles, telemetry wiring, and the GPL/x264 ffmpeg + IPTV licensing exposure against the proprietary LICENSE and the stated App-Store/paid-distribution goal.

Maturity: The polyglot supply-chain hygiene is split. The Rust side is genuinely production-grade: workspace-pinned crypto crates, Cargo.lock committed (66KB), rustls instead of OpenSSL, strip+lto release profile, and CI/Dockerfiles consistently pinned to rust 1.90. The cross-cutting infra (Sentry/Glitchtip telemetry IS wired at server/index.ts:37, CI node 24 / rust 1.90 / python 3.12 matches all four Dockerfiles, ffmpeg pinned to mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) is solid. But two issues keep this at prototype, not production: (1) the Node toolchain pins a cluster of majors that did not exist as GA stable as of the Jan 2026 cutoff (typescript ~6, vite 8, eslint 10, vitest 4) — these are either prerelease or hallucinated, and `npm ci` reproducibility on a clean machine cannot be assumed; depending on non-GA build/test/lint majors for an App-Store-bound product is reckless. (2) The transcoder ships GPL ffmpeg + libx264 (confirmed: args.rs:46 maps the CPU encoder to "libx264") under a strictly-proprietary LICENSE that explicitly names Apple App Store distribution — an unresolved copyleft-vs-proprietary conflict that is distribution-blocking for the paid/App-Store endpoint. The Python recommender's unbounded >= floors with no committed lockfile compound the non-reproducibility. Works on the author's pinned local lockfiles; not yet reproducible-from-clean nor license-clean for distribution.

21 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • Rust workspace dependencies are centralized via [workspace.dependencies] in the root Cargo.toml and inherited with .workspace=true across all 5 crates, giving a single source of truth for crypto primitives (aes-gcm 0.10.3, hkdf 0.12, hmac 0.12, sha2 0.10, subtle 2, rand 0.8) — current, appropriate versions for the contracts crate.
  • Cargo.lock IS committed (66589 bytes tracked) — the Rust dependency graph is hash-locked and reproducible, the strongest supply-chain artifact in the repo.
  • media-core's reqwest uses default-features=false + rustls-tls, deliberately avoiding system OpenSSL/native-tls — correct for reproducible static cross-compilation and to avoid OpenSSL CVE exposure.
  • Rust release profile (opt-level=3, lto=thin, codegen-units=1, strip=true) is production-appropriate; the maturin/pyo3 build also strips.
  • Version pins are CONSISTENT across CI and all Dockerfiles: CI uses node 24, rust 1.90 (dtolnay/rust-toolchain toolchain: '1.90'), python 3.12; the root, media-core, transcoder, and recommender Dockerfiles all use rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim/node:24, and python:3.12-slim respectively — no CI/Docker base-image drift.
  • edition=2024 + rust-version=1.85 are internally consistent (2024 edition requires >=1.85); pyo3 abi3-py312 matches the recommender's requires-python>=3.12 and the python:3.12 runtime, so the Python ABI target is coherent end-to-end.
  • Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry (§15 MANDATORY) is genuinely wired, not just declared: @sentry/node is imported and Sentry.init is called at server/index.ts:37 with the boot-time DSN (EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN), a PII-scrub beforeSend (server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.ts), and the DSN is surfaced to clients via /api/telemetry/config — matching the per-self-hoster crash-island design.
  • ffmpeg is pinned to a specific binaries-only image tag (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) and copied as static glibc-free binaries in all three media Dockerfiles, with a boot-time version gate (server/services/ffmpeg.ts rejects <6.0) — a disciplined, reproducible way to ship a known ffmpeg version.
  • The transcoder already implements a hardware-encoder abstraction (args.rs HwEncoder enum with VideoToolbox, nvenc/qsv/vaapi paths, libx264 only as the CPU fallback) — the architectural seam needed to drop GPL x264 on Apple platforms already exists.
  • The LICENSE is a clean, deliberate proprietary placeholder (not a copy-pasted MIT) that explicitly enumerates the Apple App Store/TestFlight path and household-only scope — the right instinct for a private, App-Store-bound product.
  • Build orchestration is thoughtful: the napi .node and PyO3 wheel are built in dedicated Rust toolchain stages and copied into runtime images, so the runtime containers don't carry a Rust toolchain; better-sqlite3's node-gyp toolchain is installed and purged in a single layer to keep the image slim.
  • package-lock.json is committed (288940 bytes) so `npm ci` is reproducible on the one machine/registry state where it was generated.
Plex parity notes

Plex's distribution model is exactly the discipline this project needs and is currently missing. Plex runs ffmpeg server-side (on the self-hosted box the user already operates and never redistributes), and its iOS/tvOS clients rely on Apple's native VideoToolbox decode/encode — they do NOT bundle GPL x264 into the App-Store binary. This repo's server-side GPL ffmpeg + libx264 path (args.rs:46) is fine for the household-only server exactly as Plex does it; the latent encoder seam (args.rs HwEncoder::VideoToolbox) shows the team already anticipated the Apple-native path. The unresolved gap is the proprietary LICENSE's explicit App-Store framing (clause (b) names TestFlight/App Store) colliding with copyleft if ANY GPL component ever rides into the future M2 Apple client (which does not yet exist). To reach Plex parity on distribution: GPL stays strictly server-side and is never redistributed by household operators; the Apple clients must use VideoToolbox and bundle zero GPL code; and a NOTICE/THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES manifest must enumerate every OSS component (a routine App-Store review expectation Plex satisfies). Separately and orthogonally to code licensing, the IPTV/Xtream re-streaming feature carries a content-rights exposure that Plex deliberately does not take on in its first-party app — re-streaming third-party IPTV through an App-Store app is a product/legal decision the LICENSE does not address at all, and is arguably a larger App-Store-rejection risk than the codec licensing.

Crate — emerald-contracts (cross-language crypto)

functional

Canonical cross-language crypto/contract crate: a Rust core plus an N-API binding consumed by the Hono/TypeScript backend and a PyO3 binding consumed by the Python recommender. Defines stream tokens (HMAC-SHA256 over a canonical JSON byte template), device tokens and internal-principal tokens (JWE alg:dir/enc:A256GCM, kid-aware multi-key verify), HKDF key derivation, sub-namespace parsing (plex/local/apple), and a telemetry PII scrubber. The tests/vectors/*.json files are intended to be the cross-language byte-parity oracle so Rust, TypeScript, and Python produce identical bytes.

Maturity: Sound RustCrypto primitives (AES-256-GCM with per-encrypt OsRng nonce, AAD-bound protected header, constant-time HMAC compare, kid-aware verify) with solid in-crate unit tests including the new BadTtl forged-token defense; held back from production-grade by the cross-language vector oracle only covering happy-path canonical bytes + HKDF (no rejection/tampering/expiry/kid-dispatch across languages), BadTtl being unmapped through both FFI bindings, a PyO3-vs-N-API surface asymmetry, FFI error/panic leakage, a 180-day device-token TTL with no in-crate revocation/replay primitive, and a scrub-sentinel divergence from the committed vector. NOTE: HEAD advanced from 44ff715 to 1e34db02 DURING this audit; an earlier-observed committed merge conflict in stream_token.rs was resolved by a concurrent session, and current HEAD compiles cleanly (RC=0).

30 files reviewed · 13 findings
What works
  • The crate compiles cleanly at current HEAD (1e34db02): cargo build -p emerald-contracts returns RC=0; no conflict markers remain in working tree or HEAD blob (grep -c = 0).
  • JWE uses RustCrypto aes-gcm with a fresh 12-byte OsRng nonce per encrypt (jwe.rs:57), and the encoded protected header is bound as AAD on both encrypt and decrypt (jwe.rs:60, jwe.rs:136) so any header tamper invalidates the GCM tag. A nonce_uniqueness test guards against a deterministic-nonce regression (jwe.rs:174).
  • decode_protected_header hard-rejects any alg != 'dir' or enc != 'A256GCM' before attempting decryption (jwe.rs:100-102), with a rejects_wrong_alg test proving an attacker cannot swap to RSA-OAEP.
  • Device and internal-principal tokens are kid-aware: an unknown kid is rejected (UnknownKid) rather than brute-forcing all keys, and aud=='device' / iss=='eex' are re-validated AFTER decryption (device_token.rs:91-96).
  • Stream-token verify uses subtle::ConstantTimeEq for the MAC compare (stream_token.rs:247), and verify_dual_key computes BOTH primary and fallback HMACs unconditionally before branching on a bool (stream_token.rs:175-176), avoiding a short-circuit timing side channel across the grace window.
  • The resolved enforce_time_window caps token lifetime at MAX_TTL_SECS = 91 days (stream_token.rs:114, 192) and rejects a forged-forward nbf>iat && exp<nbf window as BadTtl (stream_token.rs:200), with a carefully-scoped condition that deliberately keeps a normally-expired token classified as Expired — and this logic IS unit-tested in-crate (stream_token.rs:415 bad_ttl_rejected covers over-cap, forged-forward-nbf, and the expired-stays-Expired guard).
  • canonical.rs is a hand-rolled fixed-template, alphabetical-order, whitespace-free JSON serializer for the HMAC input (deliberately NOT serde, which serializes in declaration order) with a surrogate-pair / 4-byte-UTF-8 test that matches Node's Buffer encoding.
  • HKDF derive_key matches Node hkdfSync('sha256', secret, '', info, 32) with a zero-length salt and domain-separated info constants per token type, with an info_strings_are_distinct test; hkdf-parity.json pins exact OKM hex and is actually executed by the vector harness.
  • parse_sub uses OnceLock-cached anchored per-provider regexes, correctly encodes the plex:0 anonymous-account exception, and rejects leading-zero plex ids (sub.rs:15, sub.rs:121); sub-namespace.json carries 14 positive+negative cases.
  • build-with-dts-guard.mjs is a pragmatic, well-documented workaround for the napi-rs v3 CLI clobbering the hand-authored index.d.ts — it snapshots, restores from git if pre-clobbered, and only writes back on shrink.
Plex parity notes

This crate is internal security infrastructure, not a user-facing media feature, so it has no direct Plex feature to match. Its relevance to the Plex-replacement goal is that it is the cross-language trust foundation a multi-client (tvOS/iOS/web) Plex-grade system needs: stream-grant tokens (equivalent to Plex's signed transcode/stream URLs), per-device tokens, internal service-to-service principals, and a sub-namespace that models identity across plex/local/apple (a deliberate superset of Plex, which only models Plex accounts). The cryptographic design choices (AES-256-GCM AEAD, kid rotation, constant-time MAC compares, canonical-byte HMAC, a 91-day stream-token cap) are appropriate and in places stronger than necessary. Gaps that matter for Plex-equivalent trust at App-Store scale: no token revocation/replay primitive in the crate (Plex supports per-device de-authorization; here device tokens live 180 days with only bulk kid-rotation); the new forged-token TTL cap is Rust-only, unmapped through the bindings, and unverified on the TypeScript playback path that actually gates content; and the byte-parity oracle never exercises rejection/tampering/expiry/kid-dispatch across languages. Close these before relying on this crate as the security spine of a public multi-device product.

Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth

functional

The foundation layer of the React 19 + Vite SPA that is the project's real, primary client: app shell + provider tree (main.tsx -> App.tsx: StrictMode/QueryClientProvider/ConfirmProvider/AuthProvider with a lazy-loaded hash-routed tab shell), hash router (lib/router.ts), TanStack Query config (lib/queryClient.ts), the auth context implementing Plex PIN + Apple SIWA + WebAuthn passkey login/register over server-side cookie sessions (lib/auth.tsx), the fetch base + typed error envelope (lib/api/base.ts, errors.ts), eight per-domain API clients (iptv/media/sonarr/radarr/sab/tmdb/grabs/recommenderEvents), 27 data hooks bridging those into React Query, and two cosmetic helpers (navTransition, animatedFavicon).

Maturity: Shell, hash router, query config, and three real auth flows (Plex PIN polling, Apple SIWA, WebAuthn passkey via @simplewebauthn/browser) are coherently implemented against matching server routes, with consistent credentialed fetch, a typed ApiError envelope, namespaced query keys, visibility-gated polling, and unit tests on the pure logic — but it is NOT production-grade: zero React error boundaries, zero client error telemetry despite the mandatory-telemetry contract, no centralized mid-session 401 handling, no request timeouts on GETs, and GET clients throw opaque unnormalized errors on empty/non-JSON bodies.

58 files reviewed · 10 findings
What works
  • Three real, end-to-end auth flows wired to matching server routes: Plex PIN popup with 5-min deadline + 1.5s polling (signIn -> /api/auth/plex/pin + /api/auth/plex/check), Apple SIWA (appleSignIn -> /api/auth/apple), and full WebAuthn passkey login AND registration via @simplewebauthn/browser (passkeyLogin/passkeyRegister -> /api/auth/passkey/{login,register}/{options,verify}). Verified mounts: app.ts:104 /api/auth, :111 /api/auth/passkey, :112 /api/me; GET /api/me hydrates the session.
  • Session model is a server-side HttpOnly cookie, not a JS-readable bearer: auth.tsx only mirrors identity for UI gating and uses credentials:'include' throughout. localStorage holds only non-secret UI prefs (viewAs role, nav-transition flag, AI toggle) and the per-user BYO Anthropic key — never the session credential.
  • applyUser() calls queryClient.clear() synchronously on every identity change, correctly preventing cross-user cache leakage on shared devices — a well-reasoned correctness fix.
  • Auth concurrency guards are careful: signInInFlightRef blocks double sign-in; popup/poll interval ownership is tracked per attempt (stopCurrentPoll, popupRef === popup checks); the poll distinguishes popup-closed, deadline-expired, 403-denied, 4xx, and authorized with specific user copy.
  • apiFetch error normalization (throwApiError) is strong: maps domain codes (insufficient_disk_space, default_quality_profile_missing, rootfolder_unreachable, 507 free_space_unknown, 401 session-expired) to specific human copy and preserves status/code/details on a typed ApiError; covered by errors.test.ts and per-client tests.
  • Polling hooks are visibility-aware where it matters: useDownloadQueue/useSonarrQueue/useRadarrQueue gate refetchInterval on useDocumentVisible() and set refetchIntervalInBackground:false; useIptvSessions also sets refetchIntervalInBackground:false.
  • Query keys are consistently namespaced/parameterized; dependent queries gate with enabled (seriesId != null, debounced.length >= 2, ids.length > 0); optimistic mutations (favorites, feedback) snapshot+rollback correctly and invalidate the right keys onSettled.
  • animatedFavicon is well-engineered: throttled to 14fps (not per-frame), pauses on document.hidden via visibilitychange, skips under navigator.webdriver to avoid crashing headless CI, falls back to the static SVG on WebGL init failure.
  • useUserApiKey hashes the BYO key (djb2) into a non-secret fingerprint for query keys, keeping plaintext out of cache keys/logs, and handles a one-time legacy-key migration.
  • usePlexLinks ships a pure, unit-tested resolver (resolvePlexLink) with a tmdb->tvdb->imdb->search fallback chain and degraded-mode behavior when the server map is unavailable.
  • CSRF posture is coherent: server applies requireSafeOrigin globally (app.ts:66) and the SPA's state-changing calls always send credentials with a matching Origin; suggestions GET opts into requireTrustedOrigin to protect its side effects.
Plex parity notes

Correction note: the web auth flows ARE real and wired — Plex PIN, Apple SIWA, and WebAuthn passkey all hit matching server routes (server/auth.ts exports `auth`+`me`, mounted at app.ts:104/112; passkey at :111), and GET /api/me hydrates the session. So sign-in works; an earlier draft that claimed otherwise was wrong. Versus Plex, the genuine gaps in THIS layer are: (1) Casting/AirPlay to a TV is absent — useCast here is a TMDB cast-list hook, not a Chromecast controller; a Plex replacement needs real cast support for the Apple TV/iOS targets. (2) Resilience is below Plex's web client: no error boundary and no client telemetry mean a single render error blanks the whole app and operators are blind, where Plex degrades per-panel. (3) No centralized session-expiry handling, so a lapsed session yields a dead UI rather than graceful re-auth. (4) Continue-watching/resume-progress is only present for IPTV (useIptvHistory + useReportPosition at a 5s cadence); there is no unified library playback-progress hook spanning media-core/Plex sources, which Plex treats as core. Architecturally the data layer (namespaced TanStack keys, cursor pagination for large IPTV catalogs, cookie sessions, per-user cache clearing, Plex deep-link resolver, BYO-key fingerprinting) is a sound and in places more ambitious base than Plex's; the parity blockers here are casting, resilience/observability, and unified resume state — on top of the roadmap-known fact that the native Apple clients this UI is meant to feed do not exist yet.

Cross-Cutting: Production Readiness

functional

Whole-repo operability audit (observability/Glitchtip wiring + PII scrubber, graceful shutdown + in-flight/stream/ffmpeg drain, config fail-fast, external-call timeouts/retries/backpressure, resource cleanup, health vs readiness probes, SQLite backup/restore, migration safety) of theemeraldexchange on branch m3-media-core.

Maturity: This is the corrected, final dossier; it supersedes BOTH my earlier StructuredOutput calls this session (the first wrongly claimed the environment was compromised — a misread of a malformed `base64` invocation; the second under-counted evidence). The repo is genuine (544 files, canary-confirmed) and I have now read 17 operability-critical files verbatim across all four runtimes (Node, Rust x2, Python). VERDICT: 'functional', not production-grade. The codebase shows real operational discipline in specific places — boot-time config fail-fast (env.ts) is excellent, EVERY external call I traced (Sonarr/Radarr/SAB/Plex/TMDB/recommender/media-core) routes through a single AbortController-timeout wrapper, the PII scrubber is thorough and wired into both Sentry hooks, per-session ffmpeg lifecycle has SIGTERM-then-SIGKILL escalation + idle sweeper + tmpdir cleanup, and destructive migrations are refused unless a recent backup exists. But the operability layer that matters at App-Store scale and under many concurrent streams has confirmed, code-level gaps: (1) Sentry is initialized but has NO Hono request/error handler, so handler exceptions never reach Glitchtip despite the mandatory-telemetry contract; (2) both Rust services (transcoder, media-core) handle ONLY ctrl_c/SIGINT and IGNORE the SIGTERM Docker sends, so every redeploy SIGKILLs in-flight transcodes; (3) the Node shutdown handler process.exit()s without server.close() or stream/ffmpeg drain, and competes with a SECOND signal handler in iptvScheduler; (4) NEITHER Rust service nor the Python recommender has ANY Sentry/Glitchtip wiring, so 3 of 4 runtimes emit zero crash telemetry; (5) /api/health is a static literal and compose has zero healthchecks; (6) backups are manual + migration-gated only — no scheduled/automated backup, and a project audit doc records the Python backup gate as silently weakened with 2 RED tests.

17 files reviewed · 9 findings
What works
  • Config fail-fast (env.ts) is the standout strength. required()/positiveNumber()/positiveInt() throw at boot on missing/malformed values, explicitly because NaN would silently disable the disk-space safety gate (68-82). Prod gates fail CLOSED: ALLOWED_ORIGINS required or CSRF fails open (103-108); PLEX_SERVER_ID required unless ALLOW_UNSCOPED_PLEX_LOGIN=1 is explicit (121-131); SESSION_SECRET/RECOMMENDER_EVENT_SECRET enforce a 32-char minimum and reject placeholders like 'changeme' (159-213); EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN required in prod (267-274); assertSecretsDistinct runs in ALL envs (250-260).
  • Outbound-call timeout discipline is consistent and real. upstream.ts wraps every call in an AbortController (LAN 15s / WAN 10s), clears the timer in finally (60-62, 86-88), and maps AbortError/network errors to a synthesized 504 so a timeout never becomes an unhandled rejection (44-59). Confirmed used by the sidecar callers too: recommender.ts imports and calls fetchWithTimeout (15, 106) and media.ts routes the media-core proxy through it (media.ts 4, 70). Internal calls are NOT raw fetch.
  • The PII scrubber is thorough and correctly wired. telemetryPiiScrub.ts deep-walks the whole event without mutating input (151-176), redacts EEX keys + Sentry's 22-entry denylist via case-insensitive substring (43-86), strips stream-grant t=<token> and JWE ciphertext from strings (127-149), and rewrites Authorization/Cookie header values (178-211).
