Installed into BOTH beforeSend and beforeBreadcrumb (index.ts 41-42); the breadcrumb hook specifically closes the XHR-data.url token-leak class (243-274). setSentryUser() throws on persistent IDs to protect the App Store linked=No label (105-125).
  • Per-session ffmpeg lifecycle in iptvRemux.ts is genuinely well-built: SIGTERM then SIGKILL-after-5s escalation (84-101) with unref'd timers, an idle sweeper that reaps sessions after 30s no-heartbeat (104-112), tmpdir creation + best-effort recursive cleanup on stop/error/exit (51-57, 140-149), and stderr scrubbed for Xtream creds before logging (136-141). This is the most production-aware module reviewed.
  • ffmpeg is validated at boot (validateFfmpegOrExit) with a 5s ffprobe timeout, ENOENT/version handling across 4 real-world version-string families, and process exit on failure (ffmpeg.ts 106-171; index.ts 25-31).
  • SQLite opener sets sane pragmas — busy_timeout=5000, WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, foreign_keys=ON — and applies migrations transactionally with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK per file plus a slow-migration warning (db.ts 18-22, 119-137). Migrations are idempotent (applied-version set) and tolerate three legacy migration-table shapes.
  • A backup safety gate DOES exist for the dangerous path: migrator.ts refuses any `-- DESTRUCTIVE` migration unless server_state.last_backup_at is within 10 minutes, with a clear operator message pointing at POST /api/admin/backup (migrator.ts 54-92, 283-285). This is a thoughtful guard most homelab projects lack.
  • The recommender's FastAPI lifespan is correct: migrate-on-start, an AsyncIOScheduler + retention sweeper task started on boot and cleanly cancelled/awaited on shutdown with suppress(CancelledError) (main.py 74-91); per-request SQLite connections opened/closed via a generator dependency to avoid cross-request cursor races (96-109); the retention sweeper wraps work in try/except with log.exception (65-71) rather than a bare except. Its /health actually queries the DB (titles/vectors/active model) — a real readiness signal (122-135).
  • A one-way crypto-debt deadline guard refuses boot on/after 2026-06-25 if the legacy SHA-256 path was not removed (index.ts 55-65). Network posture is sound: all long-lived compose services use restart: unless-stopped and bind 127.0.0.1 only; transcoder HLS scratch is a size-capped tmpfs that auto-clears (docker-compose 239-240). Internal-principal verification has staged off/log/enforce knobs with a documented rollout path (155-161, 199-202).
Plex parity notes

Operability vs Plex Media Server expectations, on verified evidence: Plex survives restarts/upgrades without re-pairing clients and ships a defined, automated database backup; this project has NEITHER automated DB backup NOR a clean drain-on-shutdown, so a routine NAS reboot or `docker compose up -d` is materially MORE disruptive than on Plex — every live IPTV viewer is hard-killed on the Node path, every in-flight transcode is SIGKILLed on the Rust path (which doesn't even handle SIGTERM), and a lost server.db re-pairs every Apple device. Plex degrades gracefully when transcoder/library is down and exposes real server status; here /api/health cannot tell 'up' from 'degraded' and nothing gates the Cloudflare tunnel on readiness. Observability parity is worse: Plex has mature internal logging/diagnostics, whereas 3 of this stack's 4 runtimes emit no crash telemetry at all and the Node one misses handler exceptions. Where this project EXCEEDS a typical Plex install operationally: boot-time config fail-fast, per-secret HKDF isolation, an always-on outbound-timeout wrapper used even on internal sidecar calls, a destructive-migration backup gate, and staged internal-principal enforcement. CAVEAT: actual many-concurrent-stream parity depends on transcoder/media-core streaming internals I did not fully audit; the roadmap flags M4 at 45% with 'no real transcode has ever run', and the confirmed SIGTERM defect would make that path additionally fragile under production restarts regardless of whether the happy path works.

Cross-Cutting: Docs vs Reality & Honesty

functional

Audit documentation accuracy (README, DEPLOY, ROADMAP-STATUS, PRODUCT, DESIGN, the prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit, ops/recommender/monetization docs, and the M1.5 contract + decision-brief specs) against the actual codebase, to tell the owner which docs lie and rate docs trustworthiness for a new engineer.

Maturity: DOCS TRUSTWORTHINESS FOR A NEW ENGINEER: 5/10 (CONFIRMED via full reads + code cross-checks). The project's documentation is sharply bimodal. The DATED OPERATIONAL/STATUS docs are genuinely excellent and unusually honest: DEPLOY.md matches the deployed multi-service topology exactly; docker-compose.yml + deploy-nas.sh + glitchtip-setup.md + m3-internal-principal-rollout.md form a self-consistent, file:line-accurate operational corpus; ROADMAP-STATUS.md is candid to a fault; the 2026-05-28 honesty audit is real and its code findings were genuinely remediated (commit-hashed, several independently corroborated); and the brand-new MONETIZATION doc (dated today) leads with the honest App-Store blockers. Crucially, multiple claims I expected to be inflated proved TRUE on inspection: Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry is genuinely wired (server/index.ts:36-46 with a piiScrub beforeSend and a setSentryUser PII guard), the M1.5 contract really is RATIFIED/LOCKED (header line 3), zero .swift files is accurate, .gitattributes really is absent and /api/version really does omit the schemas block (ROADMAP honestly flags BOTH). That is a high-integrity backend-docs layer and the reason the score is not lower. What drags it to 5/10 is the TOP-OF-REPO PRODUCT docs a newcomer reads FIRST: README.md and PRODUCT.md describe a dead V1 Sonarr/Radarr/SAB dashboard with a provably-wrong security model (README claims Nginx injects API keys; the actual nginx/default.conf is a bare SPA static-serve with no proxy and is not even in compose), and a direction (open-Plex, no-recommender, Netlify-out-of-scope) that contradicts the shipped product; and DESIGN.md flatly forbids the Three.js/WebGL the app actually ships (confirmed: package.json three@0.184 + src/lib/gemScene.ts/EmeraldMark.tsx import three). Maturity 'functional': the operational docs are production-grade, but the front-door product docs are stale enough to actively mislead, and the strong honesty discipline applied to code/test claims has a blind spot for the product narrative.

20 files reviewed · 9 findings
What works
  • DEPLOY.md is the gold-standard doc: titled '...(V2)' (DEPLOY.md:1), documents the REAL topology (Netlify SPA + Cloudflare Tunnel + Hono backend + Python recommender on the internal net, DEPLOY.md:5-14), and proactively flags the stale V1 layer ('the legacy V1 nginx-static container ... and its DEPLOY.md recipe are superseded', DEPLOY.md:16; 'the nginx/ directory ... is dead code', DEPLOY.md:123). The recommender ports the assignment flagged are accurate/consistent: 8000 in-container (DEPLOY.md:441, compose:140), 8001 NAS-loopback (DEPLOY.md:444, compose:130).
  • Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry (contract sec 15, MANDATORY) is GENUINELY WIRED, refuting the declared-but-unwired hypothesis. server/index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init({ dsn, environment, release, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, integrations:[onUnhandledRejectionIntegration] }); env.ts hard-fails prod boot when EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is absent (and index.ts:48-52 warns otherwise); compose passes the DSN through (compose:45); server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.ts enforces the sec-15.2 no-PII rule via a setSentryUser wrapper that throws on non-anonymous ids; routes/telemetry.ts serves the DSN to clients at boot. Code, compose, and glitchtip-setup.md all agree.
  • ROADMAP-STATUS.md is candid and verified-accurate on its hardest self-reported gaps: 'Zero Swift lines (find -name *.swift = 0)' (ROADMAP:58) -> confirmed (git ls-files *.swift = 0; EmeraldKit/ holds only Resources/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy); 'No repo-root .gitattributes despite sec 7.1 calling it a hard requirement' (ROADMAP:47) -> confirmed absent; '/api/version omits the sec 7.2 schemas block' (ROADMAP:46) -> confirmed (version.ts returns only server_id/release/auth_modes/accepting_device_pairs); 'no real transcode has ever run' (ROADMAP:84). A status doc that accurately self-reports unmet requirements is rare.
  • The prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit is real, blunt, and was acted on: its remediation log (AUDIT:180-192) lists commit-hashed fixes, several independently corroborated this run -- media-core now HAS a SPA consumer (src/lib/api/media.ts + useMediaLibrary.ts + MediaTab.tsx exist, closing the audit's M10 'no frontend consumer'), and the contract header now reads RATIFIED/LOCKED (closing the audit's still-open lock item, AUDIT:195).
  • The M1.5 contract is correctly RATIFIED/LOCKED with a dated supersession note ('Status: RATIFIED for M2 (2026-05-28) ... All four previously-open decisions are now resolved in-body and the contract is locked', cross-service-contract.md:3-8); aspirational specs honestly self-label ('Status: Brainstorm-output spec drafted 2026-05-27. Pre-execution', m2-emeraldkit-apple-targets.md:3; 'Status: Decision-ready planning doc. No code or prod changes made', MONETIZATION:3).
  • MONETIZATION-AND-PUBLISHING.md (dated 2026-05-30) is exemplary honesty: it leads with 'not in its current state' and names the ffmpeg GPL-3.0 App-Store blocker (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1 --enable-gpl, MONETIZATION sec 2) and the IPTV Guideline-5.2.3 rejection risk with file:line citations -- proactively surfacing the exact distribution risks the product docs ignore.
  • LICENSE is an honest proprietary placeholder matching contract sec 14 and project memory ('LICENSE deferred to M2 TestFlight'): 'PROPRIETARY SOFTWARE LICENSE ... All rights reserved ... not licensed for redistribution'. No drift between the license brief, the contract, and the file.
  • m3-internal-principal-rollout.md is an accurate, file:line-precise operational runbook (off->log->enforce) that cross-references real code (server/services/internalPrincipal.ts, recommender/app/internal_principal.py, recommenderCaller.ts) and matches the compose posture knobs.
Plex parity notes

The docs are internally split on the Plex-parity ambition; a newcomer gets opposite signals by doc. README.md and PRODUCT.md frame Plex as an EXTERNAL app you 'open' ('open Plex', PRODUCT.md:9; 'Watch (-> Plex)', README.md:16 / PRODUCT.md:47) - the opposite of the goal to REPLACE Plex - and neither mentions the App Store, native clients, or the media server. Only the dated DEPLOY/ROADMAP/MONETIZATION docs and the specs reflect the real Plex-replacement direction, and those are admirably blunt about the gaps. CONFIRMED this run: the Apple native clients (M2 EmeraldKit + iOS/tvOS, M5 offline downloads) genuinely do not exist (git ls-files *.swift = 0; EmeraldKit/ holds only Resources/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy); the M4 transcoder, which a Plex replacement needs for non-direct-play content, is candidly NOT proven ('no real transcode has ever run', ROADMAP:84). One piece of GOOD news the status doc undersells: the web media-core browse consumer now exists (src/lib/api/media.ts + MediaTab.tsx), so the personal-library half of Plex parity has begun on the web client even though ROADMAP still says 'no client consumer'. The MONETIZATION doc (today) correctly elevates the two parity/distribution risks the product docs ignore: ffmpeg/libx264 GPL-3.0 is App-Store-incompatible (a hard blocker to shipping a Plex-replacement binary) and the IPTV viewer risks Guideline-5.2.3 rejection; LICENSE/contract sec 14 correctly mark the license as a placeholder pending M2 TestFlight. Net: the candid backend/ops docs tell the truth about Plex-parity status; the front-door product docs (README/PRODUCT) understate the ambition to the point of contradicting it, and DESIGN.md's no-WebGL rule conflicts with the shipped brand mark. The single highest-value doc-honesty fix for the Plex-replacement narrative is to retire or re-banner README+PRODUCT so the stated mission and the documented product finally agree.

Cross-Cutting: Security

functional

Whole-repo security audit (weak/default secrets, auth bypass, authZ/IDOR, SQLi, SSRF guard coverage, command injection, path traversal, cross-language crypto/token-replay, XSS/CSRF, security headers/CORS, rate limiting/DoS, secret leakage) across src/, server/, crates/, recommender/, and infra for theemeraldexchange — a self-hosted invite-only Plex-replacement streaming platform bound for the Apple App Store.

Maturity: Verified across all five layers (SPA, Hono server, Rust crates, Python recommender, infra), the security posture is well above prototype and approaches production-grade on most dimensions, with a small set of concrete gaps that keep it short of it. Strong, verified foundations: no hardcoded/default signing secrets; boot-time secret strength+distinctness enforcement; fail-closed CSRF + prod env gating; JWE-encrypted session cookies AND device tokens; genuine server-side device-token revocation; constant-time Rust HMAC stream tokens with a TTL upper-bound and a kind-aware replay cache; AES-256-GCM JWE with per-call random nonce and header-as-AAD; a properly-designed media-core acting_sub IDOR guard that REJECTS client-supplied ?sub= outside Off mode (with a test proving it); parameterized SQL across better-sqlite3 / sqlx / Python sqlite; ffmpeg/ffprobe invoked exclusively via argv arrays (no shell) in both Rust and the server; file serving by opaque integer id plus a canonicalize-and-confine symlink-escape guard; @sentry/node genuinely initialized with PII-scrub beforeSend; edge security headers; and a SPA that stores NO auth token client-side. What holds it back from production-grade: (1) the SSRF guard is DNS-rebind-bypassable and absent on several stream-byte proxies; (2) SESSION_SECRET is accepted as a stream-token fallback key for up to 90 days; (3) internal-principal enforcement on media-core/recommender defaults to OFF; (4) no application-layer rate limiting / server-wide transcode-process ceiling on the unauthenticated stream/playlist/auth surfaces; (5) secret-strength checks are prod-only; (6) the Netlify CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' and nginx serves no CSP. None of these are stub-level; they are hardening gaps in otherwise-real, tested code. Minor unread bodies (full media-core/transcoder route tables, every *arr/sab/users/devices route, recommender main.py) could surface additional per-route findings, but the representative samples verified (sonarr authZ, media-core auth+routes+IDOR, stream_token+jwe crypto) were sound — hence high confidence in 'functional'.

39 files reviewed · 9 findings
What works
  • No hardcoded/default signing secrets: SESSION_SECRET, STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET, DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET, INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET, RECOMMENDER_EVENT_SECRET all come from required()/opt() with NO literal fallback, get a >=32-char floor + placeholder blocklist in prod (secrets.ts:11-54), and are asserted pairwise-distinct at boot in ALL environments (secrets.ts:72-102).
  • CSRF fails CLOSED (csrf.ts:30-39 returns csrf_misconfigured when allowedOrigins empty AND isProd); env.ts:103-108 refuses prod boot without ALLOWED_ORIGINS; requireSafeOrigin mounted globally (app.ts:66). The SPA deliberately POSTs the Plex PIN check so the CSRF gate covers the cookie-setting branch (auth.tsx:277-288).
  • Session cookies are JWE (A256GCM, HKDF key) not just signed, hiding the embedded Plex token from a captured cookie (session.ts:1-12,343-361); HttpOnly+Secure(prod)+SameSite set correctly (session.ts:426-432). Device tokens persist a jti row and are checked for existence+revocation+expiry at verify time (session.ts:269-336) — real server-side revocation. Bearer auth refuses to fall back to cookie on invalid Bearer (auth.ts:42-47).
  • Rust stream-token crypto is correct: constant-time HMAC compare via subtle::ConstantTimeEq with length-mismatch handled without short-circuit (stream_token.rs:240-256), dual-key verify computes BOTH HMACs unconditionally to avoid a timing oracle (stream_token.rs:169-185), MAX_TTL_SECS upper bound rejects forged long-lived tokens (stream_token.rs:114,192-194), and unknown kinds are hard-rejected (stream_token.rs:277).
  • JWE (jwe.rs) uses AES-256-GCM with a fresh 96-bit OsRng nonce per encryption (jwe.rs:47,57; nonce_uniqueness test at :173-182), feeds the encoded protected header as AAD on both sides so header tampering breaks the tag (jwe.rs:59-67,136-146; rejects_wrong_alg test :195-209), and dispatches keys by kid for rotation. Internal-principal JWE carries a 60s TTL enforced on every receiver (internalPrincipal.ts:35; media-core auth.rs:26; recommender internal_principal.py:108).
  • media-core has a real IDOR / per-user-isolation guard: acting_sub() treats a verified internal-principal as authoritative and REJECTS any client-supplied ?sub= outside Off mode (routes.rs:648-671), with an explicit test that ?sub=plex:victim is rejected in Log AND Enforce mode (routes.rs:1195-1224). File serving is by opaque integer file_id resolved from the DB (routes.rs:47-48), plus a canonicalize-both-sides guard that refuses to serve a file whose real path escapes the library root via .. or symlink (routes.rs:348-360).
  • ffmpeg/ffprobe is NEVER shell-invoked: transcoder builds a Vec<String> for Command::args (args.rs:77-192), media-core probe.rs uses Command::new('ffprobe').arg(...).arg(path), server iptvRemux.ts spawns an argv array (iptvRemux.ts:120-134), and ffmpeg.ts boot-check uses execFileSync with a hardcoded ['-version'] (ffmpeg.ts:98-129). No command-injection surface in the paths read. IPTV remux segment names are regex-confined /^seg_\d{5}\.ts$/ before path.join (iptv.ts:637-643,818).
  • SQL is parameterized everywhere checked: better-sqlite3 uses bound ? params (session.ts:221-235, iptv.ts throughout), media-core sqlx uses .bind() incl. LIKE '%' || ? || '%' (routes.rs:112-145,148-256 — no string concat of user input), recommender Python uses parameterized execute and never f-string-builds SQL with user values (db.py:90-513; f-strings appear only around trusted dim/kind/filenames, not request data).
  • SSRF guard IS applied where the fetch target is attacker/upstream-influenced: segment proxy re-validates rid (iptv.ts:1024) and rewriteHlsPlaylist re-validates sub-playlist URLs (iptv.ts:680) before egress; the guard rejects http, IPv4/IPv6 private/loopback/link-local/CGNAT/metadata, and bare/.local/.internal hosts (ssrfGuard.ts:17-71). Encoded-slash IDOR on *arr DELETE is explicitly defended (sonarr.ts:836-849; Number()+safe-integer id gate).
  • SPA stores NO auth credential client-side: session is server-side HttpOnly cookie only; localStorage holds solely UI prefs and a user-entered Anthropic key scoped per sub. No dangerouslySetInnerHTML/innerHTML/eval anywhere in src/. Admin 'view as user' is UI-only with server role unchanged (auth.tsx:563-568). Invite list never returns plaintext codes; only createInvite returns the code once (auth.tsx:617-639). Non-admin *arr adds are permission-rewritten server-side so a direct POST can't dictate root folder / quality profile (sonarr.ts:400-519).
  • Telemetry is real and privacy-scoped: @sentry/node is initialized in index.ts:36-46 with beforeSend=piiScrub and beforeBreadcrumb=piiBreadcrumbScrub, Sentry.setUser is forced to anonymous and direct setUser is prohibited (telemetryPiiScrub.ts:102-124); the DSN-distribution endpoint requireAuth-gates and returns only dsn+environment+release with URL-shape validation (telemetry.ts:19-75). Edge headers: nginx sets nosniff+X-Frame-Options DENY+Referrer-Policy; netlify.toml adds a CSP with frame-ancestors 'none'. Upstream Xtream creds scrubbed from ffmpeg stderr logs (iptvRemux.ts:6-19). A boot-time deadline guard refuses to start after 2026-06-25 unless the legacy SHA-256 session-key path is removed (index.ts:61-65).
Plex parity notes

On the auth/identity/token/crypto spine this project is architecturally AHEAD of classic Plex: per-class signing secrets with boot-time distinctness+strength enforcement, JWE-encrypted session cookies AND device tokens (Plex historically used long-lived, base64-readable X-Plex-Token bearer values), genuine server-side device-token revocation (jti row + revocation table + expiry, session.ts:269-336), a short-TTL constant-time HMAC stream-token system with replay protection and persistent playlist-token revocation, fail-closed CSRF, WebAuthn/passkey + SIWA, and periodic Plex-membership revalidation. On the transcode/streaming security surface that most defines a Plex replacement, parity is GOOD where verified: ffmpeg/ffprobe is argv-only (no shell) in both the Rust transcoder and the server; media-core streams strictly by opaque integer file_id with a canonicalize-and-confine symlink-escape guard (routes.rs:348-360) and bounded range parsing; HLS remux segment names are regex-confined before path.join. That is Plex-grade path-safety and transcode-arg hygiene, plus a per-user-isolation (acting_sub) IDOR guard Plex enforces server-side. Where it falls short of a hardened media server, and must close before production at App-Store scale: a DNS-rebind-bypassable SSRF guard that is also missing on several stream-byte proxies; SESSION_SECRET accepted as a stream-token fallback key for up to 90 days; internal-service (media-core/recommender) identity enforcement defaulting to off; and no application-layer rate limiting or server-wide transcode-process ceiling (Plex sandboxes/limits its transcoder). Net: a stronger-than-Plex auth/token/crypto foundation and Plex-grade path/command-injection/IDOR hygiene on the verified parts, with SSRF hardening, key-coupling cleanup, internal-principal enforcement, edge-CSP tightening, and rate-limiting/DoS controls as the closing checklist.

Cross-Cutting: Testing & CI Rigor

functional

Whole-repo test suite + CI pipeline (.github/workflows/ci.yml) spanning server vitest, SPA vitest, Rust cargo tests, Python pytest, Playwright e2e, and cross-language vector parity. Establishes the green-checkmark gate the team treats as its production-readiness signal.

Maturity: The test infrastructure is genuinely substantial and disciplined for a single-author homelab project: I personally ran `node_modules/.bin/vitest run` twice and confirmed 74 files / 930 tests passing GREEN in ~5s at HEAD 1e34db0, and I ran `cargo check -p transcoder` to EXIT 0 confirming the Rust crates compile. CI has four jobs (test, test-no-iptv, recommender, rust), a cross-language vector-parity oracle with real (non-placeholder) fixtures, an N-API->PyO3 cross-binding gate that fails-closed via CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1, plus type-check + lint + build + coverage steps and a contract-section-13.3 IPTV-disabled re-run matrix. That is well beyond prototype. It is NOT production-grade for an App-Store-bound media platform because the green checkmark systematically over-promises on exactly the Plex-defining capabilities: (1) the entire transcode path — M4's headline feature — is exercised ONLY against a shell-script ffmpeg stub that writes a fake playlist and sleeps, by the code's own admission; no real ffmpeg byte flows through any test. (2) clippy AND cargo fmt are both continue-on-error, so Rust lint/format regressions cannot turn the build red. (3) Playwright 'E2E' boots Vite alone and mocks every /api/* call — a render smoke test, not end-to-end; zero tests drive the real Hono backend through a browser. (4) media-core's TMDB matcher takes results.first() with no confidence/language scoring (confirmed in tmdb.rs:84) and there is no accuracy benchmark and no <5s/100-file perf fixture (media-core has NO tests/ dir at all — only inline #[cfg(test)] unit tests, and scanner.rs:11 states the 5s target but no test enforces it). (5) the recommender CI job runs only 3 hand-picked pytest files; the optimizer-promotion path, db migrator, and eval harness are not gated. The suite proves units behave against mocks; it does not prove the system transcodes, identifies a library accurately, recovers from upstream failures, or holds at concurrent-stream scale.

18 files reviewed · 11 findings
What works
  • FULL VITEST SUITE IS GENUINELY GREEN: I ran `node_modules/.bin/vitest run` twice — 74 test files, 930 tests, 0 failures, ~5s wall (HEAD 1e34db0). Not theater at the unit layer; assertion counts are high and real.
  • Cross-language contract parity is a real, valuable oracle: tests/vectors/*.json (device-token-kid-rotation, hkdf-parity, stream-token-canonical, sub-namespace, telemetry-pii-scrub, internal-principal, sourcePrecedence-canonical, iptv-db-round-trip) are consumed by Rust (crates/emerald-contracts/tests/vectors.rs), TS (server contractsBinding), and Python (recommender/tests/test_emerald_contracts_parity.py). Fixtures hold real byte/hex values, not PLACEHOLDER (verified). Correct way to keep three language ports wire-compatible — ahead of where a typical homelab project would be.
  • CI fails-CLOSED on the cross-binding gate: the recommender job runs the N-API<->PyO3 cross-binding vitest with CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1 (ci.yml:198-201), so a missing/drifted PyO3 extension turns the gate RED instead of silently skipping — a deliberate, mature choice.
  • Rust workspace tests run in CI AND compile: `cargo test --workspace --exclude emerald-contracts-pyo3 --all-targets` (ci.yml:255). I independently ran `cargo check -p transcoder` to EXIT 0, confirming transcoder/media-core/contracts build and their #[cfg(test)] modules are real.
  • Transcoder session lifecycle is honestly and thoroughly tested WITHIN its stub boundary: session.rs has 10 #[tokio::test]s (start/heartbeat/seek-respawn/idle-reap/busy-cap/idempotent-stop/path-traversal-rejection/supervisor-restart-cap) and concurrency.rs has 6 tests proving the CAS-correct two-counter limiter, including rollback-on-global-miss and permit-release-on-drop — the exact races that would leak transcode slots at scale.
  • media-core TMDB parsing has solid pure-function unit coverage of edge cases: empty results, missing results key, missing id, missing title, missing/malformed date->no year, partial episode, and Retry-After clamping (tmdb.rs:464-535). The parsing is well-tested even though the SELECTION logic (first()) is not.
  • Reviewer-insurance matrix exists: the test-no-iptv job re-runs the WHOLE vitest suite with IPTV_DISABLED=1 (ci.yml:84-111) to catch cross-feature coupling to /api/iptv — there is even a dedicated server/app.iptv-disabled.test.ts. Thoughtful guard against hidden feature entanglement.
  • vitest.config.ts injects all required secrets (SESSION_SECRET, STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET, DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET, INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET, etc.) so import-time env validation passes deterministically without a .env.test file — clean.
  • Server route + service coverage is broad and security-conscious: 60 server test files with high per-file counts (suggestions 66, radarr 34, sonarr 31, iptv 28, notifications 27, iptvStreamToken 26). Dedicated tests exist for csrf, ssrfGuard, sanitize, secrets, tokenReplayCache, telemetryPiiScrub, sessionGate, members, appleAuth, plus app.csrf.test.ts and env.test.ts boot-level tests.
Plex parity notes

For a Plex replacement, the test suite proves the easy half and skips the hard half. Plex's core promises are: (1) it transcodes anything to anything reliably under concurrent load, (2) it correctly identifies your library, (3) it plays back resiliently on flaky networks across many client types. The suite verifies NONE of these as a behavior: transcode is stub-only (no real ffmpeg ever runs in a test), the matcher blindly takes results.first() (tmdb.rs:84) with no accuracy benchmark, the 5s/100-file scan budget is documented but untimed, and player error-recovery plus true end-to-end playback are untested (Playwright mocks the backend). What IS well-tested is the supporting cast Plex users take for granted: auth/token crypto with genuine cross-language parity, concurrency caps, route authZ, IPTV token security. So the green checkmark certifies the plumbing and the security model, not the streaming product. At App-Store scale the untested axes (concurrent real transcodes, matcher precision on a large foreign-language library, HLS recovery on cellular) are exactly the ones that generate 1-star reviews. The cross-language vector oracle is a genuine parity strength that will pay off when the Swift client lands. Net: the testing rigor is real and above-average for the size, but it is currently structured to make the project LOOK more finished than it is for the specific capabilities that define Plex parity.

Methodology. 13 subsystem deep-reads + 5 cross-cutting dimension audits + a Plex feature-parity matrix, run as a parallel agent workflow over the full committed tree. Every critical/high/security finding was independently re-verified against source by an adversarial verifier (confirmed / partial / refuted). Refuted findings are retained but greyed out and struck through. Generated 2026-05-30T09:05:00Z from branch m3-media-core @ ebcb386.

Per-finding verdict pairing not reconstructable from the journal, so findings retain surveyor severity."}
